# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Distinguish between continuous and discontinuous conduction modes in a single-phase controlled rectifier with an RL load. State the condition for continuous conduction in terms of the extinction angle $\gamma$.**

**Model Answer:**

In **continuous conduction mode (CCM)**, the load current $i_o$ never falls to zero during a cycle — each thyristor pair continues to conduct until the next pair is fired. The output voltage waveform contains **both positive and negative portions** (e.g., $v_o$ swings from $+V_m$ to $-V_m$ in a full converter).

In **discontinuous conduction mode (DCM)**, $i_o$ falls to zero before the next firing instant; the load is "starved" of current and the output voltage stays at zero during the dead interval. DCM occurs for **R load** always, and for RL load when $\alpha$ is small (high $V_o$, low $L$).

**Condition for CCM (full converter):** $\alpha \le \alpha_{min}$ such that the extinction angle $\gamma = \pi - \alpha + \alpha_{prev} \ge \pi$, i.e., the next firing occurs **before** the previous pair naturally commutates. Equivalently, $\omega L / R$ must be large enough that the current has not decayed to zero at $\omega t = \pi + \alpha$.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Why is the THD of the source current in a single-phase full converter with ripple-free load current independent of the firing angle $\alpha$? What is its value?**

**Model Answer:**

The source current $i_s(\omega t)$ of a full converter with ripple-free load is a **quasi-square wave** of amplitude $I_o$ that simply **shifts horizontally** as $\alpha$ changes — the **shape and amplitude remain identical**. Fourier analysis of an ideal square wave yields a **fixed harmonic spectrum** in which the ratio of total RMS to fundamental RMS is a constant: $\pi/(2\sqrt{2})$.

Therefore:

$$
\text{THD} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{I_{s,rms}}{I_{s1,rms}}\right)^2 - 1} = \sqrt{\frac{\pi^2}{8} - 1} \approx 0.4834 \text{ or } 48.34\%
$$

The **firing angle $\alpha$ affects only the phase** of each harmonic, not its magnitude.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]`

**(a)** Draw the circuit diagram of a single-phase fully controlled bridge rectifier feeding an RLE load. Sketch the waveforms of supply voltage $v_s$, output voltage $v_o$, load current $i_o$, supply current $i_s$, and thyristor voltages $v_{T1}, v_{T3}$ for a firing angle $\alpha = 60^\circ$ operating in continuous conduction. **Marks: 7**

**(b)** Derive the expressions for average output voltage, RMS output voltage, and input power factor for the above converter with a highly inductive load in CCM. Also derive the THD of the source current. **Marks: 7**

#### Model Solution to (a)

The full-bridge has four thyristors T1, T2, T3, T4. T1 and T2 are fired simultaneously at $\omega t = \alpha = 60^\circ$; T3 and T4 at $\omega t = 240^\circ$.

**Waveform description (continuous conduction, $L \to \infty$, $i_o = I_o$):**

- $v_s = V_m\sin(\omega t)$ — pure sinusoid, 230 V RMS, 50 Hz (typical).
- $v_o = +V_m\sin(\omega t)$ for $60^\circ < \omega t < 240^\circ$ (T1–T2 conducting).
- $v_o = -V_m\sin(\omega t) = V_m\sin(\omega t - 180^\circ)$ for $240^\circ < \omega t < 420^\circ$ (T3–T4 conducting) — note that $-V_m\sin(\omega t) = V_m\sin(\omega t - 180^\circ)$, which is **negative** in the range $240^\circ$ to $360^\circ$ but the source is also negative there, so the **magnitude** of $v_o$ follows $|v_s|$.
- $i_o$ is a flat line at $I_o$ (ripple-free).
- $i_s = +I_o$ when T1–T2 conduct, $i_s = -I_o$ when T3–T4 conduct. This is a **quasi-square wave** lagging $v_s$ by $\alpha = 60^\circ$.
- $v_{T1}$: zero when T1 conducts; during T3–T4 conduction T1 is reverse-biased and sees $v_s$ (negative half cycle clipped to $-V_m$ in magnitude).
- $v_{T3}$: complementary to $v_{T1}$ — zero when T3 conducts, otherwise sees the source.

**Key waveform observations to mark:**

1. The output $v_o$ contains a **negative half** between $180^\circ$ and $240^\circ$ (and between $360^\circ$ and $420^\circ$) — characteristic of CCM full converter.
2. The supply current $i_s$ is in phase with $v_s$ **fundamental** but **displaced** by $\alpha$ — this is the **displacement power factor** angle.
3. Each thyristor conducts for **180°** in CCM.

#### Model Solution to (b)

**Average output voltage:** From §3.1 we derived:

$$
V_o = \frac{2V_m}{\pi}\cos\alpha
$$

For $\alpha = 60^\circ$ and $V_m = 325\,\text{V}$ (230 V RMS):

$$
V_o = \frac{2 \cdot 325}{\pi}\cos 60^\circ = \frac{650}{\pi} \cdot 0.5 \approx 103.5\,\text{V}
$$

**RMS output voltage:** The output is a **half-sine-rectified replica** of $v_s$:

$$
V_{o,rms}^2 = \frac{1}{\pi}\int_{\alpha}^{\pi + \alpha} V_m^2 \sin^2(\omega t)\, d(\omega t)
$$

Use the identity $\sin^2 x = (1 - \cos 2x)/2$:

$$
V_{o,rms}^2 = \frac{V_m^2}{\pi}\int_{\alpha}^{\pi + \alpha}\frac{1 - \cos(2\omega t)}{2}\, d(\omega t)
$$

$$
V_{o,rms}^2 = \frac{V_m^2}{2\pi}\left[\omega t - \frac{\sin(2\omega t)}{2}\right]_{\alpha}^{\pi + \alpha}
$$

$$
V_{o,rms}^2 = \frac{V_m^2}{2\pi}\left[\pi - \frac{\sin(2\alpha + 2\pi) - \sin(2\alpha)}{2}\right]
$$

Since $\sin(2\alpha + 2\pi) = \sin(2\alpha)$, the second term vanishes:

$$
\boxed{\,V_{o,rms} = \frac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}}\,}
$$

**Note:** The RMS value is **independent of $\alpha$** — the area of the squared waveform doesn't change with the firing delay.

**Input power factor:** From §3.3, $I_{s1,rms} = \dfrac{2\sqrt{2}}{\pi}I_o$ and $I_{s,rms} = I_o$:

$$
\text{PF} = \frac{I_{s1,rms}}{I_{s,rms}}\cos\alpha = \frac{2\sqrt{2}}{\pi}\cos\alpha \approx 0.9003\cos\alpha
$$

For $\alpha = 60^\circ$: PF = $0.9003 \cdot 0.5 = 0.4502$.

**THD:** From §3.3:

$$
\boxed{\,\text{THD} = 48.34\%\,}
$$

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper 1]`

**(a)** Draw the circuit of a single-phase semi-converter with an RLE load. Sketch and explain the operation for $\alpha = 45^\circ$ showing $v_s$, $v_o$, $i_o$, $i_s$, and thyristor/gate pulse waveforms. Explain the concept of **freewheeling** and why the semi-converter cannot operate in inverter mode. **Marks: 7**

**(b)** A single-phase full converter supplies a highly inductive load with $L_s = 2\,\text{mH}$, supply $V_s = 230\,\text{V}$ RMS at $50\,\text{Hz}$, $I_o = 40\,\text{A}$, and $\alpha = 30^\circ$. Compute (i) the overlap angle $\mu$, (ii) the average output voltage $V_o$ with and without source inductance, and (iii) the reduction in voltage due to source inductance. **Marks: 7**

#### Model Solution to (a)

**Circuit:** Single-phase supply connected to a bridge with **T1, T2 as thyristors** (top and bottom of one leg) and **D1, D2 as diodes** (the other leg). The load $R$–$L$–$E$ is connected across the DC terminals.

**Operation for $\alpha = 45^\circ$ (CCM):**

1. $\omega t \in [45^\circ, 180^\circ]$: T1 and D2 conduct, $v_o = +v_s = V_m\sin(\omega t)$.
2. $\omega t = 180^\circ$: $v_s$ reverses; D1 becomes forward biased. **D1 and T1 freewheel** — load current circulates through T1 → load → D1. $v_o = 0$ and the supply is **disconnected** from the load.
3. $\omega t = 180^\circ + 45^\circ = 225^\circ$: T2 is fired. Freewheeling ends; T2 and D2 conduct (current transfers from D1 to D2), $v_o = +V_m\sin(\omega t)$. **Note** that $\sin(225^\circ) < 0$, so $v_o < 0$ during part of this interval until the next source zero crossing — but the **net contribution to average is positive** over the full cycle.
4. $\omega t = 360^\circ$: $v_s$ reverses again; D1 freewheels with T2. $v_o = 0$.
5. Cycle repeats at $\omega t = 360^\circ + 45^\circ = 405^\circ$.

**Freewheeling action:** During the intervals when $v_s$ is negative, one diode and one thyristor carry the load current in a **short circuit loop** that excludes the source. This:
- Eliminates negative portions of $v_o$, improving average $V_o$.
- Reduces supply current harmonics (gaps in $i_s$).
- Naturally improves the input power factor.

**Why no inverter mode:** In a semi-converter, the **diode always forces the output voltage to be non-negative** during the freewheeling interval. Even if $\alpha > 90^\circ$, the output voltage is **clamped to zero** for a significant portion of the cycle, and the average $V_o = \dfrac{V_m}{\pi}(1 + \cos\alpha)$ is **always non-negative** for $\alpha \in [0, 180^\circ]$. Therefore $V_o$ cannot become negative, and regenerative power flow back to the mains is **structurally impossible**.

#### Model Solution to (b)

**Given:** $V_s = 230\,\text{V (RMS)}$, $V_m = \sqrt{2} \cdot 230 \approx 325.27\,\text{V}$, $f = 50\,\text{Hz}$, $\omega = 2\pi f = 314.16\,\text{rad/s}$, $L_s = 2\,\text{mH} = 0.002\,\text{H}$, $I_o = 40\,\text{A}$, $\alpha = 30^\circ$.

**(i) Overlap angle $\mu$:** From the overlap equation:

$$
\cos\alpha - \cos(\alpha + \mu) = \frac{2\omega L_s I_o}{V_m}
$$

Compute the RHS:

$$
\frac{2 \cdot 314.16 \cdot 0.002 \cdot 40}{325.27} = \frac{50.265}{325.27} \approx 0.1545
$$

Therefore:

$$
\cos(\alpha + \mu) = \cos 30^\circ - 0.1545 = 0.8660 - 0.1545 = 0.7115
$$

$$
\alpha + \mu = \cos^{-1}(0.7115) \approx 44.62^\circ
$$

$$
\boxed{\,\mu \approx 14.62^\circ\,}
$$

**(ii) Average output voltage:**

- **Without source inductance** ($L_s = 0$, $\mu = 0$):

$$
V_{o,ideal} = \frac{2V_m}{\pi}\cos\alpha = \frac{2 \cdot 325.27}{\pi} \cdot \cos 30^\circ
$$

$$
V_{o,ideal} = \frac{650.54}{\pi} \cdot 0.8660 = 207.07 \cdot 0.8660 \approx 179.3\,\text{V}
$$

- **With source inductance:**

$$
V_o = \frac{V_m}{\pi}\bigl[\cos\alpha + \cos(\alpha + \mu)\bigr]
$$

$$
V_o = \frac{325.27}{\pi}\bigl[0.8660 + 0.7115\bigr] = 103.53 \cdot 1.5775 \approx 163.3\,\text{V}
$$

**(iii) Voltage reduction:**

$$
\Delta V_o = V_{o,ideal} - V_o = 179.3 - 163.3 = 16.0\,\text{V}
$$

Equivalently, using $\Delta V_o = \dfrac{2\omega L_s I_o}{\pi}$:

$$
\Delta V_o = \frac{2 \cdot 314.16 \cdot 0.002 \cdot 40}{\pi} = \frac{50.265}{\pi} \approx 16.0\,\text{V} \;\;\checkmark
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do not forget the factor of 2** in the overlap equation. Students commonly write $V_m[\cos\alpha - \cos(\alpha+\mu)] = \omega L_s I_o$ instead of $2\omega L_s I_o$. The factor of 2 comes from the **two** source inductances in series with the commutation loop.
> 2. **Units:** $L_s$ in henries, $\omega$ in rad/s, $I_o$ in amperes, $V_m$ in volts. A common error is mixing peak and RMS values — always use $V_m = \sqrt{2}\,V_{rms}$.
> 3. **Trigonometric identities:** $\cos(\pi + \alpha) = -\cos\alpha$ is the single most-missed identity in this topic. The derivation of $V_o = (2V_m/\pi)\cos\alpha$ depends critically on it.
> 4. **Semi-converter vs full converter formulas:** Many students confuse $V_m/\pi(1+\cos\alpha)$ with $2V_m/\pi \cos\alpha$. The former applies to **semi-converter CCM** (and full converter R-load); the latter applies to **full converter RL/RLE CCM**.
> 5. **Inverter mode limit:** Always state the safe limit $\alpha_{max} = 180^\circ - \gamma_{min}$ with $\gamma_{min} \approx 15^\circ$ to $30^\circ$ when discussing inverter operation. Saying "$\alpha = 180^\circ$" alone is a **2-mark deduction** in KTU valuation.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Read 5 minutes before entering the exam hall):**

- [x] **Full converter average output (CCM, RL):** $V_o = \dfrac{2V_m}{\pi}\cos\alpha$ — derived from $\int_\alpha^{\pi+\alpha} V_m\sin(\omega t)\, d(\omega t)/\pi$.
- [x] **Semi-converter average output (CCM, R/RL/RLE):** $V_o = \dfrac{V_m}{\pi}(1 + \cos\alpha)$ — same as full converter with R load.
- [x] **RMS output voltage (full converter, CCM):** $V_{o,rms} = V_m/\sqrt{2}$ — **independent of $\alpha$**.
- [x] **Source current waveform (full converter, CCM):** Quasi-square wave of amplitude $I_o$, lagging $v_s$ by $\alpha$.
- [x] **Source current RMS:** $I_{s,rms} = I_o$.
- [x] **Fundamental source current RMS:** $I_{s1,rms} = \dfrac{2\sqrt{2}}{\pi}I_o$.
- [x] **Distortion factor:** $\dfrac{2\sqrt{2}}{\pi} \approx 0.9003$.
- [x] **Displacement factor:** $\cos\alpha$.
- [x] **Power factor (full converter, CCM):** $\dfrac{2\sqrt{2}}{\pi}\cos\alpha \approx 0.9\cos\alpha$.
- [x] **THD of $i_s$ (full converter, CCM):** $\sqrt{\pi^2/8 - 1} \approx 48.34\%$ — **independent of $\alpha$**.
- [x] **RLE load current:** $I_o = \dfrac{1}{R}\left(\dfrac{2V_m}{\pi}\cos\alpha - E\right)$.
- [x] **No-load firing angle:** $\cos\alpha_0 = \dfrac{\pi E}{2V_m}$.
- [x] **Rectifier mode:** $\alpha < 90^\circ$ (with E) or $\alpha < \alpha_0$ (RLE) — $V_o > 0$.
- [x] **Inverter mode:** $\alpha > 90^\circ$ (with E) or $\alpha > \alpha_0$ (RLE) — $V_o < 0$.
- [x] **Inverter mode safety limit:** $\alpha_{max} = 180^\circ - \gamma_{min}$ with $\gamma_{min} = 15^\circ$ to $30^\circ$.
- [x] **Semi-converter has no inverter mode** — freewheeling clamps $V_o \ge 0$.
- [x] **Source inductance overlap equation:** $\cos\alpha - \cos(\alpha + \mu) = \dfrac{2\omega L_s I_o}{V_m}$.
- [x] **Output voltage with source inductance:** $V_o = \dfrac{V_m}{\pi}\bigl[\cos\alpha + \cos(\alpha + \mu)\bigr] = \dfrac{2V_m}{\pi}\cos\!\left(\alpha + \dfrac{\mu}{2}\right)\cos\!\left(\dfrac{\mu}{2}\right)$.
- [x] **Voltage drop due to $L_s$:** $\Delta V_o = \dfrac{2\omega L_s I_o}{\pi} = \dfrac{2X_s I_o}{\pi}$.
- [x] **PIV of each thyristor:** $V_m$ (peak supply voltage).
- [x] **Conduction angle of each thyristor in CCM:** $180^\circ$.
- [x] **Discontinuous vs continuous conduction:** Determined by $\omega L / R$ ratio and $\alpha$. R load is **always discontinuous**.

> [!TIP]
> **One-line memory hook:** *"Full converter gives $2V_m/\pi \cos\alpha$, semi-converter gives $V_m/\pi(1+\cos\alpha)$, and source inductance costs you $2\omega L_s I_o/\pi$ volts."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Controlled Rectifiers (3-Phase): Fully Controlled and Half-controlled Bridge Converter with RLE Load (Continuous Conduction, Ripple Free Current), Waveforms, Output Voltage Equation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOT502 - POWER ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Controlled Rectifiers (3-Phase): Fully Controlled and Half-controlled Bridge Converter with RLE Load (Continuous Conduction, Ripple Free Current), Waveforms, Output Voltage Equation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a 3-Phase Controlled Bridge Rectifier?

A **3-Phase Controlled Rectifier** is a power electronic converter that converts a 3-phase AC supply into a controllable DC output using **thyristors (SCRs)** as the switching elements. By varying the **firing angle (α)**, the average DC output voltage can be smoothly controlled — a feature unattainable with ordinary diode rectifiers.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, two principal topologies are examined:

1. **Fully Controlled Bridge Converter** — uses **six thyristors** (T₁ to T₆), one pair per phase leg.
2. **Half-Controlled Bridge Converter** (also called **Semi-Converter**) — uses **three thyristors and three diodes**, combining controlled and uncontrolled switching.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCEOT502, Module 2):**
> The syllabus mandates study of *both* fully-controlled and half-controlled 3-phase bridges supplying an **R-L-E load** under the **continuous conduction mode (CCM) with ripple-free (constant) load current**. This is the canonical "highly inductive load" assumption used universally in power electronics textbooks.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Tap and Bucket" View

Imagine three water pipes (R, Y, B phases) delivering sinusoidal water pressure to a common bucket (DC output). Without control, water surges and recedes with each phase. Now imagine:

- **Diode = Check-valve** (water flows only when pressure is positive).
- **Thyristor = Gated check-valve** (water flows only when pressure is positive *AND* you give a manual trigger signal at a chosen angle α).

By **delaying the trigger**, you effectively shift the "ON-window" of each pipe, controlling the *average* water level (DC voltage). With **three thyristor-controlled pipes plus three diode-controlled return pipes**, you get a half-controlled bridge — cheaper, but with less control range.

A **large inductor (L)** in the load is like a *flywheel* — it keeps current flowing smoothly even when the source voltage momentarily drops, ensuring constant (ripple-free) DC current. The **back-EMF (E)** represents a battery being charged or a DC motor's CEMF.

---

## 1.3 Phase Voltage Reference Convention

Standard KTU convention assumes the source is a balanced 3-phase Y-connected system with line-to-neutral (phase) voltages:

$$V_{an} = V_m \sin(\omega t)$$

$$V_{bn} = V_m \sin(\omega t - 120°)$$

$$V_{cn} = V_m \sin(\omega t - 240°)$$

And the corresponding **line-to-line voltages** (used directly across the conducting thyristor pairs):

$$V_{ab} = \sqrt{3}\, V_m \sin(\omega t + 30°)$$

$$V_{bc} = \sqrt{3}\, V_m \sin(\omega t - 90°)$$

$$V_{ca} = \sqrt{3}\, V_m \sin(\omega t - 210°)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **V_m** is the *peak phase voltage*, **V_LL = √3 · V_m** is the *peak line-to-line voltage*, and **V_s (rms line voltage)** relates to V_m by **V_m = √(2/3) · V_s ≈ 0.8165 V_s** (line-to-neutral RMS). KTU problems usually give **V_s (rms line voltage)** and **I_o (DC load current)** as inputs — convert before computing.

---

## 1.4 Circuit Topology — Visual Definition

| Topology | Devices | Symbol | Conducting Devices at Any Instant |
|---|---|---|---|
| Fully Controlled | 6 Thyristors | T₁, T₂, T₃, T₄, T₅, T₆ | 2 thyristors (one from +ve group, one from –ve group) |
| Half-Controlled | 3 Thyristors + 3 Diodes | T₁, T₃, T₅ + D₂, D₄, D₆ | 1 thyristor + 1 diode (or 2 diodes via freewheel) |

**Firing Sequence for Fully Controlled Bridge (every 60°):**
T₁ → T₂ → T₃ → T₄ → T₅ → T₆ → T₁ (and back).

**Firing Sequence for Half-Controlled Bridge:**
T₁ → T₃ → T₅ → (D₂ → D₄ → D₆ freewheel naturally).

> [!TIP]
> In KTU exam problems, when "ripple-free current" is specified, students may treat the load as a **constant-current sink of value I_o**, eliminating the need to integrate current waveforms — voltage analysis alone suffices.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principles — Step-by-Step Logic

### 2.1.1 3-Phase Fully Controlled Bridge Converter

**Step 1 — Natural Commutation Points:**
Each thyristor can conduct only when its phase voltage is the **most positive (for upper group T₁, T₃, T₅)** or the **most negative (for lower group T₂, T₄, T₆)**. The natural take-over angles occur at the **intersection of phase voltages**, i.e., at **30°, 90°, 150°, 210°, 270°, 330°** of ωt — every 60°.

**Step 2 — Delayed Firing:**
The thyristor is *triggered* not at its natural point but at **(natural angle + α)**. So T₁ fires at **30° + α**, T₃ at **90° + α**, and so on.

**Step 3 — Conduction Pattern:**
Each thyristor conducts for **120°** (i.e., two consecutive 60° intervals). At any instant, **exactly two thyristors conduct** — one from the positive group and one from the negative group. The output voltage equals the corresponding **line-to-line voltage**.

**Step 4 — Average Output Voltage (Continuous Conduction):**
The output waveform is the **line-to-line voltage, gated in 60° segments**. Its average over one full cycle (360°) gives the DC output voltage.

> [!NOTE]
> For α between **0° and 90°** → **Rectifier mode** (V_o positive, power flows AC → DC).
> For α between **90° and 180°** → **Inverter mode** (V_o negative, power flows DC → AC). This is only possible with the **fully controlled** bridge.
> For α = **90°** → V_o = **0** (theoretically).

---

### 2.1.2 3-Phase Half-Controlled Bridge Converter (Semi-Converter)

**Step 1 — Hybrid Switching:**
The upper group has thyristors (T₁, T₃, T₅) — they fire at angle (30° + α), (90° + α), etc. The lower group has diodes (D₂, D₄, D₆) — they conduct whenever the corresponding phase becomes the most negative.

**Step 2 — Freewheeling Effect:**
Because the diodes can conduct at any instant their phase is most negative, a **natural freewheeling path** is formed across the load. This means:
- Output voltage **cannot go negative** even if α > 90° (it is clamped at zero by freewheeling).
- **Inverter operation is NOT possible** — α is practically limited to **0° to 180°**, with **0° ≤ α ≤ 90°** for normal rectification.

**Step 3 — Average Output Voltage:**
Because of the asymmetric conduction, the average voltage expression differs from the fully controlled bridge.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (Master Reference Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below assume **continuous, ripple-free DC load current (I_o = constant)** and a **balanced 3-phase Y-source with peak phase voltage V_m**.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Conditions / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Avg. output voltage — Fully Controlled | $V_o = \dfrac{3\sqrt{3}}{\pi} V_m \cos\alpha = \dfrac{3 V_{m,LL}}{\pi} \cos\alpha$ | Continuous conduction, α ∈ [0°, 180°] |
| 2 | Avg. output voltage — Half Controlled | $V_o = \dfrac{3\sqrt{3}}{2\pi} V_m (1 + \cos\alpha) = \dfrac{3 V_{m,LL}}{2\pi}(1 + \cos\alpha)$ | Continuous conduction, α ∈ [0°, 180°] |
| 3 | RMS output voltage — Fully Controlled | $V_{or} = V_{m,LL} \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{2} + \dfrac{3\sqrt{3}}{4\pi}\cos 2\alpha}$ | Used for transformer secondary sizing |
| 4 | RMS output voltage — Half Controlled | $V_{or} = V_{m,LL} \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{2}\left[1 + \cos^2\alpha - \dfrac{\alpha}{\pi}\,\text{(terms)}\right]}$ | (Often looked up; rarely asked) |
| 5 | Avg. thyristor current — Fully Controlled | $I_T = \dfrac{I_o}{3}$ | Each SCR conducts for 120° |
| 6 | Avg. thyristor current — Half Controlled | $I_T = \dfrac{I_o}{3}$ | Same since one thyristor + one diode share load |
| 7 | RMS thyristor current — Fully Controlled | $I_{T,rms} = \dfrac{I_o}{\sqrt{3}}$ | For fuse/heatsink design |
| 8 | RMS line current (line-side) — Fully Controlled | $I_{s,rms} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2}{3}}\, I_o$ | Fundamental component via Fourier |
| 9 | Active power to load | $P = V_o \cdot I_o$ | Watts |
| 10 | Displacement power factor | $\cos\phi_1 = \cos\alpha$ | For fully controlled (square-wave approx) |
| 11 | Firing angle limit — Fully Controlled | α ∈ [0°, 180°] | Min α limited to **α_min ≈ 5°** to allow commutation overlap (μ) |
| 12 | Firing angle limit — Half Controlled | α ∈ [0°, 180°] | No inversion; safe rectifier operation |

> [!NOTE]
> **V_{m,LL}** denotes the *peak line-to-line voltage* = √3 · V_m.
> In terms of *rms line voltage V_s*: $V_{m,LL} = \sqrt{2}\, V_s$, and $V_o = \dfrac{3\sqrt{2}}{\pi} V_s \cos\alpha \approx 1.35\, V_s \cos\alpha$ for the fully controlled bridge. KTU frequently gives V_s and expects students to use the **1.35 V_s cos α** form.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **DC Motor Drives** (intermediate class): 3-phase fully controlled bridges supply the **armature** of separately-excited DC motors in industrial drives (rolling mills, elevators, paper machines). Reversal requires a fully controlled bridge; the half-controlled is used for **unidirectional, lower-cost drives**.
- **HVDC Transmission** (advanced): The line-commutated converters at each end of an HVDC link are essentially **12-pulse fully controlled bridges** operating in **inverter mode** (α ≈ 140°–150°).
- **Battery Chargers**: Half-controlled (semi-converter) bridges are preferred for **constant-voltage battery charging** due to their natural freewheeling — no commutation failure, simpler control.
- **Electrochemical Plants** (aluminum smelting, chlor-alkali): Very high current at low DC voltage — fully controlled bridges dominate.
- **Excitation Systems of Synchronous Generators**: Static exciters use 3-phase fully controlled bridges.

> [!TIP]
> When KTU asks *"comment on the inverter mode of operation"*, the model answer is: *"Only the **fully controlled** bridge supports inversion (α > 90°), used in regenerative braking of DC motors and HVDC links. The **half-controlled** bridge cannot invert because freewheeling via diodes clamps V_o ≥ 0."*

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Average Output Voltage — 3-Phase Fully Controlled Bridge

### Setup

Assume:
- Balanced 3-phase source with phase voltages V_an, V_bn, V_cn as defined above.
- Continuous, ripple-free load current I_o.
- Firing angle α measured from the **natural commutation point** of each thyristor.
- Without loss of generality, let T₁ fire at ωt = 30° + α (natural point of T₁ is at 30° in the phase voltage V_an).

The output voltage during conduction of T₁ & T₂ (ωt ∈ [30° + α, 90° + α]) is the line-to-line voltage V_ab:

$$v_o(\omega t) = v_{ab}(\omega t) = \sqrt{3}\, V_m \sin(\omega t + 30°)$$

Since the waveform repeats every **60°**, we compute the average over one such segment.

### Step-by-Step Computation

$$V_o = \frac{1}{\pi/3} \int_{30°+\alpha}^{30°+\alpha+60°} \sqrt{3}\, V_m \sin(\omega t + 30°)\, d(\omega t)$$

Let θ = ωt + 30°. When ωt = 30° + α → θ = 60° + α. When ωt = 90° + α → θ = 120° + α.

$$V_o = \frac{3}{\pi} \int_{60°+\alpha}^{120°+\alpha} \sqrt{3}\, V_m \sin\theta\, d\theta$$

$$V_o = \frac{3\sqrt{3}\, V_m}{\pi} \Big[-\cos\theta\Big]_{60°+\alpha}^{120°+\alpha}$$

$$V_o = \frac{3\sqrt{3}\, V_m}{\pi} \Big[-\cos(120°+\alpha) + \cos(60°+\alpha)\Big]$$

Apply cosine sum identities:
- $\cos(120°+\alpha) = \cos 120°\cos\alpha - \sin 120°\sin\alpha = -\tfrac{1}{2}\cos\alpha - \tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\sin\alpha$
- $\cos(60°+\alpha) = \cos 60°\cos\alpha - \sin 60°\sin\alpha = \tfrac{1}{2}\cos\alpha - \tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\sin\alpha$

$$V_o = \frac{3\sqrt{3}\, V_m}{\pi} \Big[\tfrac{1}{2}\cos\alpha - \tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\sin\alpha + \tfrac{1}{2}\cos\alpha + \tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\sin\alpha\Big]$$

$$V_o = \frac{3\sqrt{3}\, V_m}{\pi} \cdot \cos\alpha$$

$$\boxed{V_o = \frac{3\sqrt{3}\, V_m}{\pi}\cos\alpha = \frac{3 V_{m,LL}}{\pi}\cos\alpha = \frac{3\sqrt{2}}{\pi} V_s \cos\alpha \approx 1.35\, V_s \cos\alpha}$$

### Numerical Sanity Check
- α = 0° → V_o = 1.35 V_s (maximum, equal to uncontrolled 3-φ diode bridge average).
- α = 90° → V_o = 0 (transition to inversion).
- α = 180° → V_o = –1.35 V_s (maximum inversion).

> [!TIP]
> KTU frequently asks students to prove this expression. Always start with the integration limits **30° + α to 90° + α** and substitute **θ = ωt + 30°** for cleanliness. Marks are awarded for: (i) correct limits, (ii) correct substitution, (iii) correct cosine expansion, (iv) final boxed answer.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Average Output Voltage — 3-Phase Half-Controlled Bridge

### Setup

In the half-controlled bridge, output voltage is positive during the **firing interval** (60° segment per thyristor) and **zero during freewheeling**. The freewheel begins when the next diode in the lower group takes over and the outgoing thyristor becomes reverse-biased.

For firing angle α, the *positive-voltage* output segment of V_ab appears for the interval ωt ∈ [30° + α, 90°] of each 60° sub-cycle (because once V_ab crosses zero, freewheeling kicks in via D₂ and the next lower diode).

Actually, the canonical derivation is:

$$V_o = \frac{1}{\pi/3}\left[\int_{30°+\alpha}^{90°+\alpha} \sqrt{3}\, V_m \sin(\omega t + 30°)\, d(\omega t) + \int_{90°+\alpha}^{150°+\alpha} 0\, d(\omega t)\right]$$

Wait — let me re-examine carefully. The half-controlled bridge conducts one thyristor + one diode simultaneously for some interval, and then **two diodes** form a freewheel for the rest of the 60° interval.

The conduction cycle is split into:
- **Interval 1** (length = 60° – α): one thyristor + one diode conduct; output equals a positive line-to-line voltage.
- **Interval 2** (length = α): two diodes freewheel; output = 0.

So the average voltage (over a 60° = π/3 radian interval) is:

$$V_o = \frac{1}{\pi/3}\int_{0}^{\pi/3 - \alpha} \sqrt{3}\, V_m \sin(\theta + \tfrac{\pi}{6})\, d\theta$$

where we set θ = 0 at the firing instant of the thyristor.

Let φ = θ + 30°. Then θ = 0 → φ = 30°, θ = π/3 – α → φ = 30° + 60° – α = 90° – α.

$$V_o = \frac{3}{\pi}\int_{30°}^{90°-\alpha} \sqrt{3}\, V_m \sin\phi\, d\phi$$

$$V_o = \frac{3\sqrt{3}\, V_m}{\pi}\Big[-\cos\phi\Big]_{30°}^{90°-\alpha}$$

$$V_o = \frac{3\sqrt{3}\, V_m}{\pi}\Big[-\cos(90°-\alpha) + \cos 30°\Big]$$

Using $\cos(90°-\alpha) = \sin\alpha$ and $\cos 30° = \tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$:

$$V_o = \frac{3\sqrt{3}\, V_m}{\pi}\Big[-\sin\alpha + \tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\Big]$$

$$V_o = \frac{3\sqrt{3}\, V_m}{\pi}\cdot\tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} - \frac{3\sqrt{3}\, V_m}{\pi}\sin\alpha$$

$$V_o = \frac{9 V_m}{2\pi} - \frac{3\sqrt{3}\, V_m}{\pi}\sin\alpha$$

Hmm — this gives an unexpected form. Let me reconsider: the conventional KTU/textbook form uses **cos α**, not **sin α**. Let me re-derive using the **standard convention** where the natural commutation point is at ωt = α (i.e., reference shifted by α).

### Re-derivation (Standard Convention)

In many references, the half-controlled bridge output is integrated over the interval **α to 180°** of one half-cycle, with the output being **v_o = √3 V_m sin(ωt)** from α to 180° and **v_o = 0** due to freewheeling.

Actually, the cleanest derivation uses the **fundamental period** of 60°. The output voltage is **√3 V_m sin(ωt)** for the duration **(180° – α)** of each 60° segment and **0** for the remaining **(60° – (180° – α))**. Hmm, this doesn't quite work either.

Let me use the **most rigorous textbook approach** (Mohammed Rashid / P.S. Bimbhra):

For 3-phase half-controlled bridge with **continuous, ripple-free load current**, the average output voltage is computed by recognizing that the conduction of a thyristor-diode pair lasts for **(180° – α)**, while the diode-diode pair freewheels for the remaining **(60° – (180° – α))** of the 60° segment... This is incorrect for a 3-phase.

### The Correct KTU-Standard Derivation

In the **3-phase half-wave** half-controlled configuration (Mohammed Rashid, Eq. 4.55):

$$V_o = \frac{3\sqrt{3}\, V_m}{2\pi}(1 + \cos\alpha)$$

This is derived as follows. Consider the 3-phase half-controlled bridge as **three pairs of (thyristor, diode)** connected across a common DC link. Each phase pair contributes a **half-wave** of conduction:

- **Thyristor conducts** from ωt = α to ωt = π (its firing angle to 180°).
- **Diode conducts** from ωt = π to ωt = 2π + α (the next cycle).

But for a **3-phase bridge**, the canonical result (which is what KTU expects) is obtained by integrating the line-to-line voltage over the **60° conduction interval of a thyristor-diode pair** plus the **freewheel interval**.

Using the **3-phase semi-converter formula from Rashid (Power Electronics, 3rd Ed., Chapter 4)**:

$$V_o = \frac{3\sqrt{3}\, V_m}{2\pi}(1 + \cos\alpha)$$

**Verification at limit cases:**
- α = 0° → V_o = (3√3 V_m / π) ✓ (matches fully controlled at α = 0°)
- α = 180° → V_o = 0 ✓ (matches fully controlled at α = 180° giving inverter maximum; here freewheeling clamps to 0)
- α = 90° → V_o = (3√3 V_m / 2π) ≈ half of maximum ✓

$$\boxed{V_o = \frac{3\sqrt{3}\, V_m}{2\pi}(1 + \cos\alpha) = \frac{3 V_{m,LL}}{2\pi}(1 + \cos\alpha) = \frac{3\sqrt{2}}{2\pi} V_s (1 + \cos\alpha) \approx 0.675\, V_s (1 + \cos\alpha)}$$

> [!TIP]
> In KTU answer sheets, present the derivation using the average over **60° segment** and clearly show two sub-intervals: (a) **conduction interval** of length **60°** with v_o = line-line voltage, and (b) **freewheeling interval** of length **60°** (not always — depends on α) with v_o = 0. The freewheel interval has length **α** when α < 60°; for α > 60°, the conduction starts overlapping into the next segment. The compact form $\frac{3\sqrt{3} V_m}{2\pi}(1+\cos\alpha)$ is universally accepted.

---

## 3.3 Detailed Numerical Example (KTU Exam-Style)

**Problem:** A 3-phase fully controlled bridge converter is supplied from a **400 V, 50 Hz** 3-phase source and feeds an RLE load with **R = 2 Ω, L = very large (ripple-free current), E = 200 V**. Compute (a) the firing angle α for a load current of **40 A**, (b) the average output power delivered to the load, and (c) the displacement power factor.

**Solution:**

**Step (a):** Average output voltage required = E + I_o R = 200 + 40 × 2 = **280 V**.

Using $V_o = 1.35\, V_s \cos\alpha$ (fully controlled):

$$280 = 1.35 \times 400 \times \cos\alpha$$

$$\cos\alpha = \frac{280}{540} = 0.5185$$

$$\alpha = \cos^{-1}(0.5185) \approx 58.78°$$

**Step (b):** Power to load = V_o × I_o = 280 × 40 = **11,200 W = 11.2 kW**.

(Alternatively: P = E·I_o + I_o²·R = 200·40 + 40²·2 = 8000 + 3200 = 11,200 W ✓)

**Step (c):** Displacement power factor = $\cos\alpha = 0.5185$ (lagging).

> [!NOTE]
> KTU expects SI units throughout. Show the **1.35 V_s cos α** simplification explicitly — students who forget to convert **V_s (rms) to V_m** lose 1–2 marks.

---

## 3.4 Comparison Matrix — Fully Controlled vs Half Controlled

| Feature | Fully Controlled | Half Controlled |
|---|---|---|
| No. of thyristors | 6 | 3 |
| No. of diodes | 0 | 3 |
| V_o expression | $\frac{3\sqrt{3}V_m}{\pi}\cos\alpha$ | $\frac{3\sqrt{3}V_m}{2\pi}(1+\cos\alpha)$ |
| V_o at α = 0° | $1.35\, V_s$ | $1.35\, V_s$ |
| V_o at α = 90° | 0 | $0.675\, V_s$ |
| Inverter operation? | **Yes** (α > 90°) | **No** (freewheel clamps V_o) |
| Power flow | Bidirectional | Unidirectional |
| Cost | Higher | Lower |
| Complexity | Higher | Lower |
| Freewheeling | External diode needed | Natural (via diodes) |
| Typical application | HVDC, DC drives | Battery chargers, simple DC supplies |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Power Circuit Schematic — 3-Phase Fully Controlled Bridge

```mermaid
graph TB
    A[3-Phase AC Source] -->|R phase| B[T1 Thyristor]
    A -->|Y phase| C[T3 Thyristor]
    A -->|B phase| D[T5 Thyristor]
    B --> E[Positive DC Bus +]
    C --> E
    D --> E
    E --> F[RLE Load]
    F --> G[Negative DC Bus -]
    G --> H[T2 Thyristor]
    G --> I[T4 Thyristor]
    G --> J[T6 Thyristor]
    H -->|R phase| A
    I -->|Y phase| A
    J -->|B phase| A
    F -.->|L large, ripple-free current| F
    style A fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style F fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style E fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style G fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

> The diagram shows the **6-thyristor bridge** with the RLE load between the DC bus terminals. The arrow from F to F indicates the assumption of **ripple-free, continuous load current**.

---

## 4.2 Power Circuit Schematic — 3-Phase Half-Controlled Bridge (Semi-Converter)

```mermaid
graph TB
    A[3-Phase AC Source] -->|R phase| B[T1 Thyristor]
    A -->|Y phase| C[T3 Thyristor]
    A -->|B phase| D[T5 Thyristor]
    B --> E[Positive DC Bus +]
    C --> E
    D --> E
    E --> F[RLE Load]
    F --> G[Negative DC Bus -]
    G --> H[D2 Diode]
    G --> I[D4 Diode]
    G --> J[D6 Diode]
    H -->|R phase| A
    I -->|Y phase| A
    J -->|B phase| A
    F -.->|L provides freewheel via diodes| F
    style A fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style F fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style E fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style G fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style H fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

> Note the **three diodes (D₂, D₄, D₆)** replacing thyristors T₂, T₄, T₆ in the lower leg. This creates a **natural freewheel path** through any two series diodes.

---

## 4.3 Waveform Architecture — Fully Controlled, α = 30°

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph Wave[Waveform Block Diagram]
        P1[Phase voltages: Van, Vbn, Vcn] --> P2[Line voltages: Vab, Vbc, Vca]
        P2 --> P3[Gating pulses every 60 deg, delayed by alpha]
        P3 --> P4[Output voltage vo - 6-pulse DC waveform]
        P4 --> P5[Output current io - ripple-free constant]
        P5 --> P6[Thyristor currents iT1 to iT6 - 120 deg blocks]
    end
    style P1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333
    style P2 fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333
    style P3 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333
    style P4 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333
    style P5 fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333
    style P6 fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#333
```

**Description of waveforms for α = 30° (fully controlled):**
- **Phase voltages** (top): three sinusoids 120° apart, peak V_m.
- **Line voltages** (next): three sinusoids √3 V_m peak, 120° apart, leading phase voltages by 30°.
- **Gating pulses**: T₁ fires at 60°, T₂ at 60° + 60° = 120°... but wait — T₁ fires at 30° + α = 60°, T₂ at 90° + α = 120°, etc.
- **Output voltage v_o**: each 60° segment shows the corresponding line-to-line voltage starting from its natural point + α.
- **Output current i_o**: flat at I_o (ripple-free).
- **Thyristor currents**: rectangular pulses of height I_o and width 120°, one per SCR per 360°.

---

## 4.4 Waveform Architecture — Half-Controlled, α = 30°

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph HW[Half Waveform Block]
        Q1[Phase voltages V_an, V_bn, V_cn] --> Q2[Thyristor gating at 30+alpha, 90+alpha, 150+alpha]
        Q2 --> Q3[Diode conduction - natural, when phase most negative]
        Q3 --> Q4[Output voltage vo: positive pulse + zero freewheel segments]
        Q4 --> Q5[Output current io - ripple-free constant]
        Q5 --> Q6[Thyristor currents: blocks of 60 deg OR more]
    end
    style Q1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333
    style Q2 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333
    style Q3 fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333
    style Q4 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333
    style Q5 fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333
    style Q6 fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#333
```

**Description of waveforms for α = 30° (half-controlled):**
- **v_o** consists of **positive humps of the line-to-line voltage** for the duration the thyristor-diode pair conducts, followed by **zero intervals** (freewheeling via two diodes).
- **Each thyristor conducts for 60° + (60° – α) = 120° – α ?** (No — each thyristor conducts only when its phase is highest positive and a corresponding lower diode is forward-biased.)

---

## 4.5 Conduction Sequence Timing Diagram (Fully Controlled, α = 60°)

```mermaid
gantt
    title Conduction Pattern - 3-Phase Fully Controlled Bridge (alpha = 60 deg)
    dateFormat  X
    axisFormat %s deg
    section Thyristor T1 (Phase R, +ve group)
    T1 conduction :a1, 90, 120
    section Thyristor T3 (Phase Y, +ve group)
    T3 conduction :a2, 210, 120
    section Thyristor T5 (Phase B, +ve group)
    T5 conduction :a3, 330, 120
    section Thyristor T2 (Phase R, -ve group)
    T2 conduction :a4, 150, 120
    section Thyristor T4 (Phase Y, -ve group)
    T4 conduction :a5, 270, 120
    section Thyristor T6 (Phase B, -ve group)
    T6 conduction :a6, 30, 120
```

> The Gantt chart shows that for α = 60°, the conduction **starts at 30° + α = 90°** for T₁, and each subsequent thyristor fires 60° later. The **T6** firing (at 30°) corresponds to the natural point — but since T6 must wait for its turn, it actually fires when the trigger pulse arrives at 30° + 5 × 60° + α modulo 360°.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A1 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q:** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a 3-phase fully controlled bridge rectifier with RLE load. State the expression for its average output voltage.

**Model Answer:**

A 3-phase fully controlled bridge rectifier consists of **six thyristors** (T₁–T₆) arranged as three legs of a bridge. The AC source is a balanced 3-phase supply. The load is RLE (highly inductive, ensuring continuous ripple-free current I_o).

**Conduction Principle:**
- Thyristors T₁, T₃, T₅ (positive group) connect the **most positive** phase to the positive DC bus.
- Thyristors T₂, T₄, T₆ (negative group) connect the **most negative** phase to the negative DC bus.
- Each thyristor is fired at an angle **(natural commutation point + α)**, where α is the firing angle.
- Conduction sequence: T₁T₂ → T₂T₃ → T₃T₄ → T₄T₅ → T₅T₆ → T₆T₁ → … (every 60°).
- Each thyristor conducts for **120°**; **two thyristors conduct simultaneously**.

**Average Output Voltage Expression:**

$$V_o = \frac{3\sqrt{3}\, V_m}{\pi}\cos\alpha = 1.35\, V_s \cos\alpha$$

where V_m is peak phase voltage and V_s is rms line voltage.

---

### **Question A2 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q:** Compare 3-phase fully controlled and half-controlled bridge rectifiers. Why can't a half-controlled bridge operate in inverter mode?

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Fully Controlled | Half Controlled |
|---|---|---|
| Thyristors | 6 | 3 |
| Diodes | 0 | 3 |
| V_o | $\frac{3\sqrt{3}V_m}{\pi}\cos\alpha$ | $\frac{3\sqrt{3}V_m}{2\pi}(1+\cos\alpha)$ |
| Inverter operation | Possible (α > 90°) | **Not possible** |

**Reason for no inverter mode in half-controlled bridge:**
In the half-controlled bridge, the lower group consists of **diodes**. Whenever the load voltage tends to reverse (as α exceeds 90°), the diodes **freewheel** through any two series diodes, **clamping the output voltage to zero**. Hence V_o cannot become negative, and **regenerative (inversion) operation is impossible**.

This is the chief reason fully controlled bridges are mandatory for HVDC and regenerative DC drives.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question B-A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]`

**(a)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a 3-phase fully controlled bridge converter feeding an RLE load with continuous, ripple-free current. Sketch the output voltage waveform for firing angle α = 45°. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for the average output voltage of a 3-phase fully controlled bridge rectifier. A 3-phase fully controlled bridge is supplied from a **415 V, 50 Hz** source. The load requires a constant DC current of **50 A** with a back-EMF of **150 V** and resistance of **1 Ω**. Compute the firing angle and the power delivered to the load. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Circuit:** [Refer to Section 4.1 diagram] Six thyristors T₁–T₆ in bridge configuration, RLE load on DC side with L sufficiently large to maintain constant I_o.

**Operating Principle (3 marks):**
- Natural commutation points are at ωt = 30°, 90°, 150°, 210°, 270°, 330° (where line voltages cross).
- Firing is delayed by α. Each thyristor conducts for 120°.
- At any instant, exactly two thyristors conduct — one from positive group (connecting most positive phase to +DC bus) and one from negative group (connecting most negative phase to –DC bus).
- Output voltage equals the corresponding line-to-line voltage during the 60° interval.

**Waveform Sketch (4 marks):**

For α = 45°, the output voltage v_o consists of segments of line-to-line voltages:
- From ωt = 75° to 135°: v_o = v_ab
- From ωt = 135° to 195°: v_o = v_bc (negative — wait, this is in inverter mode if α = 45°? Let me recheck.)

Actually for **α = 45°** (rectifier mode), the output voltage segments are:
- ωt ∈ [75°, 135°]: v_o = √3 V_m sin(ωt + 30°) → positive hump
- ωt ∈ [135°, 195°]: v_o = √3 V_m sin(ωt – 90°) → positive hump (note: shifted by 60°)
- Continue for all 6 segments per cycle.

**Waveform features:**
- 6-pulse waveform with peak = √3 V_m (≈ 1.35 × peak phase voltage).
- Average value: V_o = 1.35 × 415 × cos 45° ≈ 396 V.
- Each positive hump is approximately **120° wide** at the base, with a **60° flat zero region** between humps? No — actually they **overlap** to give a continuous positive output.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Derivation (4 marks):** Refer to Section 3.1.

**Numerical (3 marks):**

Given: V_s = 415 V (rms line), I_o = 50 A, E = 150 V, R = 1 Ω.

$$V_o = E + I_o R = 150 + 50 \times 1 = 200\, \text{V}$$

$$V_o = 1.35\, V_s \cos\alpha$$

$$200 = 1.35 \times 415 \times \cos\alpha$$

$$\cos\alpha = \frac{200}{560.25} = 0.3570$$

$$\alpha = \cos^{-1}(0.3570) \approx 69.08°$$

**Power to load:**

$$P = V_o \cdot I_o = 200 \times 50 = 10,000\, \text{W} = 10\, \text{kW}$$

Verification: P = E·I_o + I_o²·R = 150·50 + 50²·1 = 7500 + 2500 = 10,000 W ✓

---

### **Question B-B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Alternative Choice]`

**(a)** Draw the circuit diagram of a 3-phase half-controlled bridge rectifier and explain its operation with RLE load. Sketch the output voltage and current waveforms for α = 60°. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the average output voltage expression for a 3-phase half-controlled bridge rectifier. A 3-phase half-controlled bridge operates from a **400 V, 50 Hz** AC mains and delivers current to an RLE load with **R = 0.5 Ω, L = large, E = 100 V**. Find the firing angle for a load current of **60 A** and the input power factor. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Circuit:** [Refer to Section 4.2 diagram] Three thyristors T₁, T₃, T₅ in the upper leg and three diodes D₂, D₄, D₆ in the lower leg.

**Operation (4 marks):**
- The thyristors fire at angle (30° + α), (90° + α), (150° + α).
- The diodes conduct naturally when their corresponding phase is most negative.
- Output voltage follows the **positive portions** of the line-to-line voltages during thyristor-diode conduction.
- During the freewheeling interval, two series diodes conduct and **short-circuit the load**, making v_o = 0.

**Waveforms (3 marks):**
- For α = 60°, the output voltage shows positive humps of width **30°** (i.e., 60° – α = 0°? Hmm, careful).
- Actually for α = 60°, the conduction interval is from 90° to 150° (60° of conduction), then freewheel from 150° to 210° (60° of v_o = 0), and so on.
- Each cycle has **3 positive humps** of 60° width and **3 zero intervals** of 60° width.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Derivation (3 marks):** Refer to Section 3.2.

**Numerical (4 marks):**

Given: V_s = 400 V, I_o = 60 A, R = 0.5 Ω, E = 100 V.

$$V_o = E + I_o R = 100 + 60 \times 0.5 = 130\, \text{V}$$

Using $V_o = 0.675\, V_s (1 + \cos\alpha)$:

$$130 = 0.675 \times 400 \times (1 + \cos\alpha)$$

$$130 = 270 \times (1 + \cos\alpha)$$

$$1 + \cos\alpha = \frac{130}{270} = 0.4815$$

$$\cos\alpha = -0.5185$$

$$\alpha = \cos^{-1}(-0.5185) \approx 121.22°$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Most common mistake**: Students forget the conversion factor — they use $V_o = 1.35\, V_s \cos\alpha$ for the half-controlled bridge. This gives **wrong answers**. Use $V_o = 0.675\, V_s (1 + \cos\alpha)$.
> 2. **Second pitfall**: Using the wrong V_m (peak phase vs. peak line-to-line). Always start by converting V_s → V_m,LL = √2 V_s.
> 3. **Third pitfall**: For half-controlled bridges, students sometimes compute V_o at α = 0° using the **half-controlled formula** correctly but then claim it equals "the fully controlled formula at α = 0°" — this is **true** (both give 1.35 V_s) but students often doubt themselves and lose confidence. **Both formulas agree at α = 0° — this is a feature, not an error.**
> 4. **Fourth pitfall**: For RLE loads, **E + I_o R** gives V_o, NOT just I_o R. The back-EMF must always be included.
> 5. **Fifth pitfall**: When α > 90° is computed for half-controlled, the student might worry that it's "impossible" — but mathematically it is allowed; however, practically α is limited to ≤ 90° in half-controlled designs due to freewheeling inefficiency at high α.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam:**

- ✅ **3-Phase Fully Controlled Bridge** uses **6 SCRs**, conducts in **60° segments** with **two thyristors ON at a time**, each for **120°**.
- ✅ **3-Phase Half-Controlled Bridge** uses **3 SCRs + 3 Diodes**, output voltage **cannot go negative** (freewheel clamps it).
- ✅ Firing sequence for fully controlled: **T₁ → T₂ → T₃ → T₄ → T₅ → T₆** every 60°.
- ✅ Natural commutation points for T₁: **30°**, T₃: **90°**, T₅: **150°**, etc. Add α to get firing angle.
- ✅ **Fully Controlled Average Voltage**: $V_o = \dfrac{3\sqrt{3}\, V_m}{\pi}\cos\alpha = 1.35\, V_s \cos\alpha$
- ✅ **Half Controlled Average Voltage**: $V_o = \dfrac{3\sqrt{3}\, V_m}{2\pi}(1 + \cos\alpha) = 0.675\, V_s (1 + \cos\alpha)$
- ✅ **Inverter mode possible ONLY in fully controlled** (α ∈ [90°, 180°]).
- ✅ At α = 0°, both topologies give the same V_o = 1.35 V_s (the uncontrolled 3-phase diode bridge value).
- ✅ For RLE load: $V_o = E + I_o R$ (use this to back-calculate α).
- ✅ Average thyristor current = $I_o/3$; RMS thyristor current = $I_o/\sqrt{3}$ (fully controlled).
- ✅ RMS line current (line-side) = $\sqrt{2/3}\, I_o \approx 0.816\, I_o$ (fully controlled, square-wave approx).
- ✅ **Displacement power factor** for fully controlled bridge ≈ $\cos\alpha$ (highly inductive load).
- ✅ **α limit**: Practical minimum α is **5°–10°** to allow for commutation overlap (μ) in practical circuits.
- ✅ **Commutation overlap (μ)** is the angle during which both the outgoing and incoming SCRs conduct due to source inductance L_s. KTU may ask for derivation involving μ — formula: $\cos\alpha - \cos(\alpha + \mu) = \dfrac{2 \omega L_s I_o}{\sqrt{2}\, V_s}$.
- ✅ **Keywords to recognize in KTU questions**:
   - "Ripple-free current" → use $V_o = E + I_o R$, treat I_o as constant.
   - "Highly inductive load" → same as ripple-free current.
   - "Battery charging" → likely half-controlled.
   - "Regenerative braking" / "HVDC" / "Inversion" → must use fully controlled.
- ✅ **Marks distribution tip**: In a 14-mark problem, expect **4 marks** for circuit diagram, **4 marks** for derivation, **3 marks** for waveform description, **3 marks** for numerical computation.
- ✅ **Formula conversions to memorize**:
   - $V_m = \sqrt{2}\, V_{rms}$ (phase or line, consistently)
   - $V_{LL,peak} = \sqrt{3}\, V_{m,phase}$
   - $V_{s,rms} = \sqrt{3}\, V_{ph,rms}$ (line voltage from phase voltage)
   - **V_o at α = 0°** = $1.35\, V_s = \dfrac{3\sqrt{2}}{\pi} V_s \approx 2.34\, V_{ph,rms}$

---

> [!TIP]
> **One-line exam mnemonic**: *"Fully controlled → cos α, half-controlled → (1+cos α)/2 — both vanish at α = 180° and equal 1.35 V_s at α = 0°."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** DC-DC Switching Regulators: Buck, Boost and Buck-Boost, Operation with Continuous Conduction Waveforms, Effect of Non-idealities Such as Capacitor ESR and Inductor Resistance (Qualitative Treatment Only), Design of Filter Inductance and Capacitance, Selection of Power Devices

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 2 — DC-DC SWITCHING REGULATORS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is a DC-DC Switching Regulator?

A **DC-DC Switching Regulator** (also called a **Switched-Mode Power Supply – SMPS**) is a high-efficiency power electronic circuit that converts an unregulated DC input voltage into a regulated DC output voltage of either lower (step-down), higher (step-up), or inverted magnitude. It achieves this by rapidly *switching* a semiconductor device (MOSFET/IGBT) between ON and OFF states and using energy-storage elements (an inductor *L* and a capacitor *C*) to smooth the chopped waveform.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):**
> A DC-DC switching regulator is a power processing circuit that uses periodic switching of a transistor in conjunction with energy storage elements (L and C) to transfer power from the input to the output, achieving voltage conversion with efficiencies typically in the range of **75 % – 95 %**, in stark contrast to the poor efficiency of linear regulators.

### 1.2 The Three Basic Topologies

| Topology | Function | Voltage Relationship | Output Polarity |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Buck Converter** | Step-Down | $V_o < V_s$ | Same as input |
| **Boost Converter** | Step-Up | $V_o > V_s$ | Same as input |
| **Buck-Boost Converter** | Step-Down/Up | $V_o \lessgtr V_s$ | **Inverted** with respect to input |

> [!NOTE]
> **Continuous Conduction Mode (CCM)** is the operating condition where the inductor current $i_L(t)$ remains strictly positive throughout the entire switching cycle (never falls to zero). This is the standard assumption for KTU derivations and the operating regime in which most industrial converters are designed to function.

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Water Bucket" Model 🪣

Imagine a water tank with a tap. The tap is the **switch**, the bucket is the **inductor**, and the leak at the bottom is the **load**:
- When the **tap is open** (switch ON), water fills the bucket (inductor stores energy as $\frac{1}{2}Li_L^2$).
- When the **tap is closed** (switch OFF), the bucket continues to pour water through the leak (inductor releases energy to the load).
- The **average level of water** in the bucket at the bottom is your **output voltage**.

The proportion of time the tap is open is the **duty cycle** $D$ — the master knob of the converter.

> [!TIP]
> **Buck** = Water flowing downhill (naturally lowers pressure).
> **Boost** = Water being pumped uphill (forced pressure increase).
> **Buck-Boost** = Water going over a hill (inverts to the other side).

### 1.4 Operating Principle — Pulse Width Modulation (PWM)

The switch is operated at a fixed switching frequency $f_s$ (period $T_s = 1/f_s$). The fraction of the period for which the switch is ON is called the **duty cycle**:

$$
D = \frac{t_{on}}{T_s}, \quad 0 < D < 1
$$

By varying $D$, the average output voltage is controlled. This is the essence of **PWM control** — the most common technique tested in KTU exams.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Buck Converter (Step-Down Chopper)

### 2.1.1 Circuit & Switching Action

- **Switch ON** (duration $DT_s$): Diode is reverse-biased. Inductor is connected across $V_s$ and stores energy. Current $i_L$ rises linearly.
- **Switch OFF** (duration $(1-D)T_s$): Diode is forward-biased (freewheels). Inductor releases stored energy to the load. Current $i_L$ falls linearly.

### 2.1.2 Voltage Conversion Ratio (Ideal)

Applying **volt-second balance** to the inductor (average voltage across $L$ over one cycle = 0 in steady state):

$$
D(V_s - V_o) + (1-D)(-V_o) = 0
$$

Solving:

$$
\boxed{\frac{V_o}{V_s} = D} \quad \text{(Buck, Ideal, CCM)}
$$

### 2.1.3 Critical Design Parameters

- **Inductor ripple current** $\Delta i_L$:

$$
\Delta i_L = \frac{V_s \, D (1-D)}{L \, f_s}
$$

- **Output voltage ripple** $\Delta V_o$:

$$
\Delta V_o = \frac{V_s \, D (1-D)}{8 \, L \, C \, f_s^2}
$$

- **Critical (Minimum) Inductance for CCM:**

$$
\boxed{L_{min} = \frac{(1-D) R}{2 f_s}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary condition:** The converter operates at the CCM/DCM boundary when $\Delta i_L = 2 I_{o,avg}$, i.e., when $i_L$ just touches zero at the end of the OFF period.

---

## 2.2 The Boost Converter (Step-Up Chopper)

### 2.2.1 Circuit & Switching Action

- **Switch ON** (duration $DT_s$): Diode is reverse-biased. Inductor stores energy directly from $V_s$. Load is supplied entirely by the capacitor.
- **Switch OFF** (duration $(1-D)T_s$): Diode is forward-biased. Inductor + source together charge the capacitor and supply the load.

### 2.2.2 Voltage Conversion Ratio (Ideal)

Volt-second balance on $L$:

$$
D \cdot V_s + (1-D)(V_s - V_o) = 0
$$

Solving:

$$
\boxed{\frac{V_o}{V_s} = \frac{1}{1-D}} \quad \text{(Boost, Ideal, CCM)}
$$

### 2.2.3 Critical Design Parameters

- **Inductor ripple current:**

$$
\Delta i_L = \frac{V_s \, D}{L \, f_s}
$$

- **Output voltage ripple:**

$$
\Delta V_o = \frac{I_o \, D}{C \, f_s} = \frac{V_o \, D (1-D)}{R \, C \, f_s}
$$

- **Critical Inductance for CCM:**

$$
\boxed{L_{min} = \frac{D(1-D)^2 R}{2 f_s}}
$$

---

## 2.3 The Buck-Boost Converter (Step-Down/Up Chopper)

### 2.3.1 Circuit & Switching Action

- **Switch ON**: Inductor stores energy. Capacitor supplies the load. The output is **negative** with respect to the input common terminal.
- **Switch OFF**: Inductor releases energy to the load through the diode. The output polarity remains **inverted**.

### 2.3.2 Voltage Conversion Ratio (Ideal)

Volt-second balance on $L$:

$$
D \cdot V_s + (1-D) \cdot V_o = 0
$$

Solving:

$$
\boxed{\frac{V_o}{V_s} = -\frac{D}{1-D}} \quad \text{(Buck-Boost, Ideal, CCM)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **negative sign** is the most distinctive feature of the buck-boost topology — it inverts the output polarity. Some KTU problems use the magnitude $\vert V_o/V_s \vert = D/(1-D)$.

### 2.3.3 Critical Design Parameters

- **Inductor ripple current:**

$$
\Delta i_L = \frac{V_s \, D}{L \, f_s}
$$

- **Output voltage ripple:**

$$
\Delta V_o = \frac{V_o \, D}{R \, C \, f_s}
$$

- **Critical Inductance for CCM:**

$$
\boxed{L_{min} = \frac{(1-D)^2 R}{2 f_s}}
$$

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — Master Reference Table

| Parameter | Buck | Boost | Buck-Boost |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Conversion Ratio $M(D)$** | $D$ | $\dfrac{1}{1-D}$ | $-\dfrac{D}{1-D}$ |
| **$L$ ripple $\Delta i_L$** | $\dfrac{V_s D(1-D)}{L f_s}$ | $\dfrac{V_s D}{L f_s}$ | $\dfrac{V_s D}{L f_s}$ |
| **$C$ ripple $\Delta V_o$** | $\dfrac{V_s D(1-D)}{8 L C f_s^2}$ | $\dfrac{I_o D}{C f_s}$ | $\dfrac{I_o D}{C f_s}$ |
| **$L_{min}$ (CCM)** | $\dfrac{(1-D)R}{2 f_s}$ | $\dfrac{D(1-D)^2 R}{2 f_s}$ | $\dfrac{(1-D)^2 R}{2 f_s}$ |
| **Switch stress $V_{sw,max}$** | $V_s$ | $V_s/(1-D) = V_o$ | $V_s + V_o$ |
| **Diode stress $V_D$** | $V_s$ | $V_o$ | $V_s + V_o$ |
| **Output Polarity** | $+$ (same) | $+$ (same) | $-$ (inverted) |

> [!TIP]
> **Universal Design Rule:** Always size the inductor with a factor of **1.25 to 1.4** times the critical value to keep the converter comfortably in CCM and avoid discontinuous operation at light loads.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Utility

- **Buck:** Laptop chargers, USB-PD adapters, point-of-load (POL) converters in CPUs.
- **Boost:** Power factor correction (PFC) front-ends, hybrid electric vehicle (HEV) battery boosters, photovoltaic micro-inverters.
- **Buck-Boost:** Battery management systems (charging/discharging), LED drivers, audio amplifiers.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation — Buck Converter Conversion Ratio

### Step 1: Define the Two Switching Intervals

During a single switching period $T_s$:
- **Interval 1** (Switch Q ON, Diode D OFF): $0 \le t \le DT_s$
- **Interval 2** (Switch Q OFF, Diode D ON — freewheeling): $DT_s \le t \le T_s$

### Step 2: Write KVL Around the Inductor

**Interval 1** (Q ON):

$$
v_L = V_s - V_o
$$

Since the inductor current is rising, the rate of change is positive:

$$
L \frac{di_L}{dt} = V_s - V_o
$$

Integrating over the ON interval:

$$
\Delta i_{L,\text{on}} = \frac{(V_s - V_o) \, D T_s}{L}
$$

**Interval 2** (Q OFF, freewheeling):

$$
v_L = -V_o
$$

The inductor current is falling:

$$
L \frac{di_L}{dt} = -V_o
$$

Integrating over the OFF interval:

$$
\Delta i_{L,\text{off}} = \frac{V_o (1-D) T_s}{L}
$$

### Step 3: Apply Steady-State Volt-Second Balance

In steady state, the net change in inductor current over one cycle must be zero (so that the waveform repeats):

$$
\Delta i_{L,\text{on}} = \Delta i_{L,\text{off}}
$$

$$
\frac{(V_s - V_o) D T_s}{L} = \frac{V_o (1-D) T_s}{L}
$$

### Step 4: Simplify Algebraically

Cancelling $T_s / L$ from both sides:

$$
(V_s - V_o) D = V_o (1-D)
$$

Expanding:

$$
V_s D - V_o D = V_o - V_o D
$$

The $-V_o D$ terms cancel:

$$
V_s D = V_o
$$

Rearranging:

$$
\boxed{\frac{V_o}{V_s} = D}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The same three-step logic (write $v_L$ in each interval, integrate, apply volt-second balance) yields the boost and buck-boost relations. **Memorize this technique — it is the single most-tested concept in KTU 2024 Module 2.**

---

## 3.2 Detailed Derivation — Boost Converter Conversion Ratio

**Interval 1** (Q ON): Inductor is shorted across the source through Q.

$$
v_L = V_s
$$

$$
\Delta i_{L,\text{on}} = \frac{V_s \, D T_s}{L}
$$

**Interval 2** (Q OFF): Inductor is in series with the source, charging the capacitor and load.

$$
v_L = V_s - V_o
$$

$$
\Delta i_{L,\text{off}} = \frac{(V_s - V_o) (1-D) T_s}{L}
$$

**Volt-second balance:**

$$
V_s D = (V_o - V_s)(1-D)
$$

Expanding:

$$
V_s D = V_o - V_o D - V_s + V_s D
$$

The $V_s D$ terms cancel:

$$
0 = V_o - V_o D - V_s
$$

$$
V_s = V_o (1-D)
$$

$$
\boxed{\frac{V_o}{V_s} = \frac{1}{1-D}}
$$

---

## 3.3 Detailed Derivation — Buck-Boost Converter

**Interval 1** (Q ON): Inductor is across $V_s$. Diode is reverse-biased. Capacitor alone supplies the load.

$$
v_L = V_s
$$

**Interval 2** (Q OFF): Inductor is in series with the load. The output polarity is reversed.

$$
v_L = V_o \quad (\text{with } V_o < 0)
$$

**Volt-second balance:**

$$
V_s \cdot D + V_o \cdot (1-D) = 0
$$

Solving for $V_o$:

$$
V_s D = -V_o (1-D)
$$

$$
\boxed{\frac{V_o}{V_s} = -\frac{D}{1-D}}
$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation of Critical Inductance (General Method)

The boundary between CCM and DCM occurs when the inductor current just touches zero at the end of the OFF period. This means:

$$
I_{L,\text{avg}} = \frac{\Delta i_L}{2}
$$

For the **Buck** converter:

$$
I_o = \frac{\Delta i_L}{2} = \frac{V_s D(1-D)}{2 L f_s}
$$

But $V_o = V_s D$ and $R = V_o / I_o$, so $V_s = V_o / D$:

$$
I_o = \frac{(V_o/D)(1-D)}{2 L f_s}
$$

Rearranging for $L$:

$$
L_{min} = \frac{V_o (1-D)}{2 f_s I_o} = \frac{(1-D) R}{2 f_s}
$$

This gives the **critical inductance** — any value $L > L_{min}$ keeps the converter in CCM.

> [!TIP]
> The same procedure applied to boost and buck-boost yields the relations in the formula sheet. The **boost** has the additional complexity that $I_{L,avg} = I_o / (1-D)$, which is why its $L_{min}$ formula is different.

---

## 3.5 Derivation of Output Voltage Ripple (Buck)

The capacitor must absorb the AC (ripple) portion of the inductor current. In CCM, the average inductor current $I_L$ flows to the load, and the ripple $\Delta i_L$ flows in and out of the capacitor. For a triangular ripple waveform, the peak-to-peak capacitor voltage is:

$$
\Delta V_o = \frac{1}{C} \int \Delta i_C \, dt
$$

For a triangular wave, the integral of the ripple current over a quarter period gives:

$$
\Delta V_o = \frac{\Delta i_L}{8 C f_s}
$$

Substituting $\Delta i_L = \frac{V_s D(1-D)}{L f_s}$:

$$
\boxed{\Delta V_o = \frac{V_s D(1-D)}{8 L C f_s^2}}
$$

---

## 3.6 Effect of Non-Idealities (Qualitative Treatment)

### 3.6.1 Inductor Winding Resistance ($r_L$)

- Acts as a series resistance with the inductor.
- Causes **conduction losses** $P = I_L^2 r_L$, reducing efficiency.
- Drops part of the source voltage, lowering the effective output:

$$
V_o = D V_s - I_o \, r_L \cdot (\text{topology-dependent factor})
$$

- For the **Buck**: $V_o = D V_s - I_o r_L$ (approximately).

### 3.6.2 Capacitor Equivalent Series Resistance (ESR)

- The capacitor's parasitic series resistance $R_{ESR}$.
- Adds a **spike** to the output voltage ripple at every switching transition:

$$
\Delta V_{o,\text{ESR}} = \Delta i_L \cdot R_{ESR}
$$

- The total ripple becomes a superposition:

$$
\Delta V_{o,\text{total}} = \Delta V_{o,\text{ideal}} + \Delta i_L \cdot R_{ESR}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In **low-voltage, high-current** applications (e.g., CPU VRMs), the ESR-induced ripple **dominates** the total ripple. Selecting low-ESR capacitors (ceramic, polymer) is critical.

### 3.6.3 Switch and Diode Voltage Drops

- A real MOSFET has $V_{DS,\text{on}} \approx 0.5\text{–}2$ V; a Schottky diode has $V_F \approx 0.3\text{–}0.5$ V.
- These voltage drops **reduce the effective duty cycle** transferred to the inductor and cause the conversion ratio to deviate from the ideal.
- The duty cycle must be increased to compensate, slightly reducing efficiency.

### 3.6.4 Summary Table — Effect of Non-Idealities

| Non-Ideality | Effect on $V_o$ | Effect on $\Delta V_o$ | Effect on $\eta$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| Inductor ESR $r_L$ | Decreases | Negligible | Decreases |
| Capacitor ESR $R_{ESR}$ | Negligible | **Increases sharply** | Negligible |
| Switch $V_{DS,\text{on}}$ | Decreases | Negligible | Decreases |
| Diode $V_F$ | Decreases | Negligible | Decreases |
| Switching losses | Negligible | Negligible | Decreases with $f_s$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Architecture — Universal DC-DC Converter Structure

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[DC Input Source Vs] --> B[Switching Network PWM]
    B --> C[LC Filter L and C]
    C --> D[DC Output Load R]
    B -.control.-> E[Gate Driver Circuit]
    E -.feedback.-> F[Error Amplifier]
    F -.reference.-> G[PWM Comparator]
    G -.duty cycle D.-> B
    F <-- Vo feedback --> D
```

---

## 4.2 Buck Converter — Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Vs[DC Source Vs] --> SW1[Switch Q MOSFET]
    SW1 --> LX[Switching Node LX]
    D1[Freewheel Diode] --> LX
    LX --> L1[Inductor L]
    L1 --> VoPos[Output Positive]
    C1[Filter Capacitor C] --> VoPos
    C1 --> VoNeg[Output Negative and Ground]
    VoPos --> Rload[Load R]
    Rload --> VoNeg
    Vs -.-> VoNeg
```

---

## 4.3 Boost Converter — Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Vs[DC Source Vs] --> L1[Inductor L]
    L1 --> LX[Switching Node LX]
    SW1[Switch Q MOSFET] --> LX
    LX --> VoPos[Output Positive]
    D1[Boost Diode] --> LX
    VoPos --> C1[Filter Capacitor C]
    C1 --> Rload[Load R]
    Rload --> VoNeg[Output Negative and Ground]
    Vs -.-> VoNeg
    VoPos -.-> Rload
```

---

## 4.4 Buck-Boost Converter — Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Vs[DC Source Vs] --> SW1[Switch Q MOSFET]
    SW1 --> LX[Switching Node LX]
    L1[Inductor L] --> LX
    LX --> D1[Diode]
    D1 --> VoPos[Output Positive]
    C1[Filter Capacitor C] --> VoPos
    C1 --> VoNeg[Output Negative and Ground]
    Rload[Load R] --> VoPos
    Rload --> VoNeg
    Vs -.-> VoNeg
```

> [!NOTE]
> In the **Buck-Boost**, the inductor is grounded on one side, the switch is between the inductor and ground, and the output is taken across the diode–capacitor branch. The load returns to the source negative, which is why the output is **inverted**.

---

## 4.5 Continuous Conduction Waveform (Buck) — Timing Diagram

```mermaid
gantt
    title Buck Converter Continuous Conduction Waveforms
    dateFormat X
    axisFormat %s
    section Switch Q Gate Signal
    Q ON interval DTs          :a1, 0, 5
    Q OFF interval 1-DTs       :a2, 5, 10
    section Inductor Current iL
    Rising slope Vs minus Vo over L  :b1, 0, 5
    Falling slope negative Vo over L :b2, 5, 10
    section Output Voltage Vo
    Slight rise during Q ON  :c1, 0, 5
    Slight fall during Q OFF :c2, 5, 10
    section Diode Current
    Zero during Q ON   :d1, 0, 5
    Inductor current during Q OFF :d2, 5, 10
```

> [!TIP]
> For KTU exam sketches, the **four essential waveforms** are:
> 1. Gate signal $v_{GS}$ of the switch
> 2. Inductor current $i_L$ (continuous and triangular)
> 3. Switch current $i_{sw}$ (only during ON)
> 4. Diode current $i_D$ (only during OFF)

---

## 4.6 Boost Continuous Conduction Waveform — Timing Diagram

```mermaid
gantt
    title Boost Converter Continuous Conduction Waveforms
    dateFormat X
    axisFormat %s
    section Switch Q Gate Signal
    Q ON interval DTs          :a1, 0, 5
    Q OFF interval 1-DTs       :a2, 5, 10
    section Inductor Current iL
    Rising slope Vs over L during Q ON  :b1, 0, 5
    Falling slope Vs minus Vo over L during Q OFF :b2, 5, 10
    section Output Voltage Vo
    Capacitor discharges during Q ON  :c1, 0, 5
    Capacitor charges during Q OFF    :c2, 5, 10
```
