# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper 1]**

### **Q1. With a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a Buck converter in Continuous Conduction Mode. Derive its output voltage expression.**

**Model Answer:**

A buck converter (step-down chopper) consists of a switch Q (MOSFET), a freewheeling diode D, an inductor L, and a filter capacitor C connected across a load R.

**Operation:**
- **Mode 1 (Q ON, D reverse-biased):** For duration $DT_s$, the switch is closed. The inductor sees $V_s - V_o$ and stores energy, current rises linearly.
- **Mode 2 (Q OFF, D freewheeling):** For duration $(1-D)T_s$, the inductor releases its stored energy through the diode, current falls linearly.

**Derivation:** Applying volt-second balance to the inductor in steady state:

$$
D(V_s - V_o) - (1-D)V_o = 0
$$

$$
\boxed{V_o = D \, V_s}
$$

Since $0 < D < 1$, $V_o < V_s$, hence it is a **step-down** converter. **(3 marks)**

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

### **Q2. What is Continuous Conduction Mode (CCM) in DC-DC converters? State the condition for the boundary between CCM and Discontinuous Conduction Mode (DCM).**

**Model Answer:**

**Continuous Conduction Mode (CCM)** is the operating regime of a DC-DC switching regulator in which the inductor current $i_L(t)$ remains strictly positive (never falls to zero) throughout the entire switching cycle. The current waveform is a **continuous triangular wave** with average value $I_L$ and peak-to-peak ripple $\Delta i_L$.

**Boundary Condition:** The CCM/DCM boundary is reached when the minimum inductor current touches zero, i.e.,

$$
I_{L,\text{min}} = I_L - \frac{\Delta i_L}{2} = 0
$$

This implies:

$$
\Delta i_L = 2 I_L
$$

Substituting the expressions for each topology, the **critical (minimum) inductance** required to maintain CCM is obtained:

- Buck: $L_{min} = \dfrac{(1-D)R}{2 f_s}$
- Boost: $L_{min} = \dfrac{D(1-D)^2 R}{2 f_s}$
- Buck-Boost: $L_{min} = \dfrac{(1-D)^2 R}{2 f_s}$

For any $L > L_{min}$, the converter operates in CCM. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 2]**

### **Question A: (a)** With the help of a circuit diagram and continuous conduction waveforms, explain the operation of a **Boost converter**. Derive the expression for the output voltage in terms of the duty cycle. **(7 marks)**
### **(b)** A Boost converter operates at a switching frequency of 25 kHz with a duty cycle of 0.4. The input voltage is 24 V and the load resistance is 20 Ω. Calculate the output voltage, average inductor current, and the critical value of inductance for continuous conduction. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) Operation and Derivation (7 marks):**

A **boost converter** consists of an input inductor L, a switch Q (MOSFET) connected to ground, a diode D in series with the output, and a filter capacitor C across the load R.

**Mode 1 — Switch Q ON (duration $DT_s$):** The diode is reverse-biased (since the cathode is at $V_o$ and the anode is at ground). Current flows from $V_s$ through L and Q to ground, storing energy in the inductor. The capacitor alone supplies the load; hence $V_o$ droops slightly.

**Mode 2 — Switch Q OFF (duration $(1-D)T_s$):** The diode becomes forward-biased. Both the source and the inductor now supply the load and recharge the capacitor. $V_o$ rises slightly.

**Inductor voltages:**
- Mode 1: $v_L = V_s$ (rising)
- Mode 2: $v_L = V_s - V_o$ (falling, since $V_o > V_s$)

**Volt-second balance:**

$$
V_s \cdot D + (V_s - V_o)(1-D) = 0
$$

$$
V_s D + V_s - V_s D - V_o + V_o D = 0
$$

$$
V_s = V_o (1-D)
$$

$$
\boxed{\frac{V_o}{V_s} = \frac{1}{1-D}}
$$

**(b) Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

**Given:** $f_s = 25$ kHz, $D = 0.4$, $V_s = 24$ V, $R = 20$ Ω.

**Step 1: Output voltage**

$$
V_o = \frac{V_s}{1-D} = \frac{24}{1-0.4} = \frac{24}{0.6}
$$

$$
\boxed{V_o = 40 \text{ V}}
$$

**Step 2: Load current**

$$
I_o = \frac{V_o}{R} = \frac{40}{20} = 2 \text{ A}
$$

**Step 3: Average inductor current**

For a boost converter, power balance gives $I_{L,\text{avg}} = \dfrac{I_o}{1-D}$:

$$
I_{L,\text{avg}} = \frac{2}{1-0.4} = \frac{2}{0.6}
$$

$$
\boxed{I_{L,\text{avg}} = 3.33 \text{ A}}
$$

**Step 4: Critical inductance**

$$
L_{min} = \frac{D(1-D)^2 R}{2 f_s} = \frac{0.4 \times (0.6)^2 \times 20}{2 \times 25{,}000}
$$

$$
L_{min} = \frac{0.4 \times 0.36 \times 20}{50{,}000} = \frac{2.88}{50{,}000}
$$

$$
\boxed{L_{min} = 57.6 \text{ μH}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students frequently write $I_L = I_o$ for the boost converter. **This is wrong.** For a boost, $I_L = I_o/(1-D)$ because the inductor supplies the load for only a fraction $(1-D)$ of the time. The **input current is the inductor current** in a boost converter.

---

### **Question B (Alternative Choice): (a)** Explain the operation of a **Buck-Boost converter** in Continuous Conduction Mode with circuit diagram and waveforms. Derive the expression for output voltage. **(7 marks)**
### **(b)** Design a **Buck converter** to deliver 12 V at 5 A from a 24 V DC source. The switching frequency is 50 kHz. The peak-to-peak inductor ripple current should be limited to 30 % of the average inductor current, and the output voltage ripple should be less than 1 %. Calculate the required values of L and C. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) Operation and Derivation (7 marks):**

A **buck-boost converter** combines the inductor-grounded topology of the boost with the inverted output polarity. The output is taken across a diode–capacitor branch whose return terminal is the source's negative.

**Mode 1 — Switch Q ON:** The diode is reverse-biased. The inductor is connected across $V_s$ and stores energy. The capacitor discharges into the load.

**Mode 2 — Switch Q OFF:** The inductor current forward-biases the diode, and energy is delivered to the load and capacitor. The polarity of $V_o$ is **negative** with respect to the source common.

**Volt-second balance on L:**

$$
D \cdot V_s + (1-D) \cdot V_o = 0
$$

$$
\boxed{\frac{V_o}{V_s} = -\frac{D}{1-D}}
$$

**(b) Design Problem (7 marks):**

**Given:** $V_s = 24$ V, $V_o = 12$ V, $I_o = 5$ A, $f_s = 50$ kHz, $\Delta i_L / I_L = 0.3$, $\Delta V_o / V_o = 0.01$.

**Step 1: Duty cycle**

$$
D = \frac{V_o}{V_s} = \frac{12}{24} = 0.5
$$

**Step 2: Load resistance**

$$
R = \frac{V_o}{I_o} = \frac{12}{5} = 2.4 \text{ Ω}
$$

**Step 3: Average inductor current** (Buck: $I_L = I_o$)

$$
I_L = 5 \text{ A}
$$

**Step 4: Permissible ripple current**

$$
\Delta i_L = 0.3 \times 5 = 1.5 \text{ A}
$$

**Step 5: Required inductance**

Using $\Delta i_L = \dfrac{V_s D(1-D)}{L f_s}$:

$$
L = \frac{V_s D(1-D)}{\Delta i_L \, f_s} = \frac{24 \times 0.5 \times 0.5}{1.5 \times 50{,}000}
$$

$$
L = \frac{6}{75{,}000}
$$

$$
\boxed{L = 80 \text{ μH}}
$$

**Step 6: Permissible output ripple**

$$
\Delta V_o = 0.01 \times 12 = 0.12 \text{ V}
$$

**Step 7: Required capacitance**

Using $\Delta V_o = \dfrac{V_s D(1-D)}{8 L C f_s^2}$:

$$
C = \frac{V_s D(1-D)}{8 L f_s^2 \Delta V_o} = \frac{6}{8 \times 80 \times 10^{-6} \times (50{,}000)^2 \times 0.12}
$$

$$
C = \frac{6}{8 \times 80 \times 10^{-6} \times 2.5 \times 10^9 \times 0.12}
$$

$$
C = \frac{6}{19{,}200}
$$

$$
\boxed{C = 312.5 \text{ μF}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** In buck converter design, students often forget that the **ripple voltage formula for a buck** is the *quadratic* form $V_s D(1-D)/(8LCf_s^2)$ — distinct from the *linear* form $I_o D/(Cf_s)$ used for boost and buck-boost. Using the wrong formula in a numerical problem will cost **3–4 marks instantly**. Always derive it from the capacitor's integral charge balance.

---

## 5.3 Selection of Power Devices — KTU Brief

| Device | When to Choose | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Power MOSFET** | Low voltage (<200 V), high frequency (>50 kHz), high current | Buck converters, CPU VRMs, SMPS |
| **IGBT** | High voltage (>400 V), moderate frequency (<20 kHz) | Boost PFC, motor drives, induction heating |
| **Schottky Diode** | Low $V_F$, fast recovery, low-voltage outputs | Freewheeling diode in buck converters |
| **Fast-Recovery Diode** | High-voltage outputs, moderate frequency | Boost and buck-boost rectifiers |
| **SiC / GaN Devices** | Very high frequency, high efficiency, high temperature | EV chargers, solar inverters, 5G PSUs |

> [!TIP]
> **Rule of thumb:** The switch voltage rating should be at least **1.5× the maximum voltage stress**, and the current rating should be at least **1.5× the peak inductor current**. Always include a **safety margin** as per KTU design practice.

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Buck** is step-down: $V_o = D V_s$. Output is in phase with input.
- **Boost** is step-up: $V_o = V_s/(1-D)$. Output is in phase with input.
- **Buck-Boost** inverts: $V_o = -D V_s/(1-D)$. Magnitude can be greater or smaller than $V_s$.
- **Volt-second balance** on the inductor is the universal analysis tool — apply it in every KTU derivation.
- **Charge balance** on the capacitor gives the output voltage ripple.
- **Boundary condition for CCM** is $\Delta i_L = 2 I_L$, leading to $L_{min}$ expressions.
- **Inductor resistance $r_L$** lowers $V_o$ and reduces efficiency (loss $= I_L^2 r_L$).
- **Capacitor ESR $R_{ESR}$** adds a sharp spike to $\Delta V_o$ equal to $\Delta i_L \cdot R_{ESR}$.
- **Switch voltage stress:** Buck = $V_s$, Boost = $V_o$, Buck-Boost = $V_s + V_o$.
- **Always design $L$ at 1.25 – 1.4 × $L_{min}$** to maintain CCM robustly.
- **Use a safety factor of 1.5** on voltage and current ratings of switches and diodes.
- **PWM with constant $f_s$** is the standard control technique — only $D$ is varied.
- **CCM vs DCM:** CCM = continuous triangular $i_L$, DCM = $i_L$ touches zero; CCM gives lower peak currents and EMI.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: AC Voltage Controllers (ACVC): 1-Phase Full-wave ACVC with R and RL Loads, Waveforms, RMS Output Voltage, Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOT502 - POWER ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** AC Voltage Controllers (ACVC): 1-Phase Full-wave ACVC with R and RL Loads, Waveforms, RMS Output Voltage, Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

An **AC Voltage Controller (ACVC)** is a power electronic converter that directly converts a fixed AC input voltage to a variable AC output voltage of the **same frequency** without an intermediate DC link. The conversion is achieved through **phase-angle control** of thyristor switches connected in anti-parallel across the AC supply.

A **1-Phase Full-Wave AC Voltage Controller** uses **two thyristors (T1 and T2) connected in anti-parallel** (or equivalently, a single Triac) between the AC source and the load. One thyristor handles the positive half-cycle, while the other handles the negative half-cycle. The output RMS voltage is controlled by varying the **firing angle (α)** — the instant within each half-cycle at which the thyristor is triggered into conduction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** This topic is a high-weightage area in Module 3. Focus areas are: (i) circuit topology, (ii) waveforms for R and RL loads, (iii) derivation of RMS output voltage, and (iv) understanding the **extinction angle β** for RL loads.

### 1.2 Real-World Analogy (Intuition)

Imagine a **water tap** connected to a pipe carrying a constant water flow. The tap is a mechanical valve that you can open and close at any instant during each cycle of water pressure. If you open the tap only at the **peak of each pressure wave**, you get a lot of flow; if you open it at the **end of each wave (just before the next wave begins)**, you get very little flow.

An **ACVC works exactly the same way**:
- The **AC mains supply** = constant pressure water pipe
- The **thyristor pair** = a "tap" that can be electronically opened at any chosen instant
- The **firing angle α** = the point in each half-cycle where the tap opens
- The **load** = the bucket collecting the water

By delaying when we "open the tap" (increasing α), we reduce the average energy delivered to the load. This is how your **domestic light dimmer** or **room heater regulator** works!

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Symbols

| Symbol | Quantity | Unit |
|--------|----------|------|
| $V_s$ | Peak source voltage | V |
| $V_{rms}$ | RMS output voltage | V |
| $\alpha$ | Firing angle (delay from zero-crossing) | radians/degrees |
| $\beta$ | Extinction angle (when current becomes zero) | radians/degrees |
| $\gamma$ | Conduction angle = $\beta - \alpha$ | radians/degrees |
| $\phi$ | Load impedance angle = $\tan^{-1}(\omega L / R)$ | radians/degrees |
| $R$ | Load resistance | $\Omega$ |
| $L$ | Load inductance | H |
| $\omega$ | Angular supply frequency = $2\pi f$ | rad/s |

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants:** Supply frequency in India = **50 Hz**, so $\omega = 2\pi \times 50 = 314.159$ rad/s. Period $T = 20$ ms.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Circuit Topology

A 1-Phase Full-Wave ACVC consists of:
- **AC Source:** $v_s(\omega t) = V_s \sin(\omega t)$
- **Thyristor T1 (Forward):** Anode-to-cathode aligned with positive half-cycle
- **Thyristor T2 (Reverse):** Connected in anti-parallel — conducts negative half-cycle
- **Load:** Resistive (R) or Resistive-Inductive (RL)

Both thyristors are triggered by **isolated gate pulses** at $\omega t = \alpha$ (T1) and $\omega t = \pi + \alpha$ (T2) in each AC cycle.

### 2.2 Operating Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

**Case 1: Purely Resistive (R) Load**

1. For $\omega t < \alpha$ in positive half-cycle: T1 is forward-biased but **not yet triggered**, so it is OFF. Output $v_o = 0$.
2. At $\omega t = \alpha$: Gate pulse applied to T1 → T1 turns ON.
3. For $\alpha \le \omega t \le \pi$: T1 conducts, $v_o = v_s = V_s \sin(\omega t)$.
4. At $\omega t = \pi$: Source voltage reverses. The current through T1 falls to zero naturally → T1 turns OFF (commutates naturally).
5. Same sequence repeats for T2 in the negative half-cycle ($\pi + \alpha$ to $2\pi$).

**Why does T1 turn off at $\pi$ for R load?**
Because in a purely resistive load, **current is in phase with voltage**. When voltage crosses zero at $\pi$, current also crosses zero, naturally commutating the thyristor.

**Case 2: RL Load**

1. For $\omega t < \alpha$: T1 OFF, output $v_o = 0$.
2. At $\omega t = \alpha$: T1 triggered → T1 turns ON. Current begins to build up.
3. For $\alpha \le \omega t \le \pi$: T1 conducts, $v_o = v_s$.
4. At $\omega t = \pi$: Voltage reverses, but **current in inductor cannot change instantly**. Current continues to flow through T1.
5. T1 conducts in the **reverse direction** (still forward-biased in the I-V sense) until $\omega t = \beta$, where the load current finally becomes zero.
6. T1 turns OFF at $\omega t = \beta$ (this is the **extinction angle**).
7. Between $\beta$ and $\pi + \alpha$: Output is zero (no thyristor conducting).
8. At $\omega t = \pi + \alpha$: T2 is triggered → negative half-cycle operation begins.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Concept:** The extinction angle $\beta$ is **NOT** equal to $\pi$ for RL loads. It is greater than $\pi$ because the inductor stores energy that keeps the current flowing even after the source voltage has reversed polarity.

### 2.3 Conditions for Successful Operation (RL Load)

For T1 to **lose forward bias and turn off** before T2 is fired (i.e., for proper natural commutation), the extinction angle $\beta$ must satisfy:

$$\beta \le \pi + \alpha$$

This gives the critical **firing angle condition**:
$$\alpha > \phi \quad \text{where} \quad \phi = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{\omega L}{R}\right)$$

If $\alpha \le \phi$, the load current never becomes zero before the next thyristor is triggered → the controller behaves like a **fully-ON switch** and phase control is lost.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Applicable For |
|---|-----------|---------|----------------|
| 1 | Source voltage | $v_s = V_s \sin(\omega t)$ | Both |
| 2 | RMS output (R Load) | $V_{o,rms} = V_s \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{2\pi}\left[(\pi - \alpha) + \dfrac{\sin 2\alpha}{2}\right]}$ | R Load |
| 3 | RMS output (RL Load) | $V_{o,rms} = V_s \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{2\pi}\left[(\beta - \alpha) + \dfrac{\sin 2\alpha - \sin 2\beta}{2}\right]}$ | RL Load |
| 4 | Average output (R Load) | $V_{o,avg} = \dfrac{V_s}{\pi}(1 + \cos\alpha)$ | R Load |
| 5 | Extinction angle (RL) | $\sin(\beta - \phi) = \sin(\alpha - \phi) \cdot e^{-(\beta - \alpha)/\tan\phi}$ | RL Load |
| 6 | Conduction angle | $\gamma = \beta - \alpha$ | RL Load |
| 7 | Load impedance angle | $\phi = \tan^{-1}(\omega L / R)$ | RL Load |
| 8 | Critical firing angle | $\alpha_{min} = \phi$ | RL Load |
| 9 | Power delivered (R Load) | $P = V_{o,rms}^2 / R$ | Both |
| 10 | Form Factor | $FF = V_{o,rms} / V_{o,avg}$ | Both |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** ACVCs are extensively used in **industrial heating furnaces**, **induction motor soft-starters**, **traction motor speed control**, and **HVAC fan regulators**. The RMS voltage formula is the most important equation for KTU numerical problems.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: RMS Output Voltage for R Load

**Step 1 — Define the output voltage waveform piecewise:**

$$v_o(\omega t) = \begin{cases} 0, & 0 \le \omega t < \alpha \\ V_s \sin(\omega t), & \alpha \le \omega t \le \pi \\ 0, & \pi < \omega t < \pi + \alpha \\ V_s \sin(\omega t), & \pi + \alpha \le \omega t \le 2\pi \end{cases}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the RMS definition over one full cycle:**

$$V_{o,rms} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{0}^{2\pi} v_o^2(\omega t) \, d(\omega t)}$$

**Step 3 — Use symmetry (positive and negative half-cycles are identical):**

$$V_{o,rms} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{2\pi} \cdot 2 \int_{\alpha}^{\pi} V_s^2 \sin^2(\omega t) \, d(\omega t)} = \sqrt{\frac{V_s^2}{\pi} \int_{\alpha}^{\pi} \sin^2(\omega t) \, d(\omega t)}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the trigonometric identity:**

$$\sin^2(\omega t) = \frac{1 - \cos(2\omega t)}{2}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute and integrate:**

$$V_{o,rms}^2 = \frac{V_s^2}{\pi} \int_{\alpha}^{\pi} \frac{1 - \cos(2\omega t)}{2} \, d(\omega t)$$

$$V_{o,rms}^2 = \frac{V_s^2}{2\pi} \left[ \omega t - \frac{\sin(2\omega t)}{2} \right]_{\alpha}^{\pi}$$

**Step 6 — Evaluate the limits:**

$$V_{o,rms}^2 = \frac{V_s^2}{2\pi} \left[ \left(\pi - \frac{\sin 2\pi}{2}\right) - \left(\alpha - \frac{\sin 2\alpha}{2}\right) \right]$$

Since $\sin 2\pi = 0$:

$$V_{o,rms}^2 = \frac{V_s^2}{2\pi} \left[ \pi - \alpha + \frac{\sin 2\alpha}{2} \right]$$

**Step 7 — Final KTU Formula:**

$$\boxed{V_{o,rms} = V_s \sqrt{\frac{1}{2\pi}\left[(\pi - \alpha) + \frac{\sin 2\alpha}{2}\right]}}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation: RMS Output Voltage for RL Load

**Step 1 — Output voltage waveform (T1 conducts from $\alpha$ to $\beta$):**

$$v_o(\omega t) = \begin{cases} 0, & 0 \le \omega t < \alpha \\ V_s \sin(\omega t), & \alpha \le \omega t \le \beta \\ 0, & \beta < \omega t < \pi + \alpha \\ V_s \sin(\omega t), & \pi + \alpha \le \omega t \le 2\pi + \beta - \pi \end{cases}$$

**Step 2 — RMS over one full period:**

$$V_{o,rms}^2 = \frac{V_s^2}{\pi} \int_{\alpha}^{\beta} \sin^2(\omega t) \, d(\omega t)$$

**Step 3 — Apply the identity and integrate:**

$$V_{o,rms}^2 = \frac{V_s^2}{2\pi} \left[ \omega t - \frac{\sin(2\omega t)}{2} \right]_{\alpha}^{\beta}$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the limits:**

$$V_{o,rms}^2 = \frac{V_s^2}{2\pi} \left[ \left(\beta - \frac{\sin 2\beta}{2}\right) - \left(\alpha - \frac{\sin 2\alpha}{2}\right) \right]$$

$$V_{o,rms}^2 = \frac{V_s^2}{2\pi} \left[ (\beta - \alpha) + \frac{\sin 2\alpha - \sin 2\beta}{2} \right]$$

**Step 5 — Final KTU Formula:**

$$\boxed{V_{o,rms} = V_s \sqrt{\frac{1}{2\pi}\left[(\beta - \alpha) + \frac{\sin 2\alpha - \sin 2\beta}{2}\right]}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note for Students:** Notice that the RL load formula **reduces to the R load formula** when $\beta = \pi$, since $\sin 2\pi = 0$ and $(\pi - \alpha) = (\pi - \alpha)$. This is a great **KTU cross-verification trick**.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Extinction Angle Equation for RL Load

For an RL load, applying **KVL across the closed loop** during conduction ($\alpha \le \omega t \le \beta$):

$$V_s \sin(\omega t) = R \cdot i_o(\omega t) + L \frac{di_o(\omega t)}{dt}$$

This is a **first-order linear ODE**. The standard solution is the sum of **forced (steady-state)** and **natural (transient)** responses:

$$i_o(\omega t) = \underbrace{\frac{V_s}{Z} \sin(\omega t - \phi)}_{\text{Steady-state}} + \underbrace{A \cdot e^{-\omega t \cdot R/\omega L}}_{\text{Transient}}$$

where $Z = \sqrt{R^2 + (\omega L)^2}$ and $\phi = \tan^{-1}(\omega L / R)$.

**Applying initial condition** $i_o(\alpha) = 0$ (thyristor just turned ON, inductor was unenergized):

$$0 = \frac{V_s}{Z} \sin(\alpha - \phi) + A \cdot e^{-\alpha \cdot \cot\phi}$$

$$A = -\frac{V_s}{Z} \sin(\alpha - \phi) \cdot e^{\alpha \cot\phi}$$

**Complete current expression:**

$$i_o(\omega t) = \frac{V_s}{Z} \left[ \sin(\omega t - \phi) - \sin(\alpha - \phi) \cdot e^{-(\omega t - \alpha)\cot\phi} \right]$$

**Applying extinction condition** $i_o(\beta) = 0$:

$$0 = \sin(\beta - \phi) - \sin(\alpha - \phi) \cdot e^{-(\beta - \alpha)\cot\phi}$$

**Final transcendental equation (KTU favorite):**

$$\boxed{\sin(\beta - \phi) = \sin(\alpha - \phi) \cdot e^{-(\beta - \alpha)\cot\phi}}$$

This must be solved **iteratively** (e.g., Newton-Raphson method) to find $\beta$ for given $\alpha$ and $\phi$.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: R vs RL Load

| Property | R Load | RL Load |
|----------|--------|---------|
| Extinction angle $\beta$ | $\beta = \pi$ | $\pi < \beta < (\pi + \alpha)$ |
| Conduction angle $\gamma$ | $\gamma = \pi - \alpha$ | $\gamma = \beta - \alpha$ |
| Output voltage at $\omega t = \pi$ | Goes to zero | Continues into negative region |
| Minimum firing angle | $\alpha = 0$ (max voltage) | $\alpha = \phi$ (otherwise T1/T2 conduct simultaneously) |
| Power factor | Higher | Lower (lagging current) |
| Waveform | Symmetric about $\pi$ | Asymmetric; extends past $\pi$ |
| Harmonic content | Lower | Higher |
| Practical use | Heaters, lamps | Motors, transformers, inductors |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Circuit Diagram — 1-Phase Full-Wave ACVC with Anti-Parallel Thyristors

```mermaid
graph LR
    srcA[AC Source Vs sin wt] --- node1{junction}
    node1 --- T1P[T1 Forward Thyristor]
    T1P --- node2{junction mid}
    node1 --- T2P[T2 Reverse Thyristor]
    T2P --- node2
    node2 --- loadA[Load R and L]
    loadA --- srcB[AC Source return]
    g1[Gate Pulse to T1 at alpha] -.-> T1P
    g2[Gate Pulse to T2 at pi plus alpha] -.-> T2P
```

### 4.2 Functional Architecture — Block Diagram of ACVC Operation

```mermaid
graph TD
    startA[AC Mains Supply Vs sin wt] --> recA[Synchronization Circuit Zero Crossing Detector]
    recA --> ctlA[Firing Angle Control Alpha Generator]
    ctlA --> gpgA[Isolated Gate Pulse Generator]
    gpgA --> tsrA[Anti Parallel Thyristor Pair T1 and T2]
    startA --> tsrA
    tsrA --> ldA[Load R or RL]
    ldA --> fbkA[Current and Voltage Sensing]
    fbkA --> ctlA
```

### 4.3 Signal Flow — Waveform Generation Logic

```mermaid
graph TD
    stepA[Supply voltage Vs sin wt] --> stepB{Compare with zero}
    stepB -->|positive half cycle| stepC[Wait for wt = alpha]
    stepB -->|negative half cycle| stepD[Wait for wt = pi plus alpha]
    stepC --> stepE[Trigger T1]
    stepD --> stepF[Trigger T2]
    stepE --> stepG{T1 conducts till current zero}
    stepF --> stepH{T2 conducts till current zero}
    stepG --> stepI[Output voltage = Vs sin wt during conduction]
    stepH --> stepJ[Output voltage = Vs sin wt during conduction]
    stepI --> stepK[Output is zero otherwise]
    stepJ --> stepK
```

### 4.4 Waveform Sketches — Conceptual View

Since Mermaid cannot render graphs of continuous-time waveforms directly, the key waveforms are described below in text form. A student should reproduce these on graph paper during exams.

**For R Load:**

| Waveform | Description |
|----------|-------------|
| $v_s(\omega t)$ | Pure sinusoid: $V_s \sin(\omega t)$ |
| $i_g$ (gate pulses) | Sharp pulse at $\omega t = \alpha$, another at $\omega t = \pi + \alpha$ |
| $v_o(\omega t)$ | Zero for $0$ to $\alpha$, then follows $V_s \sin(\omega t)$ from $\alpha$ to $\pi$, zero from $\pi$ to $\pi+\alpha$, then follows the negative half-cycle from $\pi+\alpha$ to $2\pi$ |
| $i_o(\omega t)$ | $v_o / R$ — in phase with $v_o$ (no phase shift) |

**For RL Load:**

| Waveform | Description |
|----------|-------------|
| $v_s(\omega t)$ | Pure sinusoid: $V_s \sin(\omega t)$ |
| $i_g$ (gate pulses) | Sharp pulse at $\omega t = \alpha$, another at $\omega t = \pi + \alpha$ |
| $v_o(\omega t)$ | Zero for $0$ to $\alpha$, then follows $V_s \sin(\omega t)$ from $\alpha$ to $\beta$ (extends past $\pi$ into negative), zero from $\beta$ to $\pi+\alpha$, then follows negative half-cycle from $\pi+\alpha$ to $2\pi+\beta-\pi$ |
| $i_o(\omega t)$ | Builds up from zero at $\alpha$, peaks between $\alpha$ and $\pi$, gradually decreases, reaches zero at $\beta$. **Lags the voltage by angle $\phi$ in steady state.** |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Drawing Tip:** Always clearly mark the **firing angle $\alpha$** (from zero-crossing of $v_s$ to gate pulse) and the **extinction angle $\beta$** (from zero-crossing to current zero) on the diagram. Examiners specifically look for these markings.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**
*Define an AC voltage controller. List any two of its industrial applications.*

**Model Answer:**
An **AC Voltage Controller (ACVC)** is a thyristor-based power electronic circuit that converts a fixed AC supply voltage into a variable AC output voltage at the same frequency, using **phase-angle control** of anti-parallel-connected thyristors (or a Triac).

**Two industrial applications:**
1. **Lighting control** — theatre dimmers, street lighting intensity regulators.
2. **Industrial heating** — temperature control of electric furnaces, ovens, and smelters.

*(For full 3 marks: brief explanation of how phase control works can be added.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
*For a 1-Phase full-wave ACVC with R load, why does the thyristor turn off naturally at $\omega t = \pi$?*

**Model Answer:**
For a **purely resistive load**, the load current $i_o = v_s / R$ is **in phase** with the supply voltage. The thyristor T1 conducts from $\omega t = \alpha$ to $\omega t = \pi$. At $\omega t = \pi$, the supply voltage $v_s = V_s \sin(\pi) = 0$, so the load current $i_o = 0$ as well. Since the thyristor current has fallen **below the holding current**, T1 turns OFF naturally — this is called **natural commutation** or **line commutation**.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU ESE Rule:** Students must answer EITHER **Question A** OR **Question B** (Module Internal Choice). Both choices are provided below.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a **1-Phase full-wave AC voltage controller with R load**. Draw the voltage and current waveforms and derive the expression for **RMS output voltage**. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A 1-Phase full-wave ACVC has $V_s = 325 \sin(314 t)$ V, $R = 10 \, \Omega$, and firing angle $\alpha = 60°$. Calculate:
   (i) RMS output voltage
   (ii) Average output voltage
   (iii) Power delivered to the load (7 Marks)

**Model Solution for (a):**

1. **Circuit Description:** Two thyristors T1 and T2 connected in anti-parallel between AC source and resistive load R. Gate pulses to T1 at $\omega t = \alpha$ and to T2 at $\omega t = \pi + \alpha$.

2. **Operation:** In each half-cycle, the thyristor is OFF from 0 to $\alpha$, then ON from $\alpha$ to $\pi$ (positive half for T1) and from $\pi+\alpha$ to $2\pi$ (negative half for T2). Output is zero elsewhere.

3. **Waveforms:** (As described in Section 4.4 — R Load)

4. **Derivation:** As derived in Section 3.1:
$$V_{o,rms} = V_s \sqrt{\frac{1}{2\pi}\left[(\pi - \alpha) + \frac{\sin 2\alpha}{2}\right]}$$

**Model Solution for (b):**

Given: $V_s = 325$ V, $\alpha = 60° = \pi/3$ rad, $R = 10 \, \Omega$.

**(i) RMS Output Voltage:**

$$V_{o,rms} = 325 \sqrt{\frac{1}{2\pi}\left[(\pi - \pi/3) + \frac{\sin(2\pi/3)}{2}\right]}$$

$$= 325 \sqrt{\frac{1}{2\pi}\left[\frac{2\pi}{3} + \frac{0.866}{2}\right]}$$

$$= 325 \sqrt{\frac{1}{2\pi}\left[2.094 + 0.433\right]} = 325 \sqrt{\frac{2.527}{6.283}}$$

$$= 325 \sqrt{0.4023} = 325 \times 0.6343 = 206.15 \text{ V}$$

**(ii) Average Output Voltage:**

$$V_{o,avg} = \frac{V_s}{\pi}(1 + \cos\alpha) = \frac{325}{\pi}(1 + \cos 60°)$$

$$= \frac{325}{\pi}(1 + 0.5) = \frac{325 \times 1.5}{\pi} = \frac{487.5}{3.1416} = 155.16 \text{ V}$$

**(iii) Power Delivered:**

$$P = \frac{V_{o,rms}^2}{R} = \frac{(206.15)^2}{10} = \frac{42497.8}{10} = 4249.78 \text{ W} \approx 4.25 \text{ kW}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the operation of a **1-Phase full-wave AC voltage controller with RL load**. Derive the expression for the **extinction angle $\beta$**. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A 1-Phase ACVC feeds an RL load with $R = 10 \, \Omega$, $L = 50 \text{ mH}$, supply $V_s = 230\sqrt{2} \sin(314 t)$ V, and firing angle $\alpha = 90°$. Determine:
   (i) Load impedance angle $\phi$
   (ii) Whether the controller operates in the **phase-control** mode.
   (iii) The RMS output voltage (assume $\beta \approx 235°$). (7 Marks)

**Model Solution for (a):**

1. **Operation:** T1 is triggered at $\omega t = \alpha$. Source voltage is applied to the RL load. Current builds up gradually. At $\omega t = \pi$, voltage reverses but inductor current continues. T1 conducts until $\omega t = \beta$ (extinction angle), when current becomes zero. T2 then operates similarly starting at $\omega t = \pi + \alpha$.

2. **KVL equation:**
$$V_s \sin(\omega t) = R \cdot i_o + L \frac{di_o}{dt}$$

3. **Solution of ODE** (steady-state + transient):
$$i_o(\omega t) = \frac{V_s}{Z} \left[ \sin(\omega t - \phi) - \sin(\alpha - \phi) \cdot e^{-(\omega t - \alpha)\cot\phi} \right]$$

4. **Applying $i_o(\beta) = 0$:**
$$\sin(\beta - \phi) = \sin(\alpha - \phi) \cdot e^{-(\beta - \alpha)\cot\phi}$$

**Model Solution for (b):**

Given: $R = 10 \, \Omega$, $L = 50 \text{ mH} = 0.05$ H, $V_s = 230\sqrt{2} = 325.27$ V, $\alpha = 90°$, $\omega = 314$ rad/s, $\beta \approx 235°$.

**(i) Load impedance angle:**

$$\omega L = 314 \times 0.05 = 15.7 \, \Omega$$

$$\phi = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{\omega L}{R}\right) = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{15.7}{10}\right) = \tan^{-1}(1.57) = 57.5°$$

**(ii) Phase control mode check:**

For proper phase control, we require $\alpha > \phi$.
Here, $\alpha = 90° > 57.5° = \phi$. **Yes, the controller operates in phase-control mode.** ✅

**(iii) RMS output voltage:**

Convert angles to radians: $\alpha = \pi/2 = 1.5708$, $\beta = 235° = 4.102$ rad.

$$V_{o,rms}^2 = \frac{V_s^2}{2\pi}\left[(\beta - \alpha) + \frac{\sin 2\alpha - \sin 2\beta}{2}\right]$$

$$\beta - \alpha = 4.102 - 1.5708 = 2.5312 \text{ rad}$$

$$\sin 2\alpha = \sin(\pi) = 0$$

$$\sin 2\beta = \sin(8.204) = \sin(8.204 - 2\pi) = \sin(1.921) = 0.939$$

$$V_{o,rms}^2 = \frac{(325.27)^2}{2\pi}\left[2.5312 + \frac{0 - 0.939}{2}\right]$$

$$= \frac{105800.6}{6.2832}\left[2.5312 - 0.4695\right]$$

$$= 16840.6 \times 2.0617 = 34720$$

$$V_{o,rms} = \sqrt{34720} = 186.33 \text{ V}$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that COST MARKS:**
> 1. **Confusing $\alpha$ and $\beta$:** Many students measure $\alpha$ from $\pi$ instead of from the zero-crossing. **Always measure from the start of the half-cycle.**
> 2. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in RMS:** The denominator is $2\pi$ for full-wave (one complete cycle), not $\pi$. Wrong denominator = **2 marks lost**.
> 3. **Using $\alpha$ instead of $\beta$ in the RL formula:** The RL load RMS formula has $\beta$ as the upper limit. Substituting $\pi$ instead of $\beta$ gives the wrong answer.
> 4. **Not checking the condition $\alpha > \phi$:** For RL loads, if $\alpha \le \phi$, the formula is **invalid**. Examiners deduct marks if this check is missing.
> 5. **Unit errors:** Converting angles between degrees and radians inconsistently is a common pitfall. Stick to **radians** for the integration.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember

- **ACVC** = AC-AC converter with same input/output frequency, no DC link.
- **Full-wave ACVC** uses **anti-parallel thyristors** or a **Triac**.
- **Phase-angle control** = varying the firing angle $\alpha$.
- **For R load:** $\beta = \pi$, conduction angle = $\pi - \alpha$, RMS formula uses $(\pi - \alpha)$.
- **For RL load:** $\pi < \beta < \pi + \alpha$, requires $\alpha > \phi$ for proper control.
- **Extinction angle equation** is transcendental and solved **iteratively**.
- **RMS formula for R load** is the most-asked derivation; **for RL load**, both formula and $\beta$ equation are important.
- **Applications:** Light dimmers, heater controls, fan regulators, soft starters, traction.
- **Average voltage formula for R load:** $V_{o,avg} = (V_s/\pi)(1 + \cos\alpha)$.
- **Triac = bidirectional thyristor** — equivalent to two anti-parallel thyristors in one package.
- **Commutation in ACVC is natural (line commutation)** — no extra commutation circuit needed (unlike choppers/inverters).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Voltage Source Inverter (VSI)** is a power electronic converter that converts a fixed DC input voltage into a controllable AC output voltage. In the **switch-mode** (or PWM) family, the DC link voltage is **stiff** (i.e., maintained by a large capacitor), and the output waveform is synthesized by high-frequency switching of fully controllable semiconductor devices such as **IGBTs, MOSFETs, or GTOs** — as opposed to the older line-commutated thyristor (SCR) family.

A **single-phase VSI** is realized in two fundamental topologies:

- **Half-Bridge VSI** — uses **2 switches** and **2 split DC-link capacitors**, producing an output of magnitude $\dfrac{V_s}{2}$.
- **Full-Bridge (H-Bridge) VSI** — uses **4 switches** arranged in an "H" configuration, producing a peak output of $V_s$.

The **Sinusoidal Pulse Width Modulation (SPWM)** technique generates gating pulses by comparing a low-frequency **reference (modulating) sine wave** $v_c(t) = V_c \sin(\omega_c t)$ against a high-frequency **triangular carrier wave** $v_{tri}(t)$ of amplitude $\hat{V}_{tri}$. The intersections produce variable-width pulses whose **fundamental component** follows the reference sinusoid.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The course outcome mapping for this topic is **CO3 — "Apply PWM techniques to control VSI outputs"** under Bloom's Level 3 (Apply) and Level 4 (Analyze). Derivations of harmonic spectra and modulator gain ($\dfrac{V_1}{V_s} = M_a$) are high-yield for ESE (End Semester Examination).

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

**Imagine a water tap connected to a high-pressure pipeline.** If you open and close the tap very rapidly — staying open *longer* during certain intervals and *shorter* during others — the *average* flow of water mimics a smooth sine wave, even though the tap itself is either fully ON or fully OFF. The human eye (or a slow-responding water tank) sees a smooth sinusoidal flow.

**Mapping to VSI:**
- The **tap** → Power semiconductor switch (IGBT/MOSFET).
- The **high-pressure pipeline** → Stiff DC source $V_s$.
- The **fast opening/closing pattern** → PWM gating signals.
- The **average flow** → Fundamental component of the output AC voltage.
- The **tap-fully-open duration** → Pulse width (controlled by the modulating sine).

A **half-bridge** is like having **one tap and a center-grounded pipe** (it can only deliver half the swing). A **full-bridge** is like having **two taps working in push-pull** (it can deliver the full swing from $+V_s$ to $-V_s$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The DC source is treated as an **ideal stiff voltage source** (zero internal impedance). In a *Current Source Inverter (CSI)*, the topology is fundamentally different — the DC link behaves as a current source (large inductor) and devices block forward voltage, not current. Never confuse VSI with CSI in KTU exams.

---

## 1.3 Terminology Box

| Term | Symbol | Definition |
|------|--------|------------|
| Modulation Index (Amplitude Ratio) | $M_a$ | $\dfrac{\hat{V}_c}{\hat{V}_{tri}}$ |
| Frequency Modulation Ratio | $M_f$ | $\dfrac{f_{tri}}{f_c}$ |
| Linear Region | $M_a \le 1.0$ | Output fundamental ∝ $M_a$ |
| Overmodulation Region | $1.0 < M_a \le 3.24$ | Non-linear, harmonics rise |
| Square-Wave Mode | $M_a \to \infty$ | Pure 50% duty square wave |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Single-Phase Half-Bridge VSI

The half-bridge consists of **two switches $S_1, S_2$** (each with an anti-parallel diode) and **two equal DC-link capacitors** $C_1, C_2$ that split $V_s$ into $\pm \dfrac{V_s}{2}$. The load is connected between the **midpoint of the switches** and the **midpoint of the capacitors**.

**Switching Logic:**
- $S_1$ ON, $S_2$ OFF → $v_o = +\dfrac{V_s}{2}$
- $S_1$ OFF, $S_2$ ON → $v_o = -\dfrac{V_s}{2}$

**Disadvantages:**
- Requires a **3-wire DC supply** (or split capacitors).
- Output voltage is only half the DC link.
- High DC-link voltage ripple due to capacitor imbalance.

## 2.2 Single-Phase Full-Bridge (H-Bridge) VSI

The full-bridge has **two legs**: Left leg ($S_1, S_2$) and Right leg ($S_3, S_4$). The load is connected between the two leg midpoints.

**Switching States (Bipolar PWM):**

| $S_1$ | $S_2$ | $S_3$ | $S_4$ | $v_o$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| ON | OFF | OFF | ON | $+V_s$ |
| OFF | ON | ON | OFF | $-V_s$ |

In **Unipolar PWM**, each leg is modulated independently, producing output levels of $+V_s, 0, -V_s$ (three-level) instead of the bipolar $\pm V_s$ only (two-level).

## 2.3 Sinusoidal PWM — Operational Theory

A reference sinusoid $v_c(t) = \hat{V}_c \sin(\omega_c t)$ is compared against a triangular carrier $v_{tri}(t)$ of peak $\hat{V}_{tri}$ and frequency $f_{tri}$:

- When $v_c > v_{tri}$ → Switch pair turns ON → Output = $+V_s$ (or $+V_s/2$ in half-bridge).
- When $v_c < v_{tri}$ → Switch pair turns OFF → Output = $-V_s$ (or $-V_s/2$ in half-bridge).

The result is a **pulse train whose average value over each carrier cycle** tracks the reference sine.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Synchronous PWM** keeps $M_f$ an **odd integer** to preserve half-wave and quarter-wave symmetry, which eliminates **all even harmonics** in the output spectrum. Asynchronous (variable $M_f$) is used only in low-cost variable-frequency drives.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Condition / Unit |
|---|----------|---------|------------------|
| 1 | Amplitude Modulation Index | $M_a = \dfrac{\hat{V}_c}{\hat{V}_{tri}}$ | Dimensionless |
| 2 | Frequency Modulation Ratio | $M_f = \dfrac{f_{tri}}{f_c}$ | Odd integer (synchronous) |
| 3 | Peak Fundamental Output (Linear) | $\hat{V}_1 = M_a \cdot V_s$ | For $M_a \le 1$ |
| 4 | Peak Fundamental Output (Full-Bridge, Linear) | $\hat{V}_{o1} = M_a \cdot V_s$ | — |
| 5 | Peak Fundamental Output (Half-Bridge, Linear) | $\hat{V}_{o1} = M_a \cdot \dfrac{V_s}{2}$ | — |
| 6 | RMS Output (Square Wave, Full-Bridge) | $V_{o,rms} = V_s$ | — |
| 7 | RMS Output (Square Wave, Half-Bridge) | $V_{o,rms} = \dfrac{V_s}{\sqrt{2}}$ | — |
| 8 | Overmodulation Limit | $M_a = 3.24$ (SPWM asymptotic) | Boundary to square wave |
| 9 | Total Harmonic Distortion (THD) | $THD = \dfrac{\sqrt{\sum_{h=2}^{\infty} V_{oh}^2}}{V_{o1}} \times 100\%$ | — |
| 10 | Bipolar Harmonic Frequencies | $f_h = (2k-1) \cdot M_f \cdot f_c \pm n \cdot f_c$ | $k = 1,2,3...; \ n = 2,4,6...$ |
| 11 | Unipolar Harmonic Frequencies | $f_h = 2k \cdot M_f \cdot f_c \pm n \cdot f_c$ | $n = 1,3,5...$ (odd sidebands) |
| 12 | Dead (Blanking) Time | $t_d = t_{d,\text{min}} + t_{settling}$ | Typically $1\,\mu s$ to $5\,\mu s$ |
| 13 | Dead-Time Voltage Error | $\Delta V = \dfrac{t_d \cdot V_s}{T_{sw}}$ | Loss of fundamental |
| 14 | Modulator Gain (Linear) | $K_{mod} = \dfrac{\hat{V}_{o1}}{V_s \cdot M_a} = 1$ | Full-bridge |

## 2.5 Operating Modes Summary

1. **Linear Modulation ($0 \le M_a \le 1$)** — $\hat{V}_1 = M_a \cdot V_s$ (full-bridge). The fundamental scales linearly; harmonic content is well-defined and easily filtered.
2. **Overmodulation ($1 < M_a \le 3.24$)** — Sinusoidal reference saturates beyond the carrier. The relationship becomes **non-linear**; $\hat{V}_1$ rises slowly, harmonics **increase sharply**, and the modulation transfer curve bends.
3. **Square-Wave Mode ($M_a \to \infty$)** — Output becomes a pure 50% duty cycle square wave. $\hat{V}_1$ reaches its **maximum theoretical value** of $\dfrac{4 V_s}{\pi} \approx 1.273 V_s$ for full-bridge. No further increase in fundamental is possible — this is the **ceiling** of any VSI.

> [!TIP]
> KTU examiners often test the **modulator gain linearity** boundary. Memorize: At $M_a = 1$, the linear-mode fundamental of full-bridge is exactly $V_s$ (peak), and the corresponding square-wave fundamental is $\dfrac{4 V_s}{\pi} = 1.273 V_s$. The **overmodulation factor** is therefore $1.273$ — this is a frequently asked numerical.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Uninterruptible Power Supplies (UPS)** — require low-THD sinusoidal output at 50/60 Hz.
- **Solar PV Grid-Tie Inverters** — SPWM with $M_a$ varied by MPPT algorithm to control grid power.
- **Induction Motor Drives** — V/Hz control adjusts $M_a$ proportional to frequency in linear region.
- **Active Power Filters** — synthesize compensating currents via high-frequency PWM.
- **Electric Vehicle Traction** — full-bridge VSI with SVM (Space Vector Modulation, an advanced SPWM variant).

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Fourier Series of the Bipolar PWM Output

The bipolar PWM waveform of a single-phase full-bridge VSI is a **two-level** waveform alternating between $+V_s$ and $-V_s$. For synchronous PWM with $M_f$ odd and integer, the output is **half-wave symmetric** ($f(t+T/2) = -f(t)$), which eliminates all even harmonics.

The output can be expressed as a Fourier series:

$$
v_o(t) = \sum_{n=1,3,5,\ldots}^{\infty} B_n \sin(n \omega_c t)
$$

where the coefficient $B_n$ is obtained by integration over a quarter period. Using the standard result from double-Fourier integral analysis (Bowes & Bird, 1975 — classical PWM theory, no specific textbook required for KTU):

$$
B_n = \dfrac{4 V_s}{\pi} \left[ \sin\!\left(\dfrac{n \pi}{2}\right) \cdot J_n\!\left(\dfrac{n \pi M_a}{2}\right) \cdot \text{(sideband terms)} \right]
$$

The complete double-Fourier expansion of the bipolar PWM output voltage of a **full-bridge** VSI is:

$$
v_o(t) = M_a V_s \sin(\omega_c t) + \dfrac{4 V_s}{\pi} \sum_{m=1}^{\infty} \sum_{n=\pm 1,\pm 3,\ldots}^{\pm \infty} \dfrac{J_n(m \pi M_a)}{m} \sin\!\left[ m \omega_{tri} t + n \omega_c t \right]
$$

where $J_n$ is the Bessel function of the first kind of order $n$.

**Step-by-step Explanation:**

- **Step 1:** Apply the **double Fourier integral** technique. The PWM signal is periodic in *two* dimensions — the carrier period $T_{tri}$ and the modulating period $T_c$. The output is therefore expressed as a 2-D Fourier series indexed by $(m, n)$ where $m$ is the carrier index and $n$ is the baseband index.

- **Step 2:** The **principal component** ($m=0$, $n=1$) gives the desired fundamental: $\hat{V}_{o1} = M_a \cdot V_s \sin(\omega_c t)$. This is the **modulation theorem** — the output fundamental amplitude is exactly the modulation index times the DC link.

- **Step 3:** All harmonics lie at frequencies:
$$
f_h = m \cdot f_{tri} \pm n \cdot f_c \quad ; \quad m = 2,4,6,\ldots \; ; \quad n = 1,3,5,\ldots
$$
These are the **sideband harmonics** centered on even multiples of the carrier frequency. For synchronous PWM with $M_f$ odd, the **dominant harmonic cluster** is at $f_{tri} = M_f f_c$.

- **Step 4:** The amplitude of each sideband harmonic is governed by the **Bessel function** ratio $\dfrac{J_n(m \pi M_a)}{m}$. As $M_a$ rises, the principal fundamental grows linearly, while sidebands redistribute energy — which is why THD actually **decreases** as $M_a \to 1$ in the linear region.

## 3.2 Derivation: Control of Fundamental Output Voltage

The peak fundamental component in the **linear region** is derived from the principal term of the Fourier expansion above:

$$
\hat{V}_{o1} = M_a \cdot V_s
$$

For a **half-bridge**, the maximum swing is $\pm V_s/2$, so:

$$
\hat{V}_{o1,\text{half}} = M_a \cdot \dfrac{V_s}{2}
$$

The RMS value of the fundamental:

$$
V_{o1,\text{rms}} = \dfrac{M_a \cdot V_s}{\sqrt{2}}
$$

**At overmodulation boundary** ($M_a = 1$, sinusoidal reference just touches carrier peak):

$$
\hat{V}_{o1,\text{linear,max}} = V_s \quad \text{(full-bridge)}
$$

**At square wave** ($M_a \to \infty$, output is a 50% duty cycle square wave):

$$
v_o(t) = \dfrac{4 V_s}{\pi} \sum_{n=1,3,5,\ldots}^{\infty} \dfrac{\sin(n \omega_c t)}{n}
$$

$$
\therefore \quad \hat{V}_{o1,\text{sq}} = \dfrac{4 V_s}{\pi} \approx 1.273 \cdot V_s
$$

The ratio of maximum square-wave fundamental to maximum linear-mode fundamental:

$$
\text{Overmodulation Gain} = \dfrac{\hat{V}_{o1,\text{sq}}}{\hat{V}_{o1,\text{linear,max}}} = \dfrac{4/\pi}{1} = 1.273
$$

This 27.3% additional headroom is why overmodulation is used to push VSI output to its theoretical ceiling.

## 3.3 Derivation: Bipolar vs Unipolar Harmonic Spectrum

### Bipolar PWM (Full-Bridge, two-level output)

Output: $v_o \in \{+V_s, -V_s\}$

Harmonic frequencies (synchronous, $M_f$ odd integer):

$$
f_h = m f_{tri} \pm n f_c, \quad m = 2,4,6,\ldots \; ; \quad n = 1,3,5,\ldots
$$

Dominant cluster: $f_{tri}, 2 f_{tri}, 3 f_{tri}, \ldots$ with sidebands. **No harmonics exist at $f_c, 2f_c, 3f_c, \ldots$ alone** (these are cancelled by half-wave symmetry).

### Unipolar PWM (Full-Bridge, three-level output)

Each leg is modulated with an **inverted** modulating wave for the complementary switch. The output is the **difference** of two bipolar outputs:

$$
v_o(t) = v_{o,\text{legA}}(t) - v_{o,\text{legB}}(t)
$$

This causes **doubling of the effective carrier frequency** at the load terminals. The harmonic spectrum becomes:

$$
f_h = 2 m f_{tri} \pm n f_c, \quad m = 1,2,3,\ldots \; ; \quad n = 1,3,5,\ldots
$$

The **dominant cluster is at $2 f_{tri}$** instead of $f_{tri}$ — meaning unipolar PWM effectively doubles the apparent switching frequency, halving the filter size for the same ripple.

| Feature | Bipolar PWM | Unipolar PWM |
|---|---|---|
| Output Levels | 2 ($\pm V_s$) | 3 ($+V_s, 0, -V_s$) |
| Dominant Harmonic Cluster | $f_{tri}$ | $2 f_{tri}$ |
| $\dfrac{dv}{dt}$ stress | Higher | Lower |
| Filter size (same ripple) | Larger | Smaller (≈ half) |
| Complexity | Lower | Higher (2 modulators) |
| Switching loss/device | Equal | Equal |
| THD at same $M_f$ | Higher | Lower |

## 3.4 Derivation: Dead (Blanking) Time Need

In a real leg, the **upper and lower switches** cannot change state instantaneously. A turn-off device has a finite **fall time $t_f$** and a turn-on device has a **rise time $t_r$**. If both devices conduct simultaneously, the DC link is short-circuited — this is called a **shoot-through fault**, which destroys the switches within microseconds.

**Minimum safe dead time:**

$$
t_{d,\text{min}} = t_{off,\text{max}} - t_{on,\text{min}} + t_{margin}
$$

where:
- $t_{off,\text{max}}$ is the **maximum** turn-off delay (including storage time for BJTs/IGBTs).
- $t_{on,\text{min}}$ is the **minimum** turn-on delay.
- $t_{margin}$ is the safety margin (typically $0.5\,\mu s$ to $1\,\mu s$).

**Voltage loss per switching cycle:**

$$
\Delta V = \dfrac{V_s \cdot t_d}{T_{sw}}
$$

where $T_{sw} = 1/f_{sw}$ is the switching period.

**Effect on fundamental (per half-cycle of $v_o$):**

$$
\hat{V}_{o1,\text{actual}} = M_a V_s \cdot \left(1 - \dfrac{2 t_d f_{sw}}{\pi}\right)
$$

This produces a **net reduction in fundamental amplitude** and injects **low-order harmonics** (3rd, 5th, 7th) that are difficult to filter. Compensation techniques include:
- **Dead-time compensation circuits** that inject a corrective pulse.
- **Adaptive blanking** that varies $t_d$ with load current polarity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Common KTU Trap:** Many students assume dead time reduces only the *switching loss*. In reality, it **distorts the output waveform** by introducing a **zero-voltage "notch"** in the current zero-crossing region, and this is a major source of **low-order harmonics and torque pulsations in motor drives**.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Total Harmonic Distortion (THD)

The theoretical THD of a bipolar SPWM full-bridge output in the linear region is approximated as:

$$
THD \approx \dfrac{1}{M_a} \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\pi^2}{8} - 1\right) - \sum_{m=1}^{M_f/2} \text{(sideband power)}}
$$

For practical design (large $M_f$ and $M_a \approx 1$):

$$
THD_{\text{bipolar}} \approx 65\% \quad ; \quad THD_{\text{unipolar}} \approx 45\%
$$

These are **asymptotic** values; actual THD is reduced by output LC filtering.

        S4n --- S3n

**Operating principle:**  
- **State 1:** S1 + S4 ON, S2 + S3 OFF → $V_{AB} = +V_s$  
- **State 2:** S2 + S3 ON, S1 + S4 OFF → $V_{AB} = -V_s$  
- **State 3 (Unipolar only):** S1 + S3 ON → $V_{AB} = 0$  
- **State 4 (Unipolar only):** S2 + S4 ON → $V_{AB} = 0$

## 4.3 Bipolar PWM Switching Pattern (Block Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph REF[REFERENCE PATH]
        Vsin[SINE WAVE GENERATOR 50 HZ]:::ref
        Vsin --> Vcnode[VC T OUTPUT TO COMPARATOR]:::ref
    end

    subgraph CAR[CARRIER PATH]
        Vtri[TRIANGULAR WAVE GENERATOR KHZ]:::car
        Vtri --> Vtrinode[VTRI T OUTPUT TO COMPARATOR]:::car
    end

    subgraph CMP[COMPARATOR AND LOGIC]
        CMPN[COMPARATOR]:::cmp

        VCNODE2 --> CMPN
        VTRINODE2 --> CMPN
        CMPN --> GATE1[GATING SIGNAL G1]:::gate
        CMPN --> GATE2[INVERTED GATING G2]:::gate
    end

    subgraph FB[POWER STAGE]
        GATE1 --> FB1[FULL BRIDGE LEG DRIVER]:::fb
        GATE2 --> FB1
        FB1 --> LOAD2[(AC LOAD)]:::load
    end

```

**Working flow:** The sine reference and triangular carrier are fed to a comparator. When $V_c > V_{tri}$, output is HIGH → S1, S4 ON. The complement (G2) drives S2, S3. Result: bipolar two-level waveform.

## 4.4 Unipolar PWM Switching Pattern (Dual Modulator)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Vsin1[SINE REF VC T]:::ref
    Vtri1[TRI CARRIER VTRI T]:::car

    Vsin1 --> INVA[INVERTER MINUS VC T]:::inv
    Vsin1 --> PASA[PASS VC T]:::pass

    PASA --> CMPA[COMPARATOR A]:::cmp
    INVA --> CMPB[COMPARATOR B]:::cmp
    Vtri1 --> CMPA
    Vtri1 --> CMPB

    CMPA --> GA1[GATE S1 S4]:::gate
    CMPB --> GB1[GATE S3 S2]:::gate

    GA1 --> FBN[FULL BRIDGE LEGS]:::fb
    GB1 --> FBN
    FBN --> LOAD3[(LOAD)]:::load

    classDef ref fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#d6b656,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef car fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#6aa84f,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef pass fill:#d9d9d9,stroke:#666,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef inv fill:#ead1dc,stroke:#a64d79,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef cmp fill:#cfe2f3,stroke:#3d85c6,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef gate fill:#fce5cd,stroke:#e69138,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef fb fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef load fill:#c9a0dc,stroke:#4d2a6b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

**Working flow:** The sine reference and its **inverted** version are each compared with the same carrier. Comparator A controls left leg (S1, S2); Comparator B controls right leg (S3, S4). The differential output $V_{AB} = V_A - V_B$ becomes **three-level** ($+V_s, 0, -V_s$), doubling the effective carrier frequency at the load.

## 4.5 Dead-Time Insertion Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    GATE_IN[IDEAL GATE PULSE]:::ref
    GATE_IN --> DT[DEAD TIME BLOCK T_D]:::dt
    DT --> DLY_ON[ON EDGE DELAYED BY T_D]:::gate
    DT --> INV_OFF[OFF EDGE PASSES IMMEDIATELY]:::gate
    DLY_ON --> S1G[S1 GATE]:::sw
    INV_OFF --> S2G[S2 GATE]:::sw
    S1G --> LEG[PHASE LEG]:::fb
    S2G --> LEG
    LEG --> OUT[OUTPUT NODE]:::out

    classDef ref fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#d6b656,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef dt fill:#ea9999,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef gate fill:#fce5cd,stroke:#e69138,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef sw fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef fb fill:#d9d9d9,stroke:#666,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef out fill:#9fc5e8,stroke:#073763,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

**Working flow:** The ideal complementary pair of gate signals is **not** fed directly to the switches. The ON-edge of the upper switch (S1) is delayed by $t_d$ after the lower switch (S2) turns OFF. The OFF-edge of S1 is unaffected. This ensures S1 and S2 are **never simultaneously ON**.
