# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define Voltage Source Inverter. With a neat sketch, explain the operation of a single-phase half-bridge VSI.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Voltage Source Inverter (VSI)** is a power electronic converter that converts a fixed DC input voltage into a variable-frequency, variable-magnitude AC output voltage using fully controllable semiconductor switches, where the DC link is maintained as a **stiff voltage source** by a large filter capacitor.

A **single-phase half-bridge VSI** consists of two switches $S_1$ and $S_2$ (each with an anti-parallel diode) connected in series across a DC source $V_s$, with the load connected between the switch midpoint and the **midpoint of two series DC-link capacitors** $C_1, C_2$ (each charged to $V_s/2$).

**Operation:**
- When $S_1$ is ON and $S_2$ is OFF, the load voltage is $+V_s/2$.
- When $S_1$ is OFF and $S_2$ is ON, the load voltage is $-V_s/2$.

Thus, the output is a two-level square/PWM waveform with a peak of $V_s/2$. The freewheeling diodes provide a path for inductive load currents during switch turn-off.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**What is meant by Sinusoidal Pulse Width Modulation (SPWM)? Define modulation index and frequency ratio.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**SPWM** is a switching technique in which the gating pulses for the VSI switches are generated by comparing a low-frequency **sinusoidal reference (modulating) wave** $v_c(t) = \hat{V}_c \sin(\omega_c t)$ with a high-frequency **triangular carrier wave** $v_{tri}(t)$ of amplitude $\hat{V}_{tri}$. The comparator output produces a pulse train whose average value tracks the reference sinusoid.

**Modulation Index (Amplitude Modulation Ratio):**

$$
M_a = \dfrac{\hat{V}_c}{\hat{V}_{tri}}
$$

It controls the **peak amplitude** of the fundamental output voltage. In the linear region ($M_a \le 1$), $\hat{V}_{o1} = M_a \cdot V_s$ (full-bridge).

**Frequency Modulation Ratio:**

$$
M_f = \dfrac{f_{tri}}{f_c}
$$

It controls the **harmonic spectrum**. For synchronous PWM, $M_f$ is chosen as an **odd integer** to ensure half-wave and quarter-wave symmetry of the output, eliminating even harmonics.

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Jul 2024 Model Question]**

**(a)** With a circuit diagram, explain the operation of a **single-phase full-bridge VSI** fed by a DC source of $V_s = 200\,V$ generating a $50\,\text{Hz}$ quasi-square wave (square wave) output driving a resistive load of $R = 10\,\Omega$. Compute:
   (i) RMS output voltage $V_{o,rms}$
   (ii) RMS load current $I_{o,rms}$
   (iii) Peak fundamental voltage $\hat{V}_{o1}$
   (iv) RMS fundamental voltage $V_{o1,rms}$ **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain **Bipolar and Unipolar Sinusoidal PWM** schemes with switching waveforms and harmonic spectrum sketches. State any **three merits** of unipolar over bipolar. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A (a) — 7 marks**

**Given:** $V_s = 200\,V$, $f = 50\,Hz$, square-wave output, $R = 10\,\Omega$.

**(i) RMS output voltage $V_{o,rms}$:**

For a full-bridge VSI in square-wave mode, $v_o$ alternates between $+V_s$ and $-V_s$ with 50% duty cycle.

$$
V_{o,rms} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{T} \int_0^T v_o^2(t) \, dt} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{T} \left( \int_0^{T/2} V_s^2 \, dt + \int_{T/2}^{T} V_s^2 \, dt \right)}
$$

$$
V_{o,rms} = \sqrt{V_s^2} = V_s = 200\,V
$$

**(ii) RMS load current:**

$$
I_{o,rms} = \dfrac{V_{o,rms}}{R} = \dfrac{200}{10} = 20\,A
$$

**(iii) Peak fundamental voltage $\hat{V}_{o1}$:**

The Fourier series of a 50% duty-cycle square wave of amplitude $V_s$ is:

$$
v_o(t) = \dfrac{4 V_s}{\pi} \sum_{n=1,3,5,\ldots}^{\infty} \dfrac{\sin(n \omega t)}{n}
$$

The fundamental (n=1) has peak:

$$
\hat{V}_{o1} = \dfrac{4 V_s}{\pi} = \dfrac{4 \times 200}{\pi} = \dfrac{800}{\pi} \approx 254.65\,V
$$

**(iv) RMS fundamental voltage:**

$$
V_{o1,rms} = \dfrac{\hat{V}_{o1}}{\sqrt{2}} = \dfrac{254.65}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 180.06\,V
$$

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A (b) — 7 marks**

**Bipolar PWM:** Both legs of the H-bridge switch in **synchronism** using a single comparator. The output $V_{AB}$ is **two-level** ($+V_s$ or $-V_s$). Gating: S1 = S4 = comparator output; S2 = S3 = complement. The harmonic spectrum has dominant cluster at $f_{tri}$ with sidebands at $f_{tri} \pm n f_c$ (n odd).

**Unipolar PWM:** The two legs are modulated **independently** — the right leg uses an **inverted** sine reference. $V_{AB} = V_A - V_B$ is **three-level** ($+V_s, 0, -V_s$). The harmonic spectrum has dominant cluster at $2 f_{tri}$ (effectively doubled switching frequency).

**Three Merits of Unipolar over Bipolar:**

1. **Lower $dv/dt$ stress** on the load (and on motor windings in drives).
2. **Smaller output filter** required for the same ripple amplitude (dominant harmonic is at $2 f_{tri}$).
3. **Lower THD** at the same $M_f$ — reduced by approximately 30% in practice.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **fundamental output voltage** of a single-phase full-bridge VSI operating in **SPWM linear modulation mode**. A full-bridge VSI is fed from a $V_s = 300\,V$ DC link. If the modulating index $M_a = 0.8$ and $M_f = 21$ (synchronous, odd integer), compute:
   (i) Peak fundamental output $\hat{V}_{o1}$
   (ii) RMS fundamental output $V_{o1,rms}$
   (iii) The frequency of the dominant harmonic cluster
   (iv) The total RMS output voltage (square wave limit) **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **three operating regions** of a SPWM VSI (linear, overmodulation, square-wave) with the modulator transfer characteristic $\hat{V}_{o1}/V_s$ vs $M_a$. What is the **need for blanking (dead) time**? Derive the expression for the fundamental voltage loss due to dead time. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B (a) — 7 marks**

**(i) Peak fundamental output $\hat{V}_{o1}$:**

In the linear region, the double-Fourier analysis gives the principal term:

$$
\hat{V}_{o1} = M_a \cdot V_s = 0.8 \times 300 = 240\,V
$$

**(ii) RMS fundamental output:**

$$
V_{o1,rms} = \dfrac{\hat{V}_{o1}}{\sqrt{2}} = \dfrac{240}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 169.71\,V
$$

**(iii) Dominant harmonic cluster frequency:**

For **bipolar** SPWM with synchronous $M_f = 21$:

$$
f_{tri} = M_f \cdot f_c
$$

Assuming $f_c = 50\,\text{Hz}$:

$$
f_{tri} = 21 \times 50 = 1050\,Hz
$$

The dominant harmonic cluster is centered at **$f_{tri} = 1050\,Hz$** with sidebands at $f_{tri} \pm f_c$, $f_{tri} \pm 3f_c$, etc.

**(iv) Total RMS output voltage (square wave limit):**

In the square wave limit ($M_a \to \infty$):

$$
V_{o,rms} = V_s = 300\,V
$$

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B (b) — 7 marks**

**Three Operating Regions of SPWM VSI:**

1. **Linear Region ($0 \le M_a \le 1$):** The reference sinusoid lies entirely within the carrier bounds. The fundamental scales linearly: $\hat{V}_{o1} = M_a \cdot V_s$. Harmonic spectrum is well-defined with dominant cluster at $M_f \cdot f_c$ (bipolar) or $2 M_f \cdot f_c$ (unipolar). This is the **preferred region** for high-quality sinusoidal output.

2. **Overmodulation Region ($1 < M_a \le 3.24$):** The reference exceeds the carrier peak for part of the cycle, causing the comparator to saturate. The $\hat{V}_{o1}$ vs $M_a$ curve becomes **non-linear** and **bends upward** (concave-down). The harmonic content **increases sharply**, especially low-order harmonics ($3^{rd}, 5^{th}, 7^{th}$). The relationship:
$$
\hat{V}_{o1} \approx V_s \cdot \left[ 1 + \dfrac{(M_a - 1)^2}{16} \right]
$$
is a commonly used approximation in the overmodulation range.

3. **Square-Wave Mode ($M_a \to \infty$):** The output becomes a pure 50% duty-cycle square wave. The fundamental reaches its **theoretical maximum**:
$$
\hat{V}_{o1,\text{sq}} = \dfrac{4 V_s}{\pi} \approx 1.273 \cdot V_s
$$
No further control is possible. The harmonic spectrum contains only **odd harmonics** at $\dfrac{1}{n}$ amplitude (1, 1/3, 1/5, ...).

**Need for Blanking (Dead) Time:**

Real semiconductor switches have **non-zero turn-on ($t_{on}$) and turn-off ($t_{off}$) times**. If both the upper and lower switch of a phase leg are gated ON simultaneously (even for a microsecond), the DC link is **short-circuited** (shoot-through fault), leading to catastrophic device failure within microseconds.

To prevent this, a **blanking (dead) time $t_d$** is inserted between the turn-OFF of one switch and the turn-ON of the complementary switch in the same leg:

$$
t_{d,\text{min}} = t_{off,\text{max}} - t_{on,\text{min}} + t_{margin}
$$

**Fundamental Voltage Loss due to Dead Time:**

The dead time creates a **zero-voltage notch** of duration $t_d$ in the output every switching cycle. The average voltage error per half-cycle of output:

$$
\Delta V = \dfrac{t_d \cdot V_s}{T_{sw}} = t_d \cdot V_s \cdot f_{sw}
$$

The reduced fundamental amplitude:

$$
\hat{V}_{o1,\text{actual}} = M_a V_s \cdot \left(1 - \dfrac{2 t_d f_{sw}}{\pi}\right)
$$

This reduction becomes significant at high switching frequencies (e.g., $f_{sw} = 20\,\text{kHz}$, $t_d = 2\,\mu s$ gives a 2.5% loss per half-cycle).

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in KTU Board Exams:**
> 1. **Forgetting the half-bridge factor of 1/2.** Many students write $\hat{V}_{o1} = M_a V_s$ for half-bridge, losing 1 mark. Correct: $\hat{V}_{o1} = M_a \cdot (V_s/2)$.
> 2. **Confusing bipolar vs unipolar harmonic formula.** Bipolar cluster at $f_{tri}$; unipolar at $2f_{tri}$. Mis-writing this is a 2-mark deduction.
> 3. **Using $V_s$ instead of $V_s/\sqrt{2}$ for half-bridge square-wave RMS.** The half-bridge peak is $V_s/2$, not $V_s$.
> 4. **Omitting the safety margin term in dead-time expression.** Examiner explicitly tests whether the student included $t_{margin}$.
> 5. **Not stating that $M_f$ must be odd for synchronous PWM.** This is a 1-mark "definition-style" deduction in part-(a) of long-answer questions.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Half-bridge VSI:** 2 switches, 2 capacitors, output $\pm V_s/2$. Requires **balanced capacitor** voltage.
- **Full-bridge VSI:** 4 switches, output $\pm V_s$. Standard for single-phase UPS, drives, and PV inverters.
- **Modulation Index $M_a = \hat{V}_c / \hat{V}_{tri}$** — controls **amplitude** of fundamental.
- **Frequency Ratio $M_f = f_{tri}/f_c$** — controls **harmonic spectrum**; use **odd integer** for synchronous PWM.
- **Linear mode:** $\hat{V}_{o1} = M_a \cdot V_s$ (full-bridge) or $M_a \cdot (V_s/2)$ (half-bridge).
- **Overmodulation:** $1 < M_a \le 3.24$, harmonic content rises, transfer curve bends.
- **Square wave:** $\hat{V}_{o1,\text{max}} = (4/\pi) \cdot V_s \approx 1.273 \cdot V_s$ — the absolute ceiling of any VSI.
- **Bipolar PWM:** 2-level output, dominant harmonic at $f_{tri}$, simpler control.
- **Unipolar PWM:** 3-level output, dominant harmonic at $2 f_{tri}$, lower $dv/dt$, smaller filter.
- **Dead time:** Mandatory to prevent shoot-through; $t_d = t_{off} - t_{on} + t_{margin}$; causes **fundamental loss and low-order harmonics**.
- **Modulator gain (linear):** $K_{mod} = \hat{V}_{o1}/(M_a V_s) = 1$ for full-bridge.
- **Merits of PWM VSI:** low harmonic distortion (with filter), flexible output control, fast dynamic response.
- **Demerits of PWM VSI:** high switching loss, EMI from high $dv/dt$, need for blanking time, complex gate drivers.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for Harmonic Frequencies:** "**B**ipolar = **B**aseband at $f_{tri}$; **U**nipolar = **U**p-shifted to $2 f_{tri}$." Visualize the doubling in unipolar as the difference of two bipolar signals whose basebands cancel.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Three-Phase Pulse Width Modulated VSI: Fundamental Output Voltage, Linear, Over Modulation and Square Wave Modes, Third Harmonic Injection PWM
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOT502 - POWER ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Three-Phase Pulse Width Modulated VSI: Fundamental Output Voltage, Linear, Over Modulation and Square Wave Modes, Third Harmonic Injection PWM

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Three-Phase PWM VSI (Pulse Width Modulated Voltage Source Inverter)** is a power electronic converter that synthesizes a controllable three-phase AC output voltage from a fixed DC bus by rapidly switching each of the six IGBT/MOSFET devices in the inverter bridge. The switches are driven by comparing a low-frequency sinusoidal reference (modulating signal) with a high-frequency triangular carrier. The **modulation index** is defined as $m_a = \dfrac{A_r}{A_c}$, where $A_r$ is the peak amplitude of the reference and $A_c$ is the peak amplitude of the carrier waveform.

A three-phase VSI consists of three legs (one per phase), each containing two controlled switches with anti-parallel diodes. The three-phase load is typically star-connected, and the line-to-line voltage is the parameter of practical interest. The **operating region** is divided into three distinct modes:

- **Linear Modulation Region** ($0 \le m_a \le 1$): The reference is fully contained within the carrier band; the fundamental of the output voltage is **linearly proportional** to $m_a$.
- **Overmodulation Region** ($1 < m_a \le \dfrac{4}{\pi}$): The reference exceeds the carrier at peaks; pulses saturate to fully ON or OFF. The fundamental-to-$m_a$ relationship becomes **non-linear**, and significant low-order harmonics appear.
- **Square-Wave Mode** ($m_a > \dfrac{4}{\pi}$): Each switch is ON for 180°, producing a quasi-square wave with maximum fundamental content but lowest harmonic quality.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuition — "The Water Tap Analogy":** Imagine a tap that can be only fully ON or fully OFF (just like a power semiconductor switch). You can simulate an intermediate flow rate by rapidly toggling the tap such that, on average, the right amount of water flows. The fraction of time the tap is ON is the **duty ratio**. In a three-phase VSI, three such taps are coordinated (like a three-cylinder engine firing in sequence) so that the **average** of each phase follows a sinusoid, while the **line-to-line difference** provides a clean sinusoidal output for the motor.

- **Overmodulation** is analogous to opening the tap fully for longer than 100% would imply — the system saturates, and any further "demand" cannot increase the average flow proportionally.
- **Third Harmonic Injection PWM** is analogous to **shaping a mountain peak by adding a smaller inverted hill on top**: the peak of the reference is reduced (so the tap can be opened more on average), but the line-to-line voltage — what the motor actually sees — is unchanged because the added harmonic is common to all three phases and cancels in the differential.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics

| Metric | Value / Definition |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Maximum Linear Modulation Index** (SPWM) | $m_a = 1.0$ |
| **Maximum Linear Modulation Index** (THI-PWM) | $m_a = \dfrac{2}{\sqrt{3}} \approx 1.1546$ |
| **Square-Wave Equivalent Modulation Index** | $m_{a,sq} = \dfrac{4}{\pi} \approx 1.273$ |
| **Max Line-to-Line Fundamental (Linear SPWM)** | $V_{ab,1,\text{peak}} = \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} V_{dc} \approx 0.866\, V_{dc}$ |
| **Max Line-to-Line Fundamental (Linear THI-PWM)** | $V_{ab,1,\text{peak}} = V_{dc}$ |
| **Max Line-to-Line Fundamental (Square Wave)** | $V_{ab,1,\text{peak}} = \dfrac{2\sqrt{3}}{\pi} V_{dc} \approx 1.1026\, V_{dc}$ |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principle of Three-Phase SPWM

A three-phase VSI uses **three sinusoidal references** displaced by 120°:

$$v_{a,\text{ref}} = m_a \sin(\omega t), \quad v_{b,\text{ref}} = m_a \sin\!\left(\omega t - \tfrac{2\pi}{3}\right), \quad v_{c,\text{ref}} = m_a \sin\!\left(\omega t - \tfrac{4\pi}{3}\right)$$

A single **triangular carrier** $v_{cr}$ of peak $A_c$ is shared by all three comparators. The gate signal for the upper switch of phase A ($S_1$) is HIGH when $v_{a,\text{ref}} > v_{cr}$. The lower switch ($S_4$) is the complement.

### 2.2 Linear Modulation Mode ($0 \le m_a \le 1$)

**Operating logic:**
1. The reference sinusoid is fully contained inside the carrier band, so no saturation occurs.
2. The duty ratio of each phase is $d(\omega t) = \dfrac{1 + m_a \sin(\omega t)}{2}$.
3. The averaged phase voltage has only the fundamental component (carrier harmonics cancel in line-to-line).
4. The fundamental component is **linear** in $m_a$.

**Key results:**

- Phase voltage fundamental: $V_{an,1,\text{peak}} = \dfrac{m_a V_{dc}}{2}$
- Line-to-line fundamental: $V_{ab,1,\text{peak}} = \sqrt{3}\, V_{an,1,\text{peak}} = \dfrac{\sqrt{3}\, m_a V_{dc}}{2}$
- Line-to-line RMS: $V_{ab,1,\text{rms}} = \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{\sqrt{2}} \cdot \dfrac{m_a V_{dc}}{2} = 0.6124\, m_a V_{dc}$

> [!NOTE]
> At $m_a = 1$, the **maximum sinusoidal line-to-line peak** is $0.866\, V_{dc}$. To go higher, one must enter overmodulation or use harmonic injection.

### 2.3 Overmodulation Mode ($1 < m_a \le \dfrac{4}{\pi}$)

**Operating logic:**
1. The reference sinusoid **exceeds the carrier** near its peaks, so the upper switch stays ON for the full carrier half-cycle in those regions.
2. The pulse widths are no longer linearly related to $m_a$ — they saturate.
3. **Low-order harmonics** (especially 5th, 7th, 11th, 13th) reappear in the line-to-line output.
4. The region is conventionally split:
   - **Region I** ($1 < m_a \le 1.1546$): The phase voltage is partially saturated. Fundamental rises but with sub-linear gain.
   - **Region II** ($1.1546 < m_a < 1.273$): Pulse widths approach 180°; fundamental approaches the square-wave value, harmonic content worsens.

**Key result (limit of Region II):**

$$\lim_{m_a \to 4/\pi} V_{ab,1,\text{peak}} = \dfrac{2\sqrt{3}}{\pi} V_{dc} \approx 1.1026\, V_{dc}$$

### 2.4 Square-Wave Mode ($m_a > 4/\pi$)

**Operating logic:**
1. Each switch is ON for exactly **180°** (full half-cycle), giving a quasi-square wave.
2. No further $m_a$ increase is meaningful; the inverter is at its **absolute maximum** fundamental output.
3. The output contains 5th, 7th, 11th, 13th, … harmonics at significant amplitudes.

**Key results:**

- Phase voltage is a square wave of amplitude $\dfrac{V_{dc}}{2}$
- $V_{an,1,\text{peak}} = \dfrac{4}{\pi} \cdot \dfrac{V_{dc}}{2} = \dfrac{2 V_{dc}}{\pi}$
- $V_{ab,1,\text{peak}} = \sqrt{3} \cdot \dfrac{2 V_{dc}}{\pi} = \dfrac{2\sqrt{3}\, V_{dc}}{\pi}$
- $V_{ab,1,\text{rms}} = \dfrac{\sqrt{6}}{\pi} V_{dc} \approx 0.7798\, V_{dc}$

### 2.5 Third Harmonic Injection PWM (THI-PWM)

**Operating logic:**
1. A third harmonic component (zero-sequence) is added to each reference to **flatten the peak** of the reference waveform.
2. The standard form is:
$$v_{a,\text{ref}} = m_a \left[ \sin(\omega t) + \dfrac{1}{6} \sin(3\omega t) \right]$$
3. The maximum value of $\left[\sin(\omega t) + \tfrac{1}{6}\sin(3\omega t)\right]$ is $\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$ (occurring at $\omega t = 60°$).
4. The constraint $m_a \cdot \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \le 1$ gives the **extended linear range**: $m_{a,\max} = \dfrac{2}{\sqrt{3}} \approx 1.1546$.
5. The 3rd harmonic and its multiples ($3, 9, 15, \ldots$) are **common-mode** signals — they cancel in line-to-line voltage.
6. The **fundamental phase voltage** is unchanged in form: $V_{an,1,\text{peak}} = \dfrac{m_a V_{dc}}{2}$.
7. **Line voltage maximum (linear, with THI)** = $V_{dc}$, which is $\dfrac{2}{\sqrt{3}} \approx 15.47\%$ higher than plain SPWM.

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Quantity | Linear SPWM ($m_a \le 1$) | Linear THI-PWM ($m_a \le 2/\sqrt{3}$) | Square-Wave Mode |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Modulation Index** | $m_a = A_r / A_c$ | $m_a = A_r / A_c$ (effective on fundamental) | $m_a = 4/\pi$ (equivalent) |
| **$V_{an,1,\text{peak}}$** | $\dfrac{m_a V_{dc}}{2}$ | $\dfrac{m_a V_{dc}}{2}$ | $\dfrac{2 V_{dc}}{\pi}$ |
| **$V_{ab,1,\text{peak}}$** | $\dfrac{\sqrt{3}\, m_a V_{dc}}{2}$ | $\dfrac{\sqrt{3}\, m_a V_{dc}}{2}$ | $\dfrac{2\sqrt{3}\, V_{dc}}{\pi}$ |
| **$V_{ab,1,\text{rms}}$** | $\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{\sqrt{2}} \cdot \dfrac{m_a V_{dc}}{2}$ | $\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{\sqrt{2}} \cdot \dfrac{m_a V_{dc}}{2}$ | $\dfrac{\sqrt{6}}{\pi} V_{dc}$ |
| **Maximum $V_{ab,1,\text{peak}}$** | $\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} V_{dc} \approx 0.866 V_{dc}$ | $V_{dc}$ | $\dfrac{2\sqrt{3}}{\pi} V_{dc} \approx 1.1026\, V_{dc}$ |
| **Gain over SPWM Linear** | $1.0$ (reference) | $\dfrac{2}{\sqrt{3}} \approx 1.1546$ | $\dfrac{4}{\pi} \approx 1.273$ |
| **Dominant Harmonics** | Carrier sidebands $\pm m_f, \pm 2m_f$ | Carrier sidebands (no low-order) | 5th, 7th, 11th, 13th, $\ldots$ |
| **Switching Loss** | High (PWM activity) | High (PWM activity) | Minimum (one transition per cycle) |
| **THD** | Lowest | Low | Highest |

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **AC Motor Drives (V/f Control)**: At low speeds, linear PWM gives clean voltage. At high speeds (field-weakening region), overmodulation and square-wave mode extend the usable speed range by up to **27.3%**.
- **Solar / Wind Inverters**: Third-harmonic injection raises the DC-bus utilization by **15.47%**, allowing the inverter to deliver more power from a given PV string voltage.
- **UPS Systems**: Linear SPWM in overmodulation is avoided because of low-order harmonics; THI-PWM offers a clean linear solution with higher DC-bus utilization.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Fundamental Output Voltage in Linear SPWM

**Setup:** Consider phase A of the three-phase VSI. The reference signal is $v_{a,\text{ref}} = m_a \sin(\omega t)$ compared with a triangular carrier of peak $A_c$. The upper switch $S_1$ is ON when $v_{a,\text{ref}} > v_{cr}$.

**Step 1 — Instantaneous Pole Voltage**
Define the **pole voltage** $V_{A0}$ as the voltage of phase A terminal with respect to the negative DC rail:
$$V_{A0}(\omega t) = \begin{cases} V_{dc} & \text{when } S_1 \text{ is ON} \\ 0 & \text{when } S_4 \text{ is ON} \end{cases}$$

**Step 2 — Local Average (Per Carrier Period)**
Within one carrier period centered at angle $\theta = \omega t$, the duty ratio of the upper switch equals the fraction of time the reference exceeds the carrier. For natural sampling, the duty ratio is:
$$d(\theta) = \frac{1 + m_a \sin(\theta)}{2}$$

Therefore, the **locally averaged pole voltage** is:
$$\overline{V_{A0}}(\theta) = d(\theta) \cdot V_{dc} = \frac{V_{dc}}{2} \left[ 1 + m_a \sin(\theta) \right]$$

**Step 3 — Convert Pole Voltage to Phase Voltage**
The phase voltage $V_{an}$ (phase A to load neutral) is related to the pole voltages by:
$$V_{an} = V_{A0} - V_{n0}$$
where $V_{n0}$ is the neutral-point offset. In a balanced three-phase system, the sum of phase voltages is zero, and:
$$V_{n0} = \frac{V_{A0} + V_{B0} + V_{C0}}{3}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the Averaged Pole Voltages**
Substituting the averaged pole voltages:
$$\overline{V_{n0}} = \frac{1}{3}\left[\overline{V_{A0}} + \overline{V_{B0}} + \overline{V_{C0}}\right] = \frac{V_{dc}}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{3}\left[3 + m_a \left(\sin\theta + \sin\!\left(\theta - \tfrac{2\pi}{3}\right) + \sin\!\left(\theta - \tfrac{4\pi}{3}\right)\right)\right]$$

The bracketed sum of three sinusoids is **zero** (since they form a balanced set), so:
$$\overline{V_{n0}} = \frac{V_{dc}}{2}$$

**Step 5 — Resulting Phase Voltage**
$$\overline{V_{an}}(\theta) = \overline{V_{A0}}(\theta) - \overline{V_{n0}} = \frac{V_{dc}}{2}\left[1 + m_a \sin\theta\right] - \frac{V_{dc}}{2} = \frac{m_a V_{dc}}{2} \sin(\omega t)$$

**Step 6 — Fundamental Peak**
$$\boxed{\,V_{an,1,\text{peak}} = \frac{m_a V_{dc}}{2}\,}$$

**Step 7 — Line-to-Line Voltage**
Using $V_{ab} = V_{an} - V_{bn}$ and the 120° phase shift of $V_{bn}$:
$$V_{ab,1,\text{peak}} = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_{an,1,\text{peak}} = \frac{\sqrt{3}\, m_a V_{dc}}{2}$$

This is valid for the entire linear region $0 \le m_a \le 1$.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Square-Wave Mode Fundamental Voltage

**Setup:** In square-wave mode, the upper switch of phase A is ON for exactly 180° ($\theta \in [0, \pi]$) and OFF for the remaining 180° ($\theta \in [\pi, 2\pi]$).

**Step 1 — Square-Wave Pole Voltage Definition**
$$V_{A0}(\theta) = \begin{cases} V_{dc}, & 0 \le \theta < \pi \\ 0, & \pi \le \theta < 2\pi \end{cases}$$

**Step 2 — Square-Wave Phase Voltage**
With load neutral, the phase voltage becomes a square wave of amplitude $\pm V_{dc}/2$ (using midpoint reference):
$$V_{an}(\theta) = \begin{cases} +\dfrac{V_{dc}}{2}, & 0 \le \theta < \pi \\ -\dfrac{V_{dc}}{2}, & \pi \le \theta < 2\pi \end{cases}$$

**Step 3 — Fourier Series of a Square Wave**
A symmetric square wave of amplitude $A$ has Fourier coefficients:
$$V_{an}(\theta) = \sum_{n=1,3,5,\ldots}^{\infty} \frac{4A}{n\pi} \sin(n\theta)$$

With $A = V_{dc}/2$:
$$V_{an}(\theta) = \frac{4(V_{dc}/2)}{\pi} \sin\theta + \frac{4(V_{dc}/2)}{3\pi} \sin(3\theta) + \cdots = \frac{2V_{dc}}{\pi}\sin\theta + \frac{2V_{dc}}{3\pi}\sin(3\theta) + \cdots$$

**Step 4 — Fundamental Phase Voltage Peak**
$$\boxed{\,V_{an,1,\text{peak}} = \frac{2V_{dc}}{\pi} \approx 0.6366\, V_{dc}\,}$$

**Step 5 — Line-to-Line Voltage**
The line voltage $V_{ab} = V_{an} - V_{bn}$. Since $V_{bn}$ is a delayed version of $V_{an}$ by 120°, the fundamental of $V_{ab}$ is:
$$V_{ab,1,\text{peak}} = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_{an,1,\text{peak}} = \frac{2\sqrt{3}\, V_{dc}}{\pi} \approx 1.1026\, V_{dc}$$

**Step 6 — Square-Wave Equivalent Modulation Index**
Comparing with the linear formula $V_{ab,1,\text{peak}} = \tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} m_a V_{dc}$:
$$\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} m_{a,\text{sq}} V_{dc} = \frac{2\sqrt{3}\, V_{dc}}{\pi} \;\Rightarrow\; m_{a,\text{sq}} = \frac{4}{\pi} \approx 1.273$$

This is the **modulation index at which linear PWM would have to operate to produce the same fundamental as the square wave** — but it cannot, because $m_a > 1$ implies overmodulation.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Third Harmonic Injection — Extended Linear Range

**Step 1 — Modified Reference Signal**
The reference for phase A with third harmonic injection is:
$$v_{a,\text{ref}}(\theta) = m_a \left[ \sin\theta + \frac{1}{6}\sin(3\theta) \right]$$

**Step 2 — Find the Maximum of the Bracket Term**
Differentiate to find the peak:
$$\frac{d}{d\theta}\left[\sin\theta + \frac{1}{6}\sin(3\theta)\right] = \cos\theta + \frac{1}{2}\cos(3\theta) = 0$$

Use the identity $\cos(3\theta) = 4\cos^3\theta - 3\cos\theta$:
$$\cos\theta + \frac{1}{2}(4\cos^3\theta - 3\cos\theta) = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; 2\cos^3\theta - \frac{1}{2}\cos\theta = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \cos\theta\left[2\cos^2\theta - \tfrac{1}{2}\right] = 0$$

The non-trivial solution is $\cos\theta = \pm \tfrac{1}{2}$, so $\theta = 60°, 120°, 240°, 300°$.

**Step 3 — Evaluate the Maximum**
At $\theta = 60°$:
$$\sin 60° + \frac{1}{6}\sin 180° = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} + 0 = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \approx 0.866$$

**Step 4 — Constrain $m_a$ for Linear Operation**
The peak of $v_{a,\text{ref}}$ must not exceed $A_c = 1$ (carrier peak, normalized):
$$m_a \cdot \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \le 1 \;\Rightarrow\; m_a \le \frac{2}{\sqrt{3}} \approx 1.1546$$

**Step 5 — Resulting Fundamental Voltage**
The fundamental of the phase voltage is unaffected by the injected 3rd harmonic (it appears in $V_{an}$ but cancels in $V_{ab}$):
$$V_{an,1,\text{peak}} = \frac{m_a V_{dc}}{2} \quad \text{(at } m_a = 2/\sqrt{3}\text{)} = \frac{V_{dc}}{\sqrt{3}}$$

**Step 6 — Line-to-Line Voltage Maximum**
$$V_{ab,1,\text{peak},\max} = \sqrt{3} \cdot \frac{V_{dc}}{\sqrt{3}} = V_{dc}$$

**Step 7 — Gain Over SPWM**
$$\text{Gain} = \frac{V_{dc}}{\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} V_{dc}} = \frac{2}{\sqrt{3}} \approx 1.1546 \quad (+15.47\%)$$

**Step 8 — Why the 3rd Harmonic Cancels in $V_{ab}$**
For phase B, the reference is $v_{b,\text{ref}} = m_a[\sin(\theta - 2\pi/3) + (1/6)\sin(3\theta - 2\pi)] = m_a[\sin(\theta - 2\pi/3) + (1/6)\sin(3\theta)]$ — the 3rd harmonic term is **unchanged** in phase (since $3 \times 2\pi/3 = 2\pi$).

Therefore, the 3rd harmonic is a **common-mode** signal:
$$V_{ab,3}(\theta) = V_{an,3}(\theta) - V_{bn,3}(\theta) = V_{3}\sin(3\theta) - V_{3}\sin(3\theta) = 0$$

Similarly, all triplen harmonics (3rd, 9th, 15th, …) cancel in the line-to-line voltage.

### 3.4 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Mode | $m_a$ Range | $V_{ab,1,\text{peak}}$ Formula | DC Bus Utilization | Harmonic Quality |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Linear SPWM | $0 \le m_a \le 1$ | $\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} m_a V_{dc}$ | Up to 86.6% | Excellent (no low-order) |
| Linear THI-PWM | $0 \le m_a \le 2/\sqrt{3}$ | $\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} m_a V_{dc}$ | Up to 100% | Excellent (no low-order) |
| Overmodulation I | $1 < m_a \le 1.1546$ | Non-linear, rises to $V_{dc}$ | 86.6% → 100% | Degrading |
| Overmodulation II | $1.1546 < m_a < 1.273$ | Non-linear, rises to $1.1026 V_{dc}$ | 100% → 110.26% | Poor |
| Square Wave | $m_a \equiv 1.273$ | $\dfrac{2\sqrt{3}}{\pi} V_{dc}$ | 110.26% | Worst |

---

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] Define modulation index and frequency modulation ratio for a three-phase PWM inverter.**

**Model Answer:**
The **modulation index** $m_a$ is the ratio of the peak amplitude of the reference (modulating) signal $A_r$ to the peak amplitude of the triangular carrier $A_c$:
$$m_a = \frac{A_r}{A_c}$$
It controls the fundamental magnitude of the inverter output voltage.

The **frequency modulation ratio** $m_f$ is the ratio of the carrier frequency $f_c$ to the fundamental (modulating) frequency $f_m$:
$$m_f = \frac{f_c}{f_m}$$
It determines the harmonic spectrum location of the PWM output and must typically be an odd multiple of 3 (e.g., 9, 15, 21) for three-phase inverters to ensure elimination of certain carrier harmonics in the line-to-line voltage.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024] State the advantages and disadvantages of operating a three-phase VSI in the square-wave mode.**

**Model Answer:**

**Advantages:**
1. The fundamental output voltage is at its **maximum** value: $V_{ab,1,\text{peak}} = \dfrac{2\sqrt{3}}{\pi} V_{dc} \approx 1.1026\, V_{dc}$, giving full DC-bus utilization.
2. **Switching losses are minimal** because each device switches only once per fundamental cycle.
3. Simple gate-driver logic; no PWM comparator required.

**Disadvantages:**
1. The output contains **large low-order harmonics** (5th at 20%, 7th at 14.3%, 11th at 9.1%, etc.).
2. **No control over the fundamental magnitude** — fixed at the square-wave value.
3. Not suitable for high-performance motor drives without large harmonic filters.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

**Question A. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the fundamental component of the phase and line-to-line output voltage of a three-phase sinusoidal PWM VSI operating in the linear modulation region.

**(b)** A three-phase VSI has a DC bus of $V_{dc} = 400$ V. It operates in linear SPWM with $m_a = 0.8$ and $m_f = 21$. Calculate the fundamental RMS line-to-line voltage and the dominant harmonic cluster frequency. State the maximum attainable $V_{ab,1,\text{rms}}$ in linear mode.

---

**Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks:**

Refer to **Section 3.1 (Derivation 1)**. The key steps are:

1. Express the duty ratio of the upper switch in phase A:
$$d(\theta) = \frac{1 + m_a \sin\theta}{2}$$
2. Compute the locally averaged pole voltage:
$$\overline{V_{A0}}(\theta) = \frac{V_{dc}}{2}\left[1 + m_a \sin\theta\right]$$
3. Compute the neutral offset (sum of three balanced phase pole averages divided by 3):
$$\overline{V_{n0}} = \frac{V_{dc}}{2}$$
4. Subtract to get the fundamental phase voltage:
$$\overline{V_{an}}(\theta) = \frac{m_a V_{dc}}{2}\sin(\omega t)$$
5. Therefore:
$$\boxed{V_{an,1,\text{peak}} = \frac{m_a V_{dc}}{2}, \qquad V_{ab,1,\text{peak}} = \frac{\sqrt{3}\, m_a V_{dc}}{2}}$$

This is valid for $0 \le m_a \le 1$. At $m_a = 1$: $V_{ab,1,\text{peak}} = 0.866\, V_{dc}$ and $V_{ab,1,\text{rms}} = 0.6124\, V_{dc}$.

---

**Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks:**

Given: $V_{dc} = 400$ V, $m_a = 0.8$, $m_f = 21$.

**Step 1 — Fundamental RMS line voltage:**
$$V_{ab,1,\text{rms}} = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{\sqrt{2}} \cdot \frac{m_a V_{dc}}{2} = \frac{1.2247 \times 0.8 \times 400}{2} = \frac{391.9}{2} = 195.96 \text{ V}$$

Alternatively using the compact formula:
$$V_{ab,1,\text{rms}} = 0.6124 \times 0.8 \times 400 = 195.97 \text{ V}$$

**Step 2 — Dominant harmonic cluster:**
The first carrier sideband cluster in $V_{ab}$ is centered at $m_f = 21$ (since carrier harmonics cancel as triplen sets, and the line-to-line voltage has clusters around $\pm m_f$ from the fundamental). If $f_1 = 50$ Hz, the dominant cluster is at:
$$f_{h} = (2k+1)m_f \pm n \text{ times } f_1 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{dominant at } m_f f_1 = 21 \times 50 = 1050 \text{ Hz}$$
More precisely the principal sideband pair is at $f_c \pm 2f_1 = 1050 \pm 100$ Hz, i.e., **950 Hz and 1150 Hz**.

**Step 3 — Maximum linear SPWM RMS:**
$$V_{ab,1,\text{rms},\max} = 0.6124 \times 1.0 \times 400 = 244.95 \text{ V}$$

---

**Question B. [KTU University Exam — June 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the principle of **third harmonic injection PWM** for a three-phase VSI. Derive the expression for the maximum modulation index in linear operation and show that the maximum line-voltage fundamental is increased by a factor of $2/\sqrt{3}$ over plain SPWM.

**(b)** A three-phase VSI uses third harmonic injection PWM with $V_{dc} = 600$ V. Find the maximum $m_a$ for linear operation, the corresponding fundamental peak line voltage, and compare this value with the maximum fundamental obtainable in the square-wave mode of operation.

---

**Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks:**

Refer to **Section 3.3 (Derivation 3)**. Key steps:

1. Modified reference for phase A:
$$v_{a,\text{ref}}(\theta) = m_a \left[\sin\theta + \tfrac{1}{6}\sin(3\theta)\right]$$
2. Differentiate and find the maximum — solve $\cos\theta + \tfrac{1}{2}\cos(3\theta) = 0$, giving $\theta = 60°$.
3. Evaluate the bracket at $\theta = 60°$: $\sin 60° + 0 = \sqrt{3}/2$.
4. Constrain $m_a \cdot \sqrt{3}/2 \le 1$ → $\boxed{m_{a,\max} = 2/\sqrt{3} \approx 1.1546}$.
5. The 3rd harmonic is a **common-mode** signal (it appears identically in all three phases because $3 \times 120° = 360°$), so it cancels in the line-to-line voltage.
6. At $m_a = 2/\sqrt{3}$: $V_{an,1,\text{peak}} = V_{dc}/\sqrt{3}$, hence $V_{ab,1,\text{peak}} = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_{dc}/\sqrt{3} = V_{dc}$.
7. Gain over plain SPWM (where max is $\sqrt{3}V_{dc}/2$): $V_{dc} / (\sqrt{3}V_{dc}/2) = 2/\sqrt{3} \approx 1.1546$ — a **15.47% increase** in linear mode utilization.

---

**Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks:**

Given: $V_{dc} = 600$ V, THI-PWM.

**Step 1 — Maximum $m_a$ in linear region:**
$$m_{a,\max} = \frac{2}{\sqrt{3}} \approx 1.1546$$

**Step 2 — Maximum fundamental peak line voltage (THI-PWM, linear):**
$$V_{ab,1,\text{peak},\max} = V_{dc} = 600 \text{ V}$$
$$V_{ab,1,\text{rms},\max} = \frac{V_{dc}}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{600}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 424.26 \text{ V}$$

**Step 3 — Square-wave maximum fundamental peak:**
$$V_{ab,1,\text{peak},\text{sq}} = \frac{2\sqrt{3}\, V_{dc}}{\pi} = \frac{2\sqrt{3} \times 600}{\pi} = \frac{2078.46}{\pi} \approx 661.56 \text{ V}$$
$$V_{ab,1,\text{rms},\text{sq}} = \frac{\sqrt{6}}{\pi} V_{dc} = \frac{2.4495 \times 600}{\pi} \approx 467.74 \text{ V}$$

**Step 4 — Comparison:**
$$\frac{V_{ab,1,\text{peak},\text{sq}}}{V_{ab,1,\text{peak},\text{THI},\max}} = \frac{661.56}{600} = 1.1026 = \frac{4}{\pi} \cdot \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$$

So square-wave mode gives an additional **10.26%** fundamental over the THI-PWM linear limit, at the cost of substantial low-order harmonics.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing pole voltage with phase voltage** — Phase voltage is the pole voltage **minus the neutral offset** $V_{n0}$. Writing $V_{an,1,\text{peak}} = m_a V_{dc}$ (instead of $m_a V_{dc}/2$) is a frequent error that loses **3–4 marks**.
> 2. **Forgetting the $\sqrt{3}$ factor** when going from phase to line voltage. Phase fundamental is $m_a V_{dc}/2$; line fundamental is **$\sqrt{3}$ times larger** = $\sqrt{3} m_a V_{dc}/2$.
> 3. **Misapplying the square-wave formula** — Writing $V_{ab,1,\text{peak}} = 4 V_{dc}/\pi$ is the formula for a *single-phase full-bridge square wave*, not a three-phase VSI line voltage. The correct three-phase value is $\mathbf{2\sqrt{3}\, V_{dc}/\pi}$.
> 4. **In third harmonic injection**, students often claim the third harmonic "disappears" — it does not disappear from the phase voltage; it cancels in the **line voltage** because it is a common-mode signal. This distinction is critical.
> 5. **Overmodulation analysis** — Students describe the overmodulation region as having a *linear* relationship with $m_a$. It is **highly non-linear** in this region; only the boundary values are easily computed.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Three-phase VSI** uses six switches in three legs; the line-to-line voltage is the useful output, while phase voltages include a common-mode offset.
- **Modulation index** $m_a = A_r/A_c$ controls fundamental magnitude; **frequency modulation ratio** $m_f = f_c/f_m$ should be an odd multiple of 3 to cancel carrier harmonics in $V_{ab}$.
- **Linear SPWM** ($0 \le m_a \le 1$): Output fundamental is **linearly proportional** to $m_a$ with $V_{ab,1,\text{peak}} = \sqrt{3} m_a V_{dc}/2$; max value is $0.866 V_{dc}$.
- **Overmodulation** ($1 < m_a < 4/\pi \approx 1.273$): Pulses saturate near the reference peaks; **non-linear** relationship; low-order harmonics (5th, 7th) reappear.
- **Square-wave mode** ($m_a = 4/\pi$): Maximum fundamental $V_{ab,1,\text{peak}} = 2\sqrt{3} V_{dc}/\pi \approx 1.1026 V_{dc}$, but heavy harmonic distortion.
- **Third Harmonic Injection (THI-PWM)**: Adds $m_a \sin(3\omega t)/6$ to references; extends linear range to $m_{a,\max} = 2/\sqrt{3} \approx 1.1546$, giving a **15.47% increase** in fundamental line voltage.
- The 3rd harmonic (and all triplen harmonics) are **common-mode** and cancel in the line-to-line voltage — this is the key engineering insight that makes THI-PWM practical.
- **DC-bus utilization summary**: Plain SPWM = 86.6% → THI-PWM linear = 100% → Square wave = 110.26%.
- **Fourier series** of a square wave: $V(t) = \dfrac{4A}{\pi}\sum \dfrac{\sin(n\omega t)}{n}$ for odd $n$, where $A$ is the amplitude — this is the foundation for all square-wave derivations.
- For three-phase, $V_{ab,1} = \sqrt{3}\, V_{an,1} \angle 30°$ — the line voltage **leads** the phase voltage by 30° and is $\sqrt{3}$ times larger in magnitude.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Single Phase IGBT Based Current Source Inverter (CSI): Comparison between VSI and CSI
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOT502 - POWER ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Single Phase IGBT Based Current Source Inverter (CSI): Comparison between VSI and CSI

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition
A **Current Source Inverter (CSI)** is a type of power electronic inverter in which the DC input appears as a **stiff (ideal) current source** to the load side, meaning the magnitude of the input current remains essentially constant irrespective of the load. It converts a DC current source into an AC current waveform of controllable frequency and amplitude. In its single-phase, **IGBT-based** form, unidirectional-controlled IGBT switches are paired with reverse-blocking series diodes to provide the bidirectional voltage-blocking and current-handling capability demanded by the AC load.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The "Faucet with a Constant Flow"**
> Imagine a kitchen faucet connected to a high-pressure water tank. No matter how you open or close the tap, the *flow rate* coming out is almost the same because the tank pressure is enormous (a *stiff current source*). A CSI works on the same idea on the electrical side: a large DC inductor (L) in series with the DC source makes the input current nearly ripple-free, like a *current "pump"*. The inverter switches then simply "route" this constant DC current to the load in alternating directions, producing an AC current waveform. The output **voltage** is not controlled directly — it is *determined by the load* (the load "decides" what voltage it needs, given the current pushed into it).

### 1.3 Key Physical Metrics & Terminology
- **Stiff DC Current Source**: A current that is independent of the load voltage (ideally infinite source impedance).
- **Large DC Link Inductor (L)**: Provides the stiff current source behavior; $L$ is chosen such that $L \gg R/\omega$.
- **Reverse-Blocking Diodes**: Series-connected with each IGBT to block reverse voltage, since standard IGBTs cannot block reverse voltage.
- **Commutation Capacitor (C)**: Stores the energy required to force-commutate the thyristor/IGBT devices in classical CSI; in modern IGBT-based CSI, commutation is achieved via the link inductor and capacitor network.
- **Load Commutation**: A technique where the load's own leading power-factor provides natural commutation of devices.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (2024 Scheme):**
> The module emphasises the *operational differences* between VSI and CSI, the role of the **DC link inductor**, the **IGBT + series diode** switch pair, and the **square-wave / PWM current output** waveforms. A comparative table is *routinely* asked in university examinations.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principle of a Single-Phase IGBT-Based CSI
A single-phase full-bridge CSI consists of four controlled switches (T1, T2, T3, T4), each realised as an **IGBT in series with a reverse-blocking fast-recovery diode** (D1, D2, D3, D4). The DC side is fed from a current source realised by a DC voltage source $V_s$ in series with a large smoothing inductor $L_s$ — the inductor maintains an almost ripple-free DC current $I_s$.

**Switching Sequence (Square-Wave Operation):**
1. **Mode 1 (0 → T/2):** Switches T1 and T2 are ON. The constant current $I_s$ is forced through the load in the positive direction (say, left to right). The load current $i_o = +I_s$.
2. **Mode 2 (T/2 → T):** Switches T3 and T4 are ON. The same DC current $I_s$ is now routed through the load in the negative direction. The load current $i_o = -I_s$.
3. The output current is therefore a quasi-square wave of amplitude $I_s$ and frequency $f_o = 1/T$.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic: Why an IGBT Needs a Series Diode in a CSI

| Step | Logic | Engineering Reasoning |
|------|-------|-----------------------|
| 1 | A CSI load (e.g., induction motor) can be **inductive**, meaning voltage and current are out of phase. | The switches must therefore **block reverse voltage** during part of the cycle. |
| 2 | A standard IGBT has an **anti-parallel (freewheeling) diode** internally, but it *cannot block reverse voltage*. | The internal diode would short the reverse voltage, causing a shoot-through fault. |
| 3 | A **reverse-blocking fast-recovery diode** is added in **series** with each IGBT. | This series combination can block voltage in **both polarities** while the IGBT controls the switching. |
| 4 | The IGBT-diode pair is now a **bidirectional voltage-blocking, unidirectional current-carrying** element — exactly what a CSI switch needs. | This is the canonical "IGBT + series diode" building block of modern IGBT-based CSIs. |

### 2.3 Output Waveform Analysis
For a **square-wave CSI** feeding a **purely resistive load R**:

$$i_o(t) = \begin{cases} +I_s, & 0 \le t < T/2 \\ -I_s, & T/2 \le t < T \end{cases}$$

The Fourier series of this square wave is:

$$i_o(t) = \frac{4 I_s}{\pi} \sum_{n=1,3,5,\ldots}^{\infty} \frac{1}{n} \sin(n \omega t)$$

The **fundamental RMS value** of the output current is:

$$I_{o1,\text{rms}} = \frac{4 I_s}{\pi \sqrt{2}} = \frac{2\sqrt{2}}{\pi} I_s \approx 0.90\, I_s$$

> [!TIP]
> The output *voltage* of a CSI is **not prescribed**; it depends on the load impedance and the load's own back-EMF (e.g., motor EMF). For an R-L load with a back-EMF $E$, the peak load voltage can easily exceed the DC source voltage, and can even become **negative** during commutation transients.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity / Parameter | Formula | Boundary / Unit | Engineering Meaning |
|---|----------------------|---------|-----------------|---------------------|
| 1 | Fundamental RMS output current (square wave) | $I_{o1,rms} = \frac{2\sqrt{2}}{\pi} I_s$ | $\approx 0.9\,I_s$ | Useful AC current delivered to load |
| 2 | RMS output current (square wave) | $I_{o,rms} = I_s$ | Equal to DC link | The DC current simply alternates in sign |
| 3 | Output power into resistive load | $P_o = I_s^2 \, R$ | Watts | Heating power in R |
| 4 | DC link inductor sizing | $L_s \gg \dfrac{R_{load}}{\omega_o}$ | Henry | Ensures stiff current source |
| 5 | Peak DC link current | $I_s = \dfrac{V_s}{R_{load}+R_s}$ (steady) | Ampere | Set by source and total series resistance |
| 6 | Switching frequency for PWM CSI | $f_{sw}$ = carrier frequency | Hz | Determines output current ripple and THD |
| 7 | Source impedance (ideal CSI) | $Z_{source} \rightarrow \infty$ | Theoretical | Open-circuit voltage source on the DC side |
| 8 | Commutation capacitor energy | $E_c = \tfrac{1}{2} C V_c^2$ | Joules | Energy stored for forced commutation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Trick:** In a square-wave CSI, the output current has a *fixed* peak ($I_s$) — it cannot be varied by PWM in the classical sense. *Amplitude* control of the output current is achieved by **varying the DC link current $I_s$** itself (e.g., by a chopper between the DC source and the DC link inductor).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Fourier Series of the Square-Wave Output Current of a CSI

Let the output current waveform of an ideal square-wave CSI be defined over one period $T$ as:

$$i_o(t) = \begin{cases} +I_s, & 0 \le t < T/2 \\ -I_s, & T/2 \le t < T \end{cases}$$

Since the waveform is an **odd function**, only sine terms appear in the Fourier series, and the Fourier coefficients reduce to:

$$b_n = \frac{2}{T} \int_{0}^{T} i_o(t) \sin(n \omega t)\, dt, \quad \omega = \frac{2\pi}{T}$$

**Step 1 — Split the integral into the two half-periods:**

$$b_n = \frac{2}{T} \left[ \int_{0}^{T/2} (+I_s) \sin(n \omega t)\, dt + \int_{T/2}^{T} (-I_s) \sin(n \omega t)\, dt \right]$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate the first integral:**

$$I_1 = \int_{0}^{T/2} \sin(n \omega t)\, dt = \left[ -\frac{\cos(n \omega t)}{n \omega} \right]_{0}^{T/2}$$

Substituting the limits $t = T/2 \Rightarrow \omega t = \pi$ and $t = 0$:

$$I_1 = -\frac{\cos(n\pi)}{n \omega} + \frac{\cos(0)}{n \omega} = \frac{1 - \cos(n\pi)}{n \omega}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the second integral:**

$$I_2 = \int_{T/2}^{T} \sin(n \omega t)\, dt = \left[ -\frac{\cos(n \omega t)}{n \omega} \right]_{T/2}^{T}$$

Substituting the limits $t = T \Rightarrow \omega t = 2\pi$ and $t = T/2 \Rightarrow \omega t = \pi$:

$$I_2 = -\frac{\cos(2 n \pi)}{n \omega} + \frac{\cos(n \pi)}{n \omega} = \frac{\cos(n \pi) - 1}{n \omega}$$

**Step 4 — Combine the two integrals:**

$$b_n = \frac{2 I_s}{T} \left[ I_1 - I_2 \right] = \frac{2 I_s}{T} \left[ \frac{1 - \cos(n\pi)}{n \omega} - \frac{\cos(n\pi) - 1}{n \omega} \right]$$

$$b_n = \frac{2 I_s}{T} \left[ \frac{2(1 - \cos(n\pi))}{n \omega} \right] = \frac{4 I_s}{n \omega T} \left[ 1 - \cos(n\pi) \right]$$

**Step 5 — Use $\omega T = 2\pi$ and note that $\cos(n\pi) = (-1)^n$:**

$$b_n = \frac{4 I_s}{n \cdot 2\pi} \left[ 1 - (-1)^n \right] = \frac{2 I_s}{n \pi} \left[ 1 - (-1)^n \right]$$

**Step 6 — Apply the parity filter:**
- For $n$ **even**: $1 - (-1)^n = 0 \Rightarrow b_n = 0$. (No even harmonics.)
- For $n$ **odd**: $1 - (-1)^n = 2 \Rightarrow b_n = \dfrac{4 I_s}{n \pi}$.

**Step 7 — Assemble the Fourier series:**

$$i_o(t) = \sum_{n=1,3,5,\ldots}^{\infty} \frac{4 I_s}{n \pi} \sin(n \omega t)$$

**Step 8 — Extract the fundamental RMS value:**

$$I_{o1,\text{peak}} = \frac{4 I_s}{\pi} \Rightarrow I_{o1,\text{rms}} = \frac{4 I_s}{\pi \sqrt{2}} = \frac{2\sqrt{2}}{\pi} I_s \approx 0.9\, I_s$$

This confirms the high-yield result used in problem solving.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Switching State and Conduction Path

| Interval | T1 | T2 | T3 | T4 | Load Current $i_o$ | Freewheeling Diode? |
|----------|----|----|----|----|---------------------|---------------------|
| $0 \to T/2$ | ON | ON | OFF | OFF | $+I_s$ | None |
| $T/2 \to T$ | OFF | OFF | ON | ON | $-I_s$ | None |
| Commutation (short overlap) | ON→OFF | ON→OFF | OFF→ON | OFF→ON | Transient | All four briefly share current |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse a CSI with a VSI and draw a *voltage* square wave at the output. The correct mental picture is that the **current** is the *commanded* variable and the **voltage** is *load-dependent*.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Single-Phase IGBT-Based Current Source Inverter

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    DCsrc[DC Voltage Source Vs] --> Ls[DC Link Inductor Ls]
    Ls -->|Stiff DC Current Is| InvBridge{H-Bridge CSI}
    InvBridge --> T1[IGBT T1]
    InvBridge --> T2[IGBT T2]
    InvBridge --> T3[IGBT T3]
    InvBridge --> T4[IGBT T4]
    T1 --- D1[Series Diode D1]
    T2 --- D2[Series Diode D2]
    T3 --- D3[Series Diode D3]
    T4 --- D4[Series Diode D4]
    InvBridge -->|Bidirectional AC current io| Load[RL Load with back EMF E]
    Load --> GroundA((Return))
    Ls --> GroundB((Return))

    CommuteCap[Commutation Capacitor C] -.Coupled to.-> InvBridge

    style DCsrc fill:#ffe4b5,stroke:#333
    style Ls fill:#e6e6fa,stroke:#333
    style InvBridge fill:#ffdab9,stroke:#333
    style Load fill:#c1ffc1,stroke:#333
    style CommuteCap fill:#fffacd,stroke:#333
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — Switching Action Over One Cycle

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Ctrl as Gate Driver
    participant T1 as IGBT T1
    participant T2 as IGBT T2
    participant T3 as IGBT T3
    participant T4 as IGBT T4
    participant Load as RL Load

    Note over Ctrl,Load: Interval 0 to T by 2
    Ctrl->>T1: GATE ON
    Ctrl->>T2: GATE ON
    Ctrl->>T3: GATE OFF
    Ctrl->>T4: GATE OFF
    T1-->>Load: Conduct +Is
    T2-->>Load: Conduct +Is
    Load-->>Load: io = +Is (positive half cycle)

    Note over Ctrl,Load: Interval T by 2 to T
    Ctrl->>T1: GATE OFF
    Ctrl->>T2: GATE OFF
    Ctrl->>T3: GATE ON
    Ctrl->>T4: GATE ON
    T3-->>Load: Conduct +Is
    T4-->>Load: Conduct +Is
    Load-->>Load: io = -Is (negative half cycle)
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Comparison Tree — VSI vs CSI

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Root[Inverter Family] --> VSI[Voltage Source Inverter VSI]
    Root --> CSI[Current Source Inverter CSI]

    VSI --> VSI1[DC side: Stiff voltage source large C]
    VSI --> VSI2[Output quantity: Voltage]
    VSI --> VSI3[Switch: IGBT with antiparallel diode]
    VSI --> VSI4[Short circuit fault possible shoot through]
    VSI --> VSI5[Used in UPS solar drives]

    CSI --> CSI1[DC side: Stiff current source large L]
    CSI --> CSI2[Output quantity: Current]
    CSI --> CSI3[Switch: IGBT with series diode]
    CSI --> CSI4[Open circuit fault possible inductor overvoltage]
    CSI --> CSI5[Used in high power motor drives induction heating]

    style VSI fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
    style CSI fill:#ffd6cc,stroke:#663300
    style Root fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#333
```

### 4.4 Operating-Waveform Sketch (Textual)

```
  i_o(t)                                          
    +Is ┤■■■■■■■■■■■■■                             
        │            ■                             
     0  ┼────────────■──────────── t               
        │             ■■■■■■■■■■■■■■              
    -Is ┤                          ■              
        ├──────┬──────┬──────┬──────┬──►           
        0     T/4    T/2    3T/4    T             

  v_o(t) for RL load                             
   +Vpk ┤      ▲ ▲                                
        │     ╱   ╲    ▲ ▲                        
     0  ┼────╱─────╲──╱───╲────────────────       
        │   ╱       ╲╱     ╲   ▲ ▲               
   -Vpk ┤  ▼ ▼              ╲ ╱                  
        ├──────┬──────┬──────┬──────┬──►           
        0     T/4    T/2    3T/4    T             
```

> [!NOTE]
> In the **CSI**, the **current** is a clean square wave; the **voltage** is a *distorted* exponential that depends on the L/R time constant of the load. This is the *opposite* of a VSI, where the voltage is clean and the current is load-dependent.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*What is a Current Source Inverter (CSI)? Why is a large inductor used in its DC link?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A Current Source Inverter (CSI) is an inverter whose DC input is a stiff (constant) current source. The constant current is normally obtained by connecting a large inductor $L_s$ in series with a DC voltage source. The inductor stores energy and opposes any sudden change in current, making $I_s$ essentially independent of the load. Thus, the inverter "sees" a constant current source at its DC terminals and the output current becomes a square wave of amplitude $I_s$.

**Allocation:** Definition 1M, Role of inductor 1M, Resulting constant $I_s$ 1M.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Why is a series diode connected with each IGBT in a single-phase CSI?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
In a CSI, the load can be inductive and the switches must block **reverse voltage** as well as forward voltage. A standard IGBT cannot block reverse voltage because of its internal anti-parallel diode. Therefore, a **reverse-blocking fast-recovery diode is connected in series** with each IGBT. This series combination becomes a bidirectional voltage-blocking, unidirectional current-carrying switch — exactly the requirement of a CSI.

**Allocation:** Reverse-voltage requirement 1M, IGBT limitation 1M, Series diode solution 1M.

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A — [KTU University Exam – Model Question as per 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the **operating principle of a single-phase IGBT-based Current Source Inverter**. Draw the output current and output voltage waveforms for an R-L load. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Circuit Diagram (2 Marks):** Draw the single-phase H-bridge with four IGBTs (T1–T4) in series with four diodes (D1–D4) and a DC source $V_s$ feeding the bridge through a large inductor $L_s$. Load is R-L in series with a possible back-EMF $E$.

2. **Operating Modes (3 Marks):**
   - **Mode 1 (0 to T/2):** T1 and T2 ON. DC link current $I_s$ flows through the load from terminal A to B. Load current $i_o = +I_s$.
   - **Mode 2 (T/2 to T):** T3 and T4 ON. DC link current $I_s$ flows through the load from B to A. Load current $i_o = -I_s$.
   - **Commutation:** Achieved by the commutation capacitor (classical) or by overlap (PWM) — the inductor $L_s$ prevents any sudden change in $I_s$.

3. **Waveforms (2 Marks):** $i_o$ is a square wave of amplitude $I_s$. $v_o$ for an R-L load is a *distorted exponential* whose positive and negative peaks depend on the load time constant $\tau = L/R$ and the back-EMF.

**(b)** Derive the **Fourier series expression for the output current** of a square-wave CSI. Hence find the **fundamental RMS value** of the output current in terms of $I_s$. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The square wave of amplitude $I_s$ is an odd function; hence the Fourier series contains only sine terms. Carrying out the standard half-wave symmetric Fourier analysis (as shown in Section 3.1) yields:

$$i_o(t) = \frac{4 I_s}{\pi} \sum_{n=1,3,5,\ldots}^{\infty} \frac{\sin(n \omega t)}{n}$$

**Step 1 (2 Marks):** Set up the integral $b_n = \frac{2}{T}\int_0^{T/2} I_s \sin(n\omega t)dt$ and split it appropriately.

**Step 2 (3 Marks):** Evaluate the integral to obtain $b_n = \frac{4 I_s}{n \pi}$ for $n$ odd, and $b_n = 0$ for $n$ even.

**Step 3 (2 Marks):** Identify the fundamental peak as $\frac{4 I_s}{\pi}$, then divide by $\sqrt{2}$:

$$I_{o1,\text{rms}} = \frac{4 I_s}{\pi \sqrt{2}} = \frac{2\sqrt{2}}{\pi} I_s \approx 0.9\, I_s$$

---

#### **Question B — [KTU University Exam – Model Question as per 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Compare the **Voltage Source Inverter (VSI)** and the **Current Source Inverter (CSI)** under the headings: (i) DC-side storage element, (ii) Output quantity controlled, (iii) Switch used, (iv) Nature of fault, (v) Typical applications. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Sl. No. | Feature | VSI | CSI |
|---------|---------|-----|-----|
| 1 | DC-side element | Large **capacitor** $C$ (stiff voltage) | Large **inductor** $L_s$ (stiff current) |
| 2 | Controlled output quantity | Output **voltage** | Output **current** |
| 3 | Switch used | IGBT with **anti-parallel diode** | IGBT with **series reverse-blocking diode** |
| 4 | Nature of dangerous fault | **Short circuit** (shoot-through) | **Open circuit** (inductor over-voltage) |
| 5 | Typical applications | UPS, solar PV inverters, low/medium-power drives | High-power AC motor drives, induction heating, active power filters |
| 6 | Output waveform (ideal) | Square-wave / PWM **voltage** | Square-wave / PWM **current** |
| 7 | Load voltage | Independent of load (set by inverter) | Depends on load (set by load and back-EMF) |

**Allocation:** 1 Mark per feature row × 7 rows = 7 Marks.

**(b)** A single-phase square-wave CSI supplies a DC link current of $I_s = 50\,\text{A}$ to a purely resistive load of $R = 10\,\Omega$. Calculate the **(i) fundamental RMS output current**, **(ii) RMS output current**, **(iii) output power dissipated in the load**, and **(iv) DC source voltage $V_s$** (assuming $R_s \to 0$). **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Fundamental RMS output current:**

$$I_{o1,rms} = \frac{2\sqrt{2}}{\pi} \cdot I_s = \frac{2 \times 1.4142}{3.1416} \times 50 = 0.9003 \times 50 = 45.0\,\text{A}$$

**(ii) RMS output current:**

For a symmetric square wave, the RMS value equals its peak:

$$I_{o,rms} = I_s = 50\,\text{A}$$

**(iii) Output power in the load:**

$$P_o = I_{o,rms}^2 \cdot R = (50)^2 \times 10 = 25{,}000\,\text{W} = 25\,\text{kW}$$

**(iv) DC source voltage (assuming the inductor is ideal and $R_s \to 0$):**
The DC source only has to overcome the resistive drop in the DC loop. With $R_s \to 0$:

$$V_s \approx I_s \cdot R_s \to 0\,\text{V}$$

In *practice*, the DC source voltage is set by the **average power balance** between DC and AC sides. Equating AC power to DC power:

$$V_s \cdot I_s = P_o = 25{,}000\,\text{W} \Rightarrow V_s = \frac{25{,}000}{50} = 500\,\text{V}$$

**Allocation:** (i) 2M, (ii) 1M, (iii) 2M, (iv) 2M.

---

### 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where students lose marks in VSI vs CSI questions:**
> 1. **Confusing the controlled variable** — students say "VSI controls current" or "CSI controls voltage". The correct statement is: VSI controls *voltage*, CSI controls *current*. (–1 Mark)
> 2. **Wrong switch symbol** — drawing an IGBT with anti-parallel diode in a CSI circuit. The CSI requires an IGBT with a **series** reverse-blocking diode. (–1 to –2 Marks)
> 3. **Ignoring the DC link inductor** — drawing a CSI without $L_s$ on the DC side. The inductor is the *defining* element of a CSI. (–1 Mark)
> 4. **Confusing the fault mode** — students frequently write "CSI is short-circuit fault prone". The truth is the opposite: an *open circuit* on the AC side causes the inductor's stored energy to produce a **dangerous over-voltage**, so CSI is *open-circuit fault prone*. (–1 Mark)
> 5. **Fourier-series sign errors** — forgetting that the square wave is *odd* and including cosine terms. (–1 Mark)

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- A **CSI** is defined by a *stiff current* at the DC terminals, achieved with a **large series inductor** $L_s$.
- The *output current* is the controlled quantity — it is a **square wave** of amplitude $I_s$ in classical operation.
- The *output voltage* is **load-dependent** and not controlled directly by the inverter.
- The **IGBT + series reverse-blocking diode** is the canonical switch pair for modern IGBT-based CSIs.
- A CSI is **open-circuit-fault prone** (inductor over-voltage), while a VSI is **short-circuit-fault prone** (shoot-through).
- A CSI is preferred for **high-power, high-current applications** like AC motor drives and induction heating; a VSI dominates in **UPS, solar PV, and low-power drives**.
- **Amplitude control** in a CSI is achieved by varying $I_s$ (e.g., using a DC chopper), *not* by PWM of the bridge.
- **Fourier fundamental** of the CSI square wave: $I_{o1,rms} = \dfrac{2\sqrt{2}}{\pi} I_s \approx 0.9\, I_s$.
- The DC source voltage of a CSI equals the **average DC voltage** required to balance the AC-side power: $V_s I_s = P_{AC}$.
- A VSI uses an IGBT with an **anti-parallel diode**; a CSI uses an IGBT with a **series reverse-blocking diode** — *opposite* switch constructions, *opposite* output quantities.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Need for Improved Utility Interface: Generation of Current Harmonics, Power Factor, Harmonics and IEEE 519 Standard, Active Shaping of the Input Line Current
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOT502 - POWER ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Need for Improved Utility Interface: Generation of Current Harmonics, Power Factor, Harmonics and IEEE 519 Standard, Active Shaping of the Input Line Current

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The "Need for an Improved Utility Interface"

### Formal KTU Definition
An **improved utility interface** refers to the use of power-electronic front-end converter stages (active or passive) that ensure the **load draws a sinusoidal current in phase with the supply voltage**, while simultaneously limiting the current harmonics injected into the grid below the levels prescribed by standards such as **IEEE 519** and **IEC 61000-3-2**.

### Conceptual Analogy
Imagine the AC mains as a clean, pressurized water pipe carrying smooth sinusoidal "flow" (current) that the city utility (grid) expects every customer to draw. A conventional **diode/thyristor rectifier** is like a household that, instead of drawing water smoothly, gulps it in violent 120°-wide gulps (square-shaped pulses). This violent gulping:

1. **Disturbs other customers** (other loads connected to the same transformer) — neighbors see ripples in their lights and heating elements.
2. **Stresses the pipes** (overheating of neutral conductors, transformers, and cables due to harmonic currents).
3. **Fails to pay for what it uses correctly** — the utility meter (analog) cannot distinguish real power from reactive/harmonic power, so the customer effectively gets "free" extra current.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The "utility interface" is the point of common coupling (PCC) between the power-electronic load and the public grid. All pollution (harmonics) and inefficiency (poor PF) at this interface must be controlled.

---

## 1.2 Generation of Current Harmonics

### Formal Definition
**Current harmonics** are sinusoidal components of the load current whose frequencies are integer multiples of the fundamental supply frequency. Mathematically, for a non-sinusoidal periodic current $i(t)$:

$$i(t) = I_0 + \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} I_n \sin(n\omega t + \phi_n)$$

where $I_n$ is the amplitude of the $n^{\text{th}}$ harmonic.

### Conceptual Analogy
A guitar string vibrating at 110 Hz (fundamental "A") will never produce a perfectly pure tone — small vibrations at 220 Hz, 330 Hz, 440 Hz, etc., are superimposed. These are "harmonics." Similarly, when a power-electronic switch chops the AC mains in a non-linear fashion, it injects these unwanted extra frequencies (harmonics) into the grid.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why do diode rectifiers generate harmonics?**
> Because the diode bridge acts as a *non-linear* load — its current waveform is **not** proportional to the instantaneous applied voltage. The silicon junction has a "dead-zone" (forward-voltage drop) and a "switching-zone" (when it begins to conduct), creating the characteristic quasi-square / peaked current waveform rich in harmonics.

---

## 1.3 Power Factor in Distorted (Non-Sinusoidal) Systems

### Formal Definition
The **true power factor** of a non-linear load is the ratio of real (average) power $P$ to apparent power $S$:

$$\text{PF} = \frac{P}{S} = \frac{V_{\text{rms}} I_{1,\text{rms}} \cos \phi_1}{V_{\text{rms}} I_{\text{rms}}} = \underbrace{\frac{I_{1,\text{rms}}}{I_{\text{rms}}}}_{k_d \, \text{(Distortion Factor)}} \cdot \underbrace{\cos \phi_1}_{k_p \, \text{(Displacement Factor)}}$$

### Conceptual Analogy
Power factor is like the "**efficiency of fuel use**" in a vehicle. Displacement factor (cos φ) tells you how well-timed the engine strokes are; distortion factor tells you how clean the fuel spray is. A car (rectifier) with terrible timing **and** dirty spray wastes fuel — you pay for "apparent fuel" but only "real fuel" drives the wheels.

---

## 1.4 IEEE 519 Standard — "The Rulebook for Clean Power"

### Formal Definition
**IEEE Std 519-2014** is the benchmark recommended practice titled *"Recommended Practice and Requirements for Harmonic Control in Electric Power Systems."* It sets **voltage distortion limits at the Point of Common Coupling (PCC)** and **current distortion limits at the customer side**, ensuring the shared grid is not contaminated by any one customer.

### Conceptual Analogy
IEEE 519 is the **"noise ordinance"** of the electrical grid. Just as a residential area has decibel limits for music after 10 PM, IEEE 519 sets the maximum permissible "harmonic noise" each customer may inject into the shared electrical "neighborhood."

---

## 1.5 Active Shaping of the Input Line Current

### Formal Definition
**Active current shaping** is the use of a *controllable* power-electronic switching stage (e.g., a **PWM boost converter** in CCM, or a **PWM voltage-source rectifier**) to force the input current to track a sinusoidal reference that is in phase with the line voltage, achieving near-unity PF and arbitrarily low THD (typically **< 5 %**).

### Conceptual Analogy
Instead of *passively hoping* the current is clean, **active shaping** is like a smart gardener who **actively trims** the current waveform into a perfect sine wave, using high-frequency switching (analogous to using fine scissors rather than a chainsaw).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU Takeaway:** Active shaping is a *closed-loop* current-controlled technique — the controller senses the line voltage and continuously adjusts the duty cycle of the switching device to make $i_{\text{in}}(t) = k \cdot v_{\text{in}}(t)$ (resistive emulation).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Conventional Rectifiers Are "Bad Citizens" of the Grid

A single-phase diode bridge with a **large smoothing inductor** draws a **square-wave current** of amplitude $I_d$:

$$i_s(t) = \begin{cases} +I_d, & 0 < \omega t < \pi \\ -I_d, & \pi < \omega t < 2\pi \end{cases}$$

The Fourier series of this square wave is:

$$i_s(t) = \frac{4 I_d}{\pi} \left[ \sin(\omega t) + \frac{1}{3}\sin(3\omega t) + \frac{1}{5}\sin(5\omega t) + \frac{1}{7}\sin(7\omega t) + \cdots \right]$$

- **Fundamental amplitude:** $I_1 = \dfrac{4 I_d}{\pi}$
- **$n^{\text{th}}$ harmonic (odd $n$):** $I_n = \dfrac{4 I_d}{n\pi}$
- **No even harmonics** (symmetry of the square wave).
- **No DC component** (current is purely AC).

The **RMS value** of the square wave is $I_s = I_d$, while the **fundamental RMS** is $I_1 = \dfrac{4 I_d}{\pi \sqrt{2}}$. Therefore:

$$\frac{I_1}{I_s} = \frac{4}{\pi \sqrt{2}} \approx 0.9003 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{Only 90 \% of the current is "useful" fundamental.}$$

---

## 2.2 Total Harmonic Distortion (THD)

### Definition
$$\text{THD} = \frac{\sqrt{\sum_{n=2}^{\infty} I_n^2}}{I_1} = \frac{\sqrt{I_{\text{rms}}^2 - I_1^2}}{I_1}$$

### Alternative Identity (Easier to Compute)
$$\text{THD} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{I_{\text{rms}}}{I_1}\right)^2 - 1}$$

### Application to the Square Wave
$$\text{THD}_{\text{sq}} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{\pi \sqrt{2}}{4}\right)^2 - 1} = \sqrt{\frac{\pi^2}{8} - 1} \approx \sqrt{0.2337} \approx 0.4834 = 48.34\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> A **single-phase diode bridge** therefore injects **48.34 % harmonic content** into the line current — unacceptable for any modern power-quality-sensitive facility.

---

## 2.3 Power Factor in the Presence of Harmonics

For a non-linear load, the **true PF** splits into two multiplicative factors:

$$\boxed{\;\text{PF} = k_d \cdot k_p = \underbrace{\frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + \text{THD}^2}}}_{\text{Distortion Factor } k_d} \cdot \underbrace{\cos \phi_1}_{\text{Displacement Factor } k_p}\;}$$

| Quantity | Formula | Range / Units |
|:--|:--|:--:|
| **True Power Factor** | $P / S$ | $0 \le \text{PF} \le 1$ |
| **Displacement Factor** $k_p$ | $\cos \phi_1$ | $0 \le k_p \le 1$ |
| **Distortion Factor** $k_d$ | $I_{1,\text{rms}} / I_{\text{rms}}$ | $0 \le k_d \le 1$ |
| **THD** | $\sqrt{\sum_{n=2}^{\infty} I_n^2}\,/\,I_1$ | in % |
| **Crest Factor** | $I_{\text{peak}} / I_{\text{rms}}$ | $\ge 1$ |
| **Fundamental of square wave** | $I_1 = 4I_d / \pi$ | A |
| **RMS of square wave** | $I_s = I_d$ | A |
| **PF of single-phase diode bridge (large L, no overlap)** | $0.9 \cdot \cos \alpha$ | — |
| **PF of 3-phase 6-pulse (large L, no overlap)** | $0.955 \cdot \cos \alpha$ | — |
| **THD of 3-phase 6-pulse** | $31.08\,\%$ | — |
| **THD of 12-pulse rectifier** | $15.22\,\%$ | — |
| **IEEE 519 individual voltage limit (≤ 69 kV)** | $3\,\%$ | — |
| **IEEE 519 TDD voltage limit (≤ 69 kV)** | $5\,\%$ | — |

---

## 2.4 Three-Phase 6-Pulse Rectifier — The Industrial Benchmark

A 6-pulse rectifier with large $L_d$ draws a **quasi-square line current** of amplitude $I_d$ with 120° conduction. Fourier analysis gives:

$$i_a(t) = \frac{2\sqrt{3}}{\pi} I_d \left[ \sin(\omega t - 30^\circ) - \frac{1}{5}\sin(5\omega t - 150^\circ) + \frac{1}{7}\sin(7\omega t - 210^\circ) - \frac{1}{11}\sin(11\omega t - 30^\circ) + \cdots \right]$$

**Characteristic harmonic order:** $h = 6k \pm 1$ for $k = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$ → $5, 7, 11, 13, 17, 19, \ldots$

**Amplitude of $h^{\text{th}}$ harmonic:** $I_h = I_1 / h$

| $h$ | 5 | 7 | 11 | 13 | 17 | 19 | 23 | 25 |
|:--:|:--:|:--:|:--:|:--:|:--:|:--:|:--:|:--:|
| $I_h / I_1$ (%) | 20.0 | 14.3 | 9.1 | 7.7 | 5.9 | 5.3 | 4.4 | 4.0 |

$$\text{THD}_{6\text{-pulse}} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{5^2} + \frac{1}{7^2} + \frac{1}{11^2} + \frac{1}{13^2} + \cdots} = 31.08\,\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Triplen harmonics (3, 9, 15, …) cancel** in balanced 3-phase systems (no neutral current), which is why the dominant harmonics are 5 and 7.

---

## 2.5 IEEE 519-2014 — Key Limits

### A) Voltage Distortion Limits at PCC

| Bus Voltage $V$ at PCC | Individual Harmonic | Total Harmonic Distortion (THD) |
|:--|:--:|:--:|
| $V \le 69$ kV | $3.0\,\%$ | $5.0\,\%$ |
| $69 < V \le 161$ kV | $1.5\,\%$ | $2.5\,\%$ |
| $V > 161$ kV | $1.0\,\%$ | $1.5\,\%$ |

### B) Current Distortion Limits (TDD) — for $V \le 69$ kV

| Maximum $I_{sc}/I_L$ | $h < 11$ | $11 \le h < 17$ | $17 \le h < 23$ | $23 \le h < 35$ | **TDD** |
|:--:|:--:|:--:|:--:|:--:|:--:|
| $< 20$ | $4.0\,\%$ | $2.0\,\%$ | $1.5\,\%$ | $0.6\,\%$ | $5.0\,\%$ |
| $20 - 50$ | $7.0\,\%$ | $3.5\,\%$ | $2.5\,\%$ | $1.0\,\%$ | $8.0\,\%$ |
| $50 - 100$ | $10.0\,\%$ | $4.5\,\%$ | $4.0\,\%$ | $1.5\,\%$ | $12.0\,\%$ |
| $100 - 1000$ | $12.0\,\%$ | $5.5\,\%$ | $5.0\,\%$ | $2.0\,\%$ | $15.0\,\%$ |
| $> 1000$ | $15.0\,\%$ | $7.0\,\%$ | $6.0\,\%$ | $2.5\,\%$ | $20.0\,\%$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **TDD (Total Demand Distortion)** is the same as THD, but referenced to the **maximum demand load current** $I_L$ over a 15- or 30-minute interval, *not* to the instantaneous fundamental. This prevents customers from cheating the standard by reducing load at the time of measurement.

---

## 2.6 Active Shaping — Operating Principle

The **single-phase active PFC boost converter** works as follows:

1. **Sensing:** The input voltage $v_s(t)$ is sensed and rectified to give a half-sinusoid $|v_s(t)|$.
2. **Reference generation:** A reference current is created: $i_{\text{ref}}(t) = k \cdot |v_s(t)|$, scaled by the output of the **voltage error amplifier** (outer loop, slow).
3. **Current shaping:** An **inner current loop** (fast) modulates the MOSFET duty cycle $D(t)$ such that the inductor current $i_L(t)$ tracks $i_{\text{ref}}(t)$.
4. **Result:** $i_s(t) = $ sinusoid, in phase with $v_s(t)$ → **PF ≈ 1, THD < 5 %**.

The **input impedance** of a perfectly controlled PFC converter behaves as a **pure resistance** $R_e = V_s^2 / (2 P_o)$ — the converter is "invisible" to the grid.

> [!TIP]
> **Why Boost, not Buck?** The boost topology has a continuous input current waveform and can shape current over the *entire* AC half-cycle (including the zero-crossing region), whereas a buck converter has a "dead-zone" near the zero-crossing where it cannot conduct.
