# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> All derivations are written for the typical KTU 14-mark question pattern: derive the harmonic spectrum, calculate THD, and compute PF.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Harmonic Spectrum of the Single-Phase Diode Bridge Input Current

### Statement
For a single-phase full-bridge diode rectifier with a *very large* DC-side filter inductor $L_d$ (continuous, ripple-free DC current $I_d$), the AC-side line current is a *pure square wave* of amplitude $I_d$ in phase with the corresponding half-cycle of the supply voltage. Derive the Fourier series, the fundamental RMS, the harmonic amplitudes, the THD, and the displacement & distortion factors.

### Step 1 — Express the current waveform
The square wave is an *odd function* with *half-wave symmetry* (i.e., $i(t + T/2) = -i(t)$). Therefore:

$$i_s(t) = \begin{cases} +I_d, & 0 \le \omega t \le \pi \\ -I_d, & \pi \le \omega t \le 2\pi \end{cases}$$

### Step 2 — Compute the Fourier coefficients
For a function with half-wave symmetry, only **odd** harmonics exist, and the sine series simplifies to:

$$a_n = \frac{2}{T} \int_0^T i_s(t) \sin(n \omega t) \, dt = \frac{2}{\pi} \left[ \int_0^{\pi} I_d \sin(n\omega t) \, d(\omega t) + \int_{\pi}^{2\pi} (-I_d) \sin(n\omega t) \, d(\omega t) \right]$$

### Step 3 — Evaluate the integrals
For odd $n$:

$$a_n = \frac{2 I_d}{\pi} \left[ \int_0^{\pi} \sin(n\omega t)\,d(\omega t) - \int_{\pi}^{2\pi} \sin(n\omega t)\,d(\omega t) \right]$$

$$\int_0^{\pi} \sin(n\omega t)\,d(\omega t) = \left[ -\frac{\cos(n\omega t)}{n} \right]_0^{\pi} = \frac{-\cos(n\pi) + 1}{n} = \frac{1 - (-1)^n}{n} = \frac{2}{n} \quad (n \text{ odd})$$

$$\int_{\pi}^{2\pi} \sin(n\omega t)\,d(\omega t) = \left[ -\frac{\cos(n\omega t)}{n} \right]_{\pi}^{2\pi} = \frac{-\cos(2n\pi) + \cos(n\pi)}{n} = \frac{-1 + (-1)^n}{n} = \frac{-2}{n} \quad (n \text{ odd})$$

### Step 4 — Substitute back

$$a_n = \frac{2 I_d}{\pi} \left[ \frac{2}{n} - \left(\frac{-2}{n}\right) \right] = \frac{2 I_d}{\pi} \cdot \frac{4}{n} = \frac{8 I_d}{n\pi} \quad (n \text{ odd})$$

Wait — this is the *full* sine series, but with the half-range convention the standard square-wave Fourier series is recovered as:

$$i_s(t) = \frac{4 I_d}{\pi} \sum_{n=1,3,5,\ldots}^{\infty} \frac{1}{n} \sin(n \omega t)$$

### Step 5 — Final Fourier series

$$\boxed{\;i_s(t) = \frac{4 I_d}{\pi} \left[ \sin(\omega t) + \frac{1}{3}\sin(3\omega t) + \frac{1}{5}\sin(5\omega t) + \frac{1}{7}\sin(7\omega t) + \cdots \right]\;}$$

### Step 6 — RMS value
For a square wave of amplitude $I_d$:

$$I_{s,\text{rms}} = I_d \quad (\text{since } I_{\text{rms}} = \sqrt{\tfrac{1}{2\pi}\int_0^{2\pi}I_d^2\,d(\omega t)} = I_d)$$

### Step 7 — Fundamental RMS
The peak of the fundamental is $I_1 = \dfrac{4 I_d}{\pi}$. Therefore:

$$I_{1,\text{rms}} = \frac{I_1}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{4 I_d}{\pi \sqrt{2}}$$

### Step 8 — Distortion factor $k_d$

$$k_d = \frac{I_{1,\text{rms}}}{I_{s,\text{rms}}} = \frac{4 I_d / (\pi\sqrt{2})}{I_d} = \frac{4}{\pi \sqrt{2}} = \frac{2\sqrt{2}}{\pi} \approx 0.9003$$

### Step 9 — THD

$$\text{THD} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{I_{s,\text{rms}}}{I_{1,\text{rms}}}\right)^2 - 1} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{\pi\sqrt{2}}{4}\right)^2 - 1} = \sqrt{\frac{\pi^2}{8} - 1} \approx 0.4834$$

$$\boxed{\;\text{THD}_{\text{single-phase}} = 48.34\,\%}$$

### Step 10 — Displacement & True Power Factor
Since the square wave is in phase with the voltage (no phase shift between fundamental of current and voltage):

$$k_p = \cos \phi_1 = 1.0$$

$$\boxed{\;\text{PF} = k_d \cdot k_p = 0.9003 \times 1.0 = 0.9003 \approx 0.9\;}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — THD of a Three-Phase 6-Pulse Rectifier

### Statement
A three-phase 6-pulse diode/thyristor bridge with very large $L_d$ draws a quasi-square line current. Derive THD and PF.

### Step 1 — Waveform of line current
Each line conducts for **120°** with amplitude $+I_d$, then for **60°** with $0$, then for **120°** with $-I_d$, and so on.

### Step 2 — RMS of line current
With current $\pm I_d$ present for $\tfrac{2}{3}$ of the cycle (and zero for $\tfrac{1}{3}$):

$$I_{L,\text{rms}} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{2\pi} \cdot 2 \cdot I_d^2 \cdot \frac{2\pi}{3}} = I_d \sqrt{\frac{2}{3}}$$

### Step 3 — Fundamental RMS
The fundamental peak of the quasi-square wave (from standard Fourier analysis of a 120°-conduction waveform) is:

$$I_1 = \frac{2\sqrt{3}}{\pi} I_d \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; I_{1,\text{rms}} = \frac{2\sqrt{3}}{\pi \sqrt{2}} I_d = \frac{\sqrt{6}}{\pi} I_d$$

### Step 4 — Distortion factor

$$k_d = \frac{I_{1,\text{rms}}}{I_{L,\text{rms}}} = \frac{\sqrt{6}\,I_d / \pi}{I_d \sqrt{2/3}} = \frac{\sqrt{6}}{\pi} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{3}{2}} = \frac{\sqrt{9}}{\pi} = \frac{3}{\pi} \approx 0.955$$

### Step 5 — THD

$$\text{THD} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{\pi}{3}\right)^2 - 1} = \sqrt{\frac{\pi^2}{9} - 1} = \sqrt{1.0966 - 1} = \sqrt{0.0966} \approx 0.3108$$

$$\boxed{\;\text{THD}_{6\text{-pulse}} = 31.08\,\%}$$

### Step 6 — Power factor (with firing angle $\alpha$)

$$\boxed{\;\text{PF}_{6\text{-pulse}} = \frac{3}{\pi} \cos \alpha \approx 0.955 \cos \alpha\;}$$

For $\alpha = 0$ (diode bridge): $\text{PF} \approx 0.955$. For $\alpha = 30°$: $\text{PF} \approx 0.827$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Harmonic Content of a 12-Pulse Rectifier

A 12-pulse system uses **two 6-pulse bridges** fed by a **Δ-Y and Y-Y transformer pair** with **30° phase shift**. The triplen and certain characteristic harmonics cancel.

**Resulting characteristic harmonic order:** $h = 12k \pm 1$ → $11, 13, 23, 25, 35, 37, \ldots$

$$\text{THD}_{12\text{-pulse}} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{11^2} + \frac{1}{13^2} + \frac{1}{23^2} + \frac{1}{25^2} + \cdots} \approx 15.22\,\%$$

This is **half** of the 6-pulse THD, hence the popularity of 12-pulse rectifiers in industrial drives and HVDC.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Boost PFC Operating Point

### Statement
A single-phase boost PFC delivers $P_o = 1$ kW to a 400 V DC bus from a 230 V RMS, 50 Hz mains. Determine the equivalent resistance seen by the grid, the peak input current, and the required duty cycle at the AC voltage peak.

### Step 1 — Equivalent emulated resistance
The input of a boost PFC *emulates* a resistor $R_e$:

$$R_e = \frac{V_{s,\text{rms}}^2}{P_o} \cdot \frac{1}{2} = \frac{230^2}{1000} \cdot \frac{1}{2} = \frac{52900}{2000} = 26.45 \;\Omega$$

> (The factor $\tfrac{1}{2}$ comes from averaging across a half-cycle in a single-phase full-bridge input stage.)

### Step 2 — Peak input current

$$I_{s,\text{peak}} = \frac{V_{s,\text{peak}}}{R_e} = \frac{230 \sqrt{2}}{26.45} = \frac{325.27}{26.45} \approx 12.30\;\text{A}$$

### Step 3 — Required duty cycle at voltage peak
At the voltage peak, $v_{\text{in}}(t) = V_{s,\text{peak}}$. For a boost converter in CCM:

$$V_o = \frac{V_{\text{in}}}{1 - D} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; D = 1 - \frac{V_{\text{in}}}{V_o} = 1 - \frac{325.27}{400} = 1 - 0.8132 = 0.1868$$

$$\boxed{\;D_{\text{at-peak}} \approx 0.187\;}$$

### Step 4 — Duty cycle at voltage zero-crossing
As $v_{\text{in}} \to 0$, $D \to 1$ (MOSFET almost always on), enabling continuous current shaping through the zero-crossing.

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation underpins every active-PFC design in KTU board examinations. Always state the **assumed output power**, **input voltage**, and **bus voltage** explicitly to obtain full marks.

---

## 3.5 Summary Matrix — Harmonics, PF, and Mitigation

| Topology | PF (α = 0) | THD | Dominant Harmonics | Mitigation Method |
|:--|:--:|:--:|:--|:--|
| 1-φ Diode bridge (large L) | 0.90 | 48.34 % | 3, 5, 7, 9 … | Active boost PFC |
| 1-φ Diode bridge (large C) | 0.60 – 0.65 | 80 – 100 % | All orders | Active boost PFC |
| 3-φ 6-pulse | 0.955 | 31.08 % | 5, 7, 11, 13 | 12/18-pulse, PWM rectifier |
| 3-φ 12-pulse | 0.989 | 15.22 % | 11, 13, 23, 25 | Active filter |
| 3-φ PWM (active) | ≈ 0.99 | < 5 % | Negligible | Inherently clean |

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### Question A1  `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Set A]`
**Define the following with respect to power-quality assessment of non-linear loads:**
(a) Total Harmonic Distortion (THD)
(b) Displacement Power Factor and Distortion Power Factor
(c) Total Demand Distortion (TDD) as per IEEE 519

#### Model Answer
(a) **THD** is the ratio of the RMS of the harmonic content (all components above the fundamental) to the RMS of the fundamental, expressed as a percentage. It quantifies the *non-sinusoidality* of a periodic waveform.

$$\text{THD} = \frac{\sqrt{\sum_{n=2}^{\infty} I_n^2}}{I_1} \times 100\,\%$$

(b) The **Displacement Power Factor** $k_p = \cos \phi_1$ depends only on the phase angle between the **fundamental components** of voltage and current. The **Distortion Power Factor** $k_d = I_{1,\text{rms}} / I_{\text{rms}}$ depends on the harmonic content of the current. The true PF is the product $k_p \cdot k_d$.

(c) **TDD (Total Demand Distortion)** is identical in formula to THD, but the reference is the **maximum demand load current** $I_L$ (sustained over 15 or 30 min), not the instantaneous fundamental. It is the metric used in IEEE 519 to prevent customers from gaming the measurement.

---

### Question A2  `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Set B]`
**Explain in brief why conventional diode-bridge rectifiers are considered "non-linear loads" and list three consequences of injecting harmonic currents into the utility grid.**

#### Model Answer
A diode bridge is *non-linear* because its current waveform $i_s(t)$ is **not** linearly proportional to the applied voltage $v_s(t)$. During each half-cycle, the diodes conduct only when the instantaneous input voltage exceeds the DC-bus voltage, drawing short, peaked (or quasi-square) current pulses. The resulting current contains frequencies other than the supply fundamental — i.e., **harmonics**.

**Three consequences of harmonic injection:**
1. **Overheating of transformers, cables, and neutral conductors** due to skin-effect and proximity-effect losses that scale with $n^2$ for the $n^{\text{th}}$ harmonic.
2. **Resonance excitation** with utility power-factor-correction capacitors, leading to over-voltages and fuse/capacitor failures.
3. **Malfunction of sensitive electronic equipment** (PLCs, computers, medical instruments) due to voltage zero-crossing noise and waveform distortion.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **ESE Pattern (14 Marks):** Each Part B question carries 14 marks and is divided into two sub-parts of 7 marks each. Both alternatives (Q-A and Q-B) are provided below; only **one** need be attempted.

---

### Question A — Module 3 Choice 1  `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** A single-phase full-bridge diode rectifier supplies a *highly inductive* DC load drawing a constant current $I_d = 50$ A. The AC mains is 230 V, 50 Hz. For the AC-side line current:
- (i) Derive the Fourier series of the input current waveform.
- (ii) Calculate the fundamental RMS and the 3rd, 5th, and 7th harmonic RMS values.
- (iii) Compute the **THD** of the input current.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Define the **true power factor** for a non-linear load. For the rectifier in part (a), compute the **displacement factor**, **distortion factor**, and **true PF**. Comment on whether this load meets the IEEE 519 current-TDD limit of 5 % for $I_{sc}/I_L < 20$.

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — (i) Fourier series derivation:**
With $L_d \to \infty$, the AC line current is a pure square wave of amplitude $\pm I_d$:

$$i_s(t) = \frac{4 I_d}{\pi} \left[ \sin(\omega t) + \frac{1}{3}\sin(3\omega t) + \frac{1}{5}\sin(5\omega t) + \frac{1}{7}\sin(7\omega t) + \cdots \right]$$

Substituting $I_d = 50$ A:

$$i_s(t) = \frac{200}{\pi} \left[ \sin(\omega t) + \frac{1}{3}\sin(3\omega t) + \frac{1}{5}\sin(5\omega t) + \frac{1}{7}\sin(7\omega t) + \cdots \right] = 63.66 \left[ \cdots \right] \text{ A}$$

**Part (a) — (ii) Harmonic RMS values:**

- Fundamental peak: $I_1 = 4I_d/\pi = 4(50)/\pi = 63.66$ A
- Fundamental RMS: $I_{1,\text{rms}} = I_1/\sqrt{2} = 63.66/\sqrt{2} = 45.02$ A
- 3rd harmonic peak: $I_3 = I_1/3 = 21.22$ A → RMS: $15.01$ A
- 5th harmonic peak: $I_5 = I_1/5 = 12.73$ A → RMS: $9.00$ A
- 7th harmonic peak: $I_7 = I_1/7 = 9.09$ A → RMS: $6.43$ A

**Part (a) — (iii) THD:**

$$\text{THD} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{I_{s,\text{rms}}}{I_{1,\text{rms}}}\right)^2 - 1} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{50}{45.02}\right)^2 - 1} = \sqrt{1.2337 - 1} = \sqrt{0.2337} = 0.4834$$

$$\boxed{\;\text{THD} = 48.34\,\%}$$

**Part (b) — True PF definition:**

For a non-linear load supplied by a sinusoidal voltage $v_s(t) = V_m \sin \omega t$ and drawing a non-sinusoidal current $i_s(t)$:

$$\text{PF} = \frac{P}{S} = \frac{V_{s,\text{rms}} \cdot I_{1,\text{rms}} \cdot \cos \phi_1}{V_{s,\text{rms}} \cdot I_{s,\text{rms}}} = k_d \cdot k_p$$

**Part (b) — Computations:**

- **Displacement factor:** $k_p = \cos \phi_1 = 1$ (square wave in phase with voltage)
- **Distortion factor:** $k_d = I_{1,\text{rms}}/I_{s,\text{rms}} = 45.02/50 = 0.9003$
- **True PF:** $\text{PF} = 0.9003 \times 1 = 0.9003 \approx 0.90$

**Part (b) — IEEE 519 compliance:**

A THD of **48.34 %** vastly exceeds the IEEE 519 TDD limit of **5 %** (for $I_{sc}/I_L < 20$). The 3rd harmonic alone is 33.3 % of the fundamental, exceeding the 4 % individual limit for $h < 11$. **The load fails IEEE 519 and must be fitted with active PFC or a passive harmonic filter.**

---

### Question B — Module 3 Choice 2  `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the operation of a **single-phase active boost PFC converter**. Derive the expression for the **emulated input resistance** and compute its value for $V_s = 230$ V RMS, $P_o = 750$ W.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 3-phase, 6-pulse diode rectifier feeds an inductive DC load drawing $I_d = 100$ A. Compute:
- (i) The fundamental RMS line current and the RMS of the 5th, 7th, and 11th harmonics.
- (ii) The total RMS line current.
- (iii) The **THD** of the line current and the **displacement** and **true** power factors.

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Block diagram and operation:**

The active boost PFC consists of:
1. A **full-bridge diode rectifier** at the input.
2. A **boost inductor** $L$ in series.
3. A **MOSFET switch** (IGBT for higher power) from the boost-node to ground.
4. A **boost diode** from the boost-node to the DC bus.
5. A **DC bus capacitor** $C$ for energy storage and output ripple filtering.
6. A **PWM controller** implementing two control loops:
   - **Outer (slow) voltage loop:** Regulates the DC bus voltage $V_o$ to its reference.
   - **Inner (fast) current loop:** Forces the inductor current $i_L(t)$ to track $i_{\text{ref}}(t) = k(t) \cdot |v_s(t)|$ where $k(t)$ is the output of the voltage loop.

**Derivation of emulated resistance:**
The instantaneous input power delivered to the DC bus over one switching cycle equals the input power averaged over one AC half-cycle. For a pure resistor $R_e$:

$$p_{\text{in}}(t) = \frac{v_s^2(t)}{R_e} = \frac{V_m^2 \sin^2 \omega t}{R_e}$$

Averaging over a half-cycle:

$$P_{\text{in}} = \frac{V_m^2}{2 R_e} = \frac{V_{s,\text{rms}}^2}{R_e}$$

Solving for $R_e$:

$$\boxed{\;R_e = \frac{V_{s,\text{rms}}^2}{P_{\text{in}}} = \frac{V_{s,\text{rms}}^2}{P_o}\;}$$

**Numerical value for $V_s = 230$ V, $P_o = 750$ W:**

$$R_e = \frac{230^2}{750} = \frac{52900}{750} \approx 70.53\;\Omega$$

(The factor of 2 appears in single-phase PFC derivations where $R_e = V_{s,\text{rms}}^2 / (2 P_o)$ — both forms are equivalent; the $2 P_o$ form comes from the full-bridge input stage. Here we used the convention $R_e = V_{s,\text{rms}}^2 / P_o$ for the line-side emulated resistance.)

**Part (b) — (i) Harmonic RMS values:**

The line current of a 3-phase 6-pulse rectifier with large $L_d$ is a quasi-square wave. Its Fourier series is:

$$i_a(t) = \frac{2\sqrt{3}}{\pi} I_d \sum_{k=1,2,3,\ldots}^{\infty} \left[\frac{(-1)^{k+1}}{6k-1} \sin\bigl((6k-1)\omega t\bigr) + \frac{(-1)^{k+1}}{6k+1} \sin\bigl((6k+1)\omega t\bigr)\right]$$

- Fundamental peak: $I_1 = \dfrac{2\sqrt{3}}{\pi} I_d = \dfrac{2\sqrt{3}}{\pi}(100) \approx 110.26$ A
- Fundamental RMS: $I_{1,\text{rms}} = I_1/\sqrt{2} \approx 77.97$ A
- 5th harmonic RMS: $I_{5,\text{rms}} = I_{1,\text{rms}}/5 = 15.59$ A
- 7th harmonic RMS: $I_{7,\text{rms}} = I_{1,\text{rms}}/7 = 11.14$ A
- 11th harmonic RMS: $I_{11,\text{rms}} = I_{1,\text{rms}}/11 = 7.09$ A

**Part (b) — (ii) Total RMS line current:**

With conduction for $\tfrac{2}{3}$ of the cycle:

$$I_{L,\text{rms}} = I_d \sqrt{\frac{2}{3}} = 100 \sqrt{\frac{2}{3}} = 81.65\;\text{A}$$

**Part (b) — (iii) THD, $k_p$, PF:**

$$\text{THD} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{I_{L,\text{rms}}}{I_{1,\text{rms}}}\right)^2 - 1} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{81.65}{77.97}\right)^2 - 1} = \sqrt{1.0963 - 1} = 0.3106 = 31.06\,\%$$

Displacement factor: $k_p = \cos \phi_1 = 1$ (rectifier with $\alpha = 0$)

True PF: $\text{PF} = k_d \cdot k_p = (I_{1,\text{rms}}/I_{L,\text{rms}}) \cdot 1 = 77.97/81.65 = 0.955$

$$\boxed{\;\text{THD} \approx 31.08\,\%,\quad k_p = 1,\quad \text{PF} \approx 0.955\;}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $\sqrt{2}$:** When asked for the *RMS* harmonic current, students write $I_h = 4I_d/(n\pi)$ (peak value) instead of dividing by $\sqrt{2}$. **This loses 2 marks.**
> 2. **Confusing THD with TDD:** THD is referenced to the *fundamental*; TDD is referenced to the *maximum demand load current*. They are equal only when the load is operating at full demand. For a partial-load condition, TDD < THD.
> 3. **Sign error in displacement factor:** The current *leads* the voltage in some converter topologies (e.g., capacitive-filter rectifiers) — the displacement angle $\phi_1$ is **negative**, but $\cos \phi_1$ is still positive. Never write $\text{PF} = -\cos \phi_1$.
> 4. **Dropping the factor $4/\pi\sqrt{2}$:** The single-phase diode-bridge PF is *exactly* $0.9003$, **not** $0.9$ approximately. Writing "PF = 0.9" is acceptable only when the derivation is shown.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — print this before every KTU exam!**

- [ ] **Fourier series of single-phase square wave** (diode bridge, large $L_d$): $i(t) = \frac{4I_d}{\pi}\sum_{n=1,3,5,\ldots}\frac{1}{n}\sin(n\omega t)$.
- [ ] **THD of single-phase diode bridge = 48.34 %**.
- [ ] **PF of single-phase diode bridge = 0.9003** (= $2\sqrt{2}/\pi$).
- [ ] **THD of 3-phase 6-pulse = 31.08 %**.
- [ ] **PF of 3-phase 6-pulse = $(3/\pi)\cos\alpha$ = 0.955 $\cos\alpha$**.
- [ ] **THD of 12-pulse = 15.22 %** (harmonic order $12k\pm 1$).
- [ ] **Characteristic harmonics of 6-pulse** = $6k\pm 1$ → $5, 7, 11, 13, 17, 19$.
- [ ] **True PF = $k_d \cdot k_p = \frac{I_{1,\text{rms}}}{I_{\text{rms}}} \cdot \cos\phi_1$**.
- [ ] **$k_d = 1/\sqrt{1 + \text{THD}^2}$**.
- [ ] **Crest factor** $= I_{\text{peak}}/I_{\text{rms}}$.
- [ ] **IEEE 519 voltage limits** (≤ 69 kV): Individual 3 %, THD 5 %.
- [ ] **IEEE 519 current limits**: expressed in TDD; stricter for *weak* grids (low $I_{sc}/I_L$).
- [ ] **Active PFC** = closed-loop current shaping; boost topology is most common; emulates a resistor $R_e = V_{s,\text{rms}}^2/P_o$.
- [ ] **Passive mitigation** = multi-pulse transformers, tuned LC filters, power-factor-correction capacitors.
- [ ] **Active mitigation** = shunt APF, series APF, hybrid APF, PWM rectifiers, active PFC front-ends.
- [ ] **Triplen harmonics (3, 9, 15, …) cancel in balanced 3-phase systems.**
- [ ] **Active shaping achieves PF > 0.99 and THD < 5 %** in well-designed single-phase boost PFCs.
- [ ] **At AC voltage peak, the boost PFC duty cycle is minimum** ($D_{\min} = 1 - V_m/V_o$); **at zero-crossing, $D \to 1$**.
- [ ] **Remember the 30° phase shift in 12-pulse rectifiers** between Δ-Y and Y-Y secondaries — this is the key to harmonic cancellation.
- [ ] **Always state assumptions** (large $L_d$, no overlap, balanced supply, sinusoidal mains) at the start of a derivation — this is worth 1 mark in KTU answers.

> **Final KTU Tip:** When a question asks for "improved utility interface," always discuss **both** passive (LC filters, multi-pulse) **and** active (PWM rectifier, APF, boost PFC) solutions, citing the trade-off between **cost**, **complexity**, **size**, and **harmonic reduction achieved**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Electric Drives: Advantages of Adjustable Speed Electric Drives, Block Diagram, Types of Loads, Classification of Load Torque, Motor Torque-Load Combination: Characteristics and Dynamic Equation, Steady State Stability
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOT502 - POWER ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Electric Drives: Advantages of Adjustable Speed Electric Drives, Block Diagram, Types of Loads, Classification of Load Torque, Motor Torque-Load Combination: Characteristics and Dynamic Equation, Steady State Stability

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Definition of an Electric Drive

An **Electric Drive** is a sophisticated electromechanical system employed to regulate the speed, position, and torque of an electric motor in accordance with the operational demands of a mechanical load. As per the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus, it is formally defined as an integrated arrangement comprising a **source**, a **power electronic converter**, a **motor**, a **mechanical load**, and a **control unit** working in unison to deliver controlled mechanical motion.

Mathematically, the generalized function of an electric drive can be expressed as the conversion of electrical energy into mechanical energy with controllable parameters.

$$P_{electrical} = V \cdot I \cdot \cos{\phi} \quad \longrightarrow \quad P_{mechanical} = T \cdot \omega$$

where the right-hand side output (rotational mechanical power) is the controlled variable.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** An electric drive is **not just a motor**. It is the *entire system* — power supply, converter, motor, mechanical coupling, and feedback control — that performs the controlled motion task.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy: The Car Accelerator System

Imagine you are driving a car. The **engine** is the motor, the **gearbox** acts as the power conditioning stage, the **road** is the load, and your **foot on the accelerator** is the control input. By varying the accelerator position, you change the speed and torque delivered to the wheels — this is *adjustable speed drive* in action. Without the accelerator, the car would either stay at rest or run at one fixed speed; you would have no control. An electric drive does the same thing for industrial processes — fans, pumps, conveyors, elevators, rolling mills, and robotic arms.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Three Decision Questions a Drive Answers:**
> 1. *How fast?* → Speed control
> 2. *How much force?* → Torque control
> 3. *Where exactly?* → Position control
>
> Every industrial drive answers at least one of these questions.

## 1.3 Advantages of Adjustable Speed Electric Drives (ASD)

Adjustable Speed Drives (also called **Variable Frequency Drives / VFDs** when using AC motors) are the backbone of modern industrial automation. The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly demands understanding of their advantages.

> [!NOTE]
> **Primary Advantages of Adjustable Speed Drives**

1. **Energy Conservation:** The single largest economic benefit. Industrial fans and pumps driven by ASDs can save **30% to 60%** of electrical energy compared to fixed-speed drives with throttling valves or dampers.

2. **Improved Process Control:** Precision control of speed and torque leads to tighter tolerances in manufacturing (e.g., paper mills, textile looms, rolling mills).

3. **Soft Starting:** Eliminates the inrush current (typically **6 to 8 times** the rated current) of direct-on-line starters. This reduces stress on the motor windings, supply grid, and mechanical couplings.

4. **Reduced Mechanical Wear:** Smooth acceleration and deceleration reduce wear on belts, gears, bearings, and shafts — significantly extending equipment life.

5. **Power Factor Correction:** Modern PWM-based drives operate at near-unity power factor at rated load, eliminating the need for separate capacitor banks in many cases.

6. **Reversibility and Regeneration:** Four-quadrant drives can feed braking energy back to the supply, further reducing net energy consumption.

7. **Compact Mechanical Transmission:** Variable speed eliminates the need for complex gearboxes, pulleys, and mechanical speed-change mechanisms.

8. **Lower Maintenance Cost:** Fewer mechanical parts subject to wear translates directly to lower OPEX.

9. **Better Product Quality:** In processes like glass manufacturing or paper winding, constant *tension* (not constant speed) is required — only ASDs can deliver this.

10. **Compliance with Standards:** Helps meet energy efficiency regulations (e.g., IE3/IE4 motor standards, ISO 50001).

## 1.4 Block Diagram of an Electric Drive — Overview

The functional block diagram of a modern electric drive consists of the following major stages:

**Source → Power Converter → Motor → Load**  
**with feedback loop: Sensors → Controller → Reference Command**

This will be elaborated in detail in Section 2 with full mathematical modeling.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Key Takeaway:** Power Electronics is the *enabling technology* for electric drives. Without semiconductor switches (SCR, IGBT, MOSFET, GTO), modern adjustable speed drives would be impossible.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Functional Block Diagram of an Electric Drive

A complete electric drive system has the following functional blocks:

### Block 1: Power Source
- AC mains (1-phase or 3-phase, **50 Hz** in India)
- DC battery
- Renewable source (solar PV, wind)

### Block 2: Power Electronic Converter
The "brain" of the drive. Its topology depends on the motor type:
- **AC-DC Converter (Rectifier):** For DC motor armature supply
- **DC-DC Converter (Chopper):** For DC motor speed control
- **AC-AC Converter (Cycloconverter / AC Voltage Controller):** For low-speed large motors
- **DC-AC Converter (Inverter — VSI / CSI):** For AC motor (induction / synchronous) speed control using V/f method
- **AC-DC-AC Converter (Rectifier + Inverter):** For VFDs (most common industrial topology)

### Block 3: Electric Motor
- **DC Motors:** Separately Excited, Shunt, Series, Compound
- **AC Motors:** Induction (squirrel cage, wound rotor), Synchronous, BLDC, PMSM, SRM

### Block 4: Mechanical Load
The device that the motor drives. Examples: pump, fan, compressor, conveyor, elevator, lathe, robotic arm.

### Block 5: Sensors and Controller
- **Sensors:** Tachogenerator, encoder, current transformer (CT), Hall-effect sensor
- **Controller:** PI/PID controller, microcontroller, DSP, FPGA — compares actual vs reference and generates firing pulses for the converter

## 2.2 Types of Loads in Electric Drives

Loads are classified based on the relationship between load torque ($T_L$) and speed ($\omega$).

### A. Passive Loads
Loads that always oppose motion (energy dissipative or storage). Examples: fans, pumps, conveyors.

> [!NOTE]
> **Classification of Passive Loads by Torque-Speed Relationship**

| Type of Load | Torque Equation | Torque-Speed Curve | Examples |
|--------------|-----------------|-------------------|----------|
| **Constant Torque Load** | $T_L = \text{constant}$ | Horizontal line | Conveyors, hoists, compressors, extruders |
| **Linear Torque Load (Viscous)** | $T_L \propto \omega$ | Straight line through origin | Mixers, viscous friction loads |
| **Parabolic (Square Law) Load** | $T_L \propto \omega^2$ | Parabolic curve | Fans, centrifugal pumps, blowers |
| **Inverse Parabolic (Hyperbolic) Load** | $T_L \propto 1/\omega$ (so $P = T_L \cdot \omega = \text{constant}$) | Hyperbolic curve | Rolling mills, machine tool spindles, coil winders |
| **Constant Power Load** | $T_L = P / \omega$ | Hyperbolic curve | Same as inverse parabolic |

### B. Active Loads
Loads that can either assist or oppose motion. Energy is two-way. Example: **hoist** (gravity assists lowering, opposes lifting), locomotive on a slope, wind loads.

## 2.3 Classification of Load Torque (Components)

The total load torque on a motor shaft is the sum of the following components:

$$T_L = T_{friction} + T_{windage} + T_{core} + T_{mechanical\;work} + T_{inertial}$$

### A. Friction Torque ($T_{friction}$)
Two sub-types:
1. **Coulomb (Dry) Friction:** $T_f = \mu \cdot F_N \cdot r$ — independent of speed
2. **Viscous Friction:** $T_v = B \cdot \omega$ — proportional to speed

### B. Windage Torque ($T_{windage}$)
Air resistance: $T_w \propto \omega^2$

### C. Core / Stray Loss Torque
Proportional to speed squared for AC machines: $T_{core} \propto \omega^2$

### D. Torque Due to Useful Mechanical Work
This is the *load torque* the process demands — lifting, compressing, cutting, mixing.

### E. Torque Due to Deformation (in rolling mills, calendar rolls)
$T \propto L$ (length of material being deformed)

### F. Inertial Torque (Transient only, not steady state)
$T_{inertia} = J \cdot \frac{d\omega}{dt}$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Trap:** Many students forget that during steady-state operation ($\frac{d\omega}{dt} = 0$), the inertial torque is ZERO. It appears only during acceleration or deceleration.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $T - T_L = J \dfrac{d\omega_m}{dt} + B \omega_m$ | Fundamental dynamic equation of motor-load system | N·m |
| 2 | $T - T_L = J \dfrac{2\pi}{60} \dfrac{dN}{dt} + B \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$ | Same, in terms of N (rpm) | N·m |
| 3 | $\omega_m = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$ | Angular speed from rpm | rad/s |
| 4 | $J = m k^2$ | Moment of inertia (GD² form, with $k$ = radius of gyration) | kg·m² |
| 5 | $J_{equivalent} = J_{motor} + J_{load} \left(\dfrac{N_{load}}{N_{motor}}\right)^2$ | Reflected load inertia to motor shaft | kg·m² |
| 6 | $P = T \cdot \omega_m$ | Mechanical power | W |
| 7 | $T_L \propto \omega^2$ | Fan/pump load (square law) | — |
| 8 | $T_L = \text{constant}$ | Hoist, conveyor | — |
| 9 | Stability condition: $\dfrac{dT}{d\omega} < \dfrac{dT_L}{d\omega}$ | Stable operating point | — |
| 10 | $T_{acceleration} = J \dfrac{d\omega}{dt}$ | Accelerating torque | N·m |

> **Note on $\vert$ usage:** All absolute-value / modulus operators in the formulas above (none used, but if required) should be written as `\vert x \vert` in LaTeX to avoid breaking the markdown table.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility of These Concepts

Understanding the dynamic equation and load characteristics is critical for:
- **Choosing the right motor rating** (so it never stalls under peak load).
- **Designing starting/braking circuits** (so motor torque always exceeds load torque during acceleration).
- **Tuning PID controllers** (the load's $J$ and $B$ determine the controller response).
- **Predicting energy consumption** in HVAC systems (fans and pumps are 60% of industrial motor load globally).
- **Ensuring stability** of high-performance servo drives (CNC machines, robotics).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of the Fundamental Dynamic Equation of Motor-Load System

### Step 1: Newton's Second Law for Rotational Motion

For a body rotating about a fixed axis, the net torque equals the rate of change of angular momentum.

$$\tau_{net} = \frac{d}{dt}(J \cdot \omega)$$

### Step 2: Define Net Torque on the Motor Shaft

The net torque on the combined motor-load rotating mass is the difference between the **motor (electromagnetic) torque** $T$ (driving) and the **load torque** $T_L$ (opposing, including friction).

$$\tau_{net} = T - T_L$$

### Step 3: Assume J is Constant

For a rigid rotor, $J$ does not change with time.

$$\frac{d}{dt}(J \cdot \omega) = J \cdot \frac{d\omega}{dt}$$

### Step 4: Equate and Simplify

$$T - T_L = J \cdot \frac{d\omega}{dt}$$

### Step 5: Add Viscous Friction Term (Bω)

If the load includes viscous friction (which is a function of speed even at steady state), the friction torque must be separated from the net accelerating torque.

$$T - T_L = J \cdot \frac{d\omega}{dt} + B \cdot \omega$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sign Convention Used in KTU:**
> - $T$ = motor (developed electromagnetic) torque — always **positive** (driving)
> - $T_L$ = load torque — **opposes** motion
> - Positive $(T - T_L)$ → acceleration
> - Negative $(T - T_L)$ → deceleration

### Step 6: Convert to N (rpm) Form

Substitute $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$:

$$T - T_L = J \cdot \frac{2\pi}{60} \cdot \frac{dN}{dt} + B \cdot \frac{2\pi N}{60}$$

Final compact form:

$$T - T_L = \frac{J}{9.55} \cdot \frac{dN}{dt} + \frac{B \cdot N}{9.55}$$

## 3.2 Derivation of Steady-State Condition

At **steady state**, the speed is constant, so $\dfrac{d\omega}{dt} = 0$.

Substituting into the fundamental equation:

$$T_{ss} - T_{L,ss} = 0$$

$$\boxed{T_{ss} = T_{L,ss}}$$

**Physical Meaning:** In steady state, the motor torque exactly balances the load torque. The system is in *torque equilibrium*. There is no acceleration.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Conceptual Trap:** The motor current and torque are NOT zero at no-load steady state. The motor still develops enough torque to overcome friction and windage. Only the *net* torque on the shaft is zero.

## 3.3 Stability Analysis of Steady-State Operating Point

### Step 1: Plot Motor and Load Torque-Speed Curves

For a given motor, $T(\omega)$ is a known function (linear, quadratic, or constant depending on motor type). The load torque $T_L(\omega)$ is also a known function. The intersection of these two curves gives the steady-state operating point $(\omega_0, T_0)$.

### Step 2: Perturb the System

Suppose the load torque suddenly increases by a small amount $\Delta T_L$. The system is pushed off the equilibrium. The new speed becomes $\omega_0 + \Delta\omega$.

### Step 3: Calculate Restoring Torque

The change in net torque (the *restoring torque*) is:

$$\Delta T_{net} = \left.\frac{\partial T}{\partial \omega}\right|_{\omega_0} \cdot \Delta\omega - \left.\frac{\partial T_L}{\partial \omega}\right|_{\omega_0} \cdot \Delta\omega$$

$$\Delta T_{net} = \left(\frac{dT}{d\omega} - \frac{dT_L}{d\omega}\right) \cdot \Delta\omega$$

### Step 4: Apply Newton's Equation for the Perturbation

$$J \cdot \frac{d(\Delta\omega)}{dt} = -\left(\frac{dT_L}{d\omega} - \frac{dT}{d\omega}\right) \cdot \Delta\omega$$

(negative sign because the *excess* load torque causes deceleration)

### Step 5: Solve the First-Order ODE

This is of the form $\dfrac{d(\Delta\omega)}{dt} = -k \cdot \Delta\omega$, where

$$k = \frac{1}{J}\left(\frac{dT_L}{d\omega} - \frac{dT}{d\omega}\right)$$

The solution is $\Delta\omega(t) = \Delta\omega(0) \cdot e^{-kt}$.

### Step 6: Stability Condition

For the perturbation to *decay* (i.e., system returns to original point), we need $k > 0$:

$$\frac{dT_L}{d\omega} - \frac{dT}{d\omega} > 0$$

$$\boxed{\frac{dT}{d\omega} < \frac{dT_L}{d\omega}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Stability Rule for KTU 2024:**
> - **Stable Operating Point:** The slope of the motor torque-speed curve is **less** than the slope of the load torque-speed curve at the operating point.
> - **Unstable Operating Point:** The slope of the motor torque-speed curve is **greater** than the slope of the load torque-speed curve.

### Step 7: Practical Example — Induction Motor Driving a Fan

- **Induction motor:** $T \propto s$ (where $s$ = slip). Slope $\dfrac{dT}{d\omega}$ in the operating region is **negative** (as speed increases, torque decreases in the stable region of the curve).
- **Fan load:** $T_L \propto \omega^2$. Slope $\dfrac{dT_L}{d\omega}$ is **positive** (as speed increases, load torque increases).
- Therefore: $\dfrac{dT}{d\omega} < 0 < \dfrac{dT_L}{d\omega}$ → **always stable**.

### Step 8: Practical Example — Shunt Motor Driving a Hoist

- **Shunt motor:** $T \approx \text{constant}$ in the operating region. Slope $\dfrac{dT}{d\omega} \approx 0$ (slightly negative due to armature reaction).
- **Hoist (constant torque load):** $T_L = \text{constant}$. Slope $\dfrac{dT_L}{d\omega} = 0$.
- Both slopes are approximately equal → **marginally stable**. This is why hoist drives often need feedback control (e.g., Ward-Leonard system) to ensure proper speed regulation.

## 3.4 Multi-Load (Equivalent Inertia) Derivation

When the motor and load are coupled through a gear train with ratio $n = \dfrac{N_1}{N_2}$, the inertia must be reflected to a common shaft (say, the motor shaft).

### Step 1: Energy Equivalence

Kinetic energy of load on the load shaft = Kinetic energy of equivalent load on motor shaft.

$$\frac{1}{2} J_L \omega_L^2 = \frac{1}{2} J_{L,eq} \omega_M^2$$

### Step 2: Use Gear Ratio

$$\omega_L = \frac{\omega_M}{n}$$

### Step 3: Solve for $J_{L,eq}$

$$\frac{1}{2} J_L \left(\frac{\omega_M}{n}\right)^2 = \frac{1}{2} J_{L,eq} \omega_M^2$$

$$J_{L,eq} = \frac{J_L}{n^2}$$

### Step 4: Total Equivalent Inertia

$$\boxed{J_{eq} = J_M + \frac{J_L}{n^2}}$$

> **Note on $\vert$ usage:** All modulus bars in formulas above are rendered using LaTeX `\vert` to ensure no markdown table breakage.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Operating Point Scenarios

| Scenario | Motor Type | Load Type | Slope $dT/d\omega$ | Slope $dT_L/d\omega$ | Stability |
|----------|------------|-----------|---------------------|------------------------|-----------|
| 1 | Induction (operating region) | Fan ($\omega^2$) | Negative | Positive | **Stable** |
| 2 | Series DC motor | Fan | Negative | Positive | **Stable** |
| 3 | Shunt DC motor | Hoist (constant $T_L$) | $\approx 0$ | $0$ | **Marginally Stable** |
| 4 | Shunt DC motor (with armature reaction) | Hoist | Negative | $0$ | **Unstable** (if $|slope_M| > |slope_L|$) |
| 5 | Induction (in unstable region, $s > s_{max}$) | Constant $T_L$ | Positive | $0$ | **Unstable** |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block Diagram of an Electric Drive System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Power Source AC or DC] --> A[Power Electronic Converter Rectifier Chopper Inverter]
    A --> B[Electric Motor DC IM PMSM SRM]
    B --> C[Mechanical Coupling Gearbox Belt Drive]
    C --> D[Mechanical Load Fan Pump Hoist Conveyor]
    D --> E[Sensors Speed Torque Position Current]
    E --> F[Controller PID DSP Microcontroller]
    F --> G[Reference Commands Speed Torque Position]
    G --> A
    F -.-> B
```

**Description of Flow:**
- The **Power Source** feeds the **Power Electronic Converter**.
- The converter conditions the electrical power (AC-DC, DC-DC, DC-AC, or AC-AC) and feeds the **Motor**.
- The motor drives the **Mechanical Load** through a **Coupling**.
- **Sensors** measure speed, torque, position, or current from the load and motor.
- The **Controller** compares measured values with **Reference Commands** and adjusts the converter's firing pulses to maintain desired performance.

## 4.2 Torque-Speed Characteristics: Motor vs Load (Steady-State Stability Visual)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Motor Torque Speed Curve] --> C[Operating Point Intersection]
    B[Load Torque Speed Curve] --> C
    C --> D{Stability Check}
    D -->|Slope Motor less than Slope Load| E[STABLE Point]
    D -->|Slope Motor greater than Slope Load| F[UNSTABLE Point]
    D -->|Slope Motor equals Slope Load| G[MARGINALLY STABLE]
    E --> H[System returns to operating point after disturbance]
    F --> I[System diverges from operating point]
    G --> J[System oscillates or drifts slowly]
```

**Description:**
- The **Operating Point** is the intersection of motor and load torque-speed curves.
- The **Stability Check** compares the slopes of both curves at that point.
- **Stable:** System returns to operating point after any small disturbance.
- **Unstable:** System diverges — speed runs away or stalls.
- **Marginally Stable:** System oscillates around the operating point.

## 4.3 Load Torque Classifications (Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Total Load Torque TL] --> B[Friction Torque]
    A --> C[Windage Torque]
    A --> D[Core Loss Torque]
    A --> E[Work Torque Useful Mechanical Output]
    A --> F[Inertial Torque Transient Only]
    B --> B1[Coulomb Dry Friction Speed Independent]
    B --> B2[Viscous Friction Proportional to omega]
    C --> C1[Proportional to omega squared]
    D --> D1[Proportional to omega squared for AC machines]
    E --> E1[Constant Torque Hoist Conveyor]
    E --> E2[Linear Torque Mixer]
    E --> E3[Parabolic Torque Fan Pump]
    E --> E4[Hyperbolic Torque Rolling Mill]
    F --> F1[Equals J d omega dt]
```

**Description:**
The total load torque is decomposed into its physical constituents. The **Inertial Torque** is the only one that vanishes at steady state.

## 4.4 Four-Quadrant Operation of an Electric Drive

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Quadrant I Forward Motoring] --> B[Quadrant II Forward Braking Regenerative]
    B --> C[Quadrant III Reverse Motoring]
    C --> D[Quadrant IV Reverse Braking Regenerative]
    D --> A
    A1[omega greater than 0, T greater than 0] -.-> A
    B1[omega greater than 0, T less than 0] -.-> B
    C1[omega less than 0, T less than 0] -.-> C
    D1[omega less than 0, T greater than 0] -.-> D
```

**Description:**
- **Q1 (Forward Motoring):** Positive speed, positive torque — normal operation
- **Q2 (Forward Braking):** Positive speed, negative torque — plugging or regenerative braking
- **Q3 (Reverse Motoring):** Negative speed, negative torque — reverse running
- **Q4 (Reverse Braking):** Negative speed, positive torque — reverse braking
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A: Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks = 6 Marks)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Define an electric drive. List any four advantages of adjustable speed electric drives.**

**Model Answer:**

An **electric drive** is a system that controls the motion of a mechanical load by regulating the speed, torque, and position of an electric motor using power electronic converters and feedback control.

**Four Advantages of Adjustable Speed Drives:**
1. **Energy savings** — significant (up to 60% in fan/pump applications) compared to fixed-speed drives with throttling.
2. **Soft starting** — eliminates high inrush currents (6–8 times rated), reducing stress on motor and supply.
3. **Improved process control** — precise speed/torque regulation improves product quality.
4. **Reduced maintenance** — fewer mechanical parts, smooth acceleration reduces wear.

*(Any 4 valid advantages accepted; 1 mark for definition + 0.5 mark each for advantages.)*

### Question 2 (3 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Derive the steady-state stability condition for a motor-load system.**

**Model Answer:**

At steady state, $T = T_L$. For small perturbation $\Delta\omega$ around operating point:

$$J \cdot \frac{d(\Delta\omega)}{dt} = -\left(\frac{dT_L}{d\omega} - \frac{dT}{d\omega}\right) \cdot \Delta\omega$$

For stability, the coefficient must be negative (so perturbation decays):

$$\frac{dT}{d\omega} < \frac{dT_L}{d\omega}$$

*(1 mark for setting up the equation, 1 mark for solution, 1 mark for final condition with interpretation.)*

## 5.2 PART B: Long Answer Questions (Choice-Based, 14 Marks)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Part (a) (7 Marks):**
With the help of a block diagram, explain the functional parts of an electric drive system. Discuss the role of the power electronic converter in detail.

**Model Answer:**

The block diagram of an electric drive has the following functional blocks:

1. **Power Source:** Provides electrical energy — AC mains (3-phase, 50 Hz), DC battery, or renewable source.
2. **Power Electronic Converter:** Conditions the power to the required form (AC-DC, DC-DC, AC-AC, DC-AC).
3. **Electric Motor:** Converts electrical to mechanical energy (DC, IM, PMSM, SRM, etc.).
4. **Mechanical Load:** The driven equipment (pump, fan, conveyor, hoist).
5. **Sensors:** Measure speed, torque, position, current — provide feedback signals.
6. **Controller:** Compares feedback with reference, generates firing pulses for converter (PID/DSP/MCU).

**Role of Power Electronic Converter (in detail):**

The power electronic converter is the heart of the modern adjustable speed drive. Its functions are:

(i) **Conversion of Power Form:**
- For DC motor: AC → DC (rectifier) or DC → DC (chopper)
- For AC motor: AC → DC → AC (inverter) — V/f control, vector control, DTC
- For low-speed high-power: AC → AC (cycloconverter)

(ii) **Control of Magnitude and Frequency:**
- Varying DC link voltage controls speed of DC motor
- Varying frequency and voltage together (V/f = constant) controls speed of induction motor

(iii) **Four-Quadrant Operation:**
- Forward motoring, forward braking, reverse motoring, reverse braking
- Achieved through anti-parallel converters or IGBT modules

(iv) **Protection and Soft Starting:**
- Current limit, overvoltage protection
- Gradual voltage/frequency ramp prevents inrush

(v) **Regeneration:**
- During braking, motor acts as generator; converter feeds energy back to supply (active front-end)

**Block Diagram:**

```
[Power Source] → [Power Converter] → [Motor] → [Mechanical Load]
        ↑                                          ↓
        |                                    [Sensors]
        |                                          ↓
        ←——————[Controller] ←———————— [Reference]
```

*(4 marks for block diagram + explanation, 3 marks for converter role with examples.)*

---

**Part (b) (7 Marks):**
Classify the different types of loads encountered in electric drives. Explain the torque-speed characteristics of each with suitable examples.

**Model Answer:**

Loads are classified into **passive** and **active** types.

### Passive Loads:

| Type | Torque Equation | Examples |
|------|----------------|----------|
| **Constant Torque** | $T_L = K$ | Hoist, conveyor belt, compressor, extruder |
| **Linear Torque (Viscous)** | $T_L = B\omega$ | Mixers, dampers |
| **Parabolic (Square Law)** | $T_L = K\omega^2$ | Fans, centrifugal pumps, blowers |
| **Inverse Parabolic (Constant Power)** | $T_L = P/\omega$ | Rolling mills, machine tool spindles, coil winders |

### Active Loads:

Load torque can be **assisting** or **opposing** depending on direction. Energy flows two-way.
- Example: **Hoist lifting** (gravity opposes), **Hoist lowering** (gravity assists)
- Example: **Locomotive on incline** (gravity component adds or subtracts)

**Special Load: Impact Loads**
- Sudden, repetitive torque peaks
- Example: Punch press, hammer mill, rock crusher
- Requires oversized motor or flywheel

**Special Load: Pulsating Loads**
- Periodic torque variation
- Example: Reciprocating compressor, weaving loom
- Requires flywheel for smoothing

**Torque-Speed Curves (Graphical):**
- Constant torque: Horizontal line
- Linear: Straight line through origin
- Parabolic: Quadratic curve
- Hyperbolic: $P = \text{constant}$ curve (decreasing)

*(3 marks for classification table, 2 marks for active loads, 2 marks for special loads.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Part (a) (7 Marks):**
Derive the fundamental dynamic equation of a motor-load system. Explain the significance of each term.

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1:** Apply Newton's second law for rotation.

$$\tau_{net} = \frac{d}{dt}(J\omega)$$

**Step 2:** For a rigid body, $J$ = constant, so

$$\tau_{net} = J\frac{d\omega}{dt}$$

**Step 3:** Net torque on the shaft = motor torque - load torque:

$$T - T_L = J\frac{d\omega}{dt}$$

**Step 4:** Adding viscous friction $B\omega$ (which is part of load torque but always present at speed):

$$T - T_L = J\frac{d\omega}{dt} + B\omega$$

**Step 5:** Converting to N (rpm) using $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$:

$$T - T_L = \frac{J}{9.55}\frac{dN}{dt} + \frac{B \cdot N}{9.55}$$

**Significance of Each Term:**

- **$T$ (Motor Electromagnetic Torque):** Developed by the motor due to electromagnetic interaction; acts as the driving torque.
- **$T_L$ (Total Load Torque):** Sum of friction, windage, core losses, and useful work torque; opposes motion.
- **$J\dfrac{d\omega}{dt}$ (Inertial Torque):** Appears only during speed change. Zero at steady state. Determines acceleration.
- **$B\omega$ (Viscous Friction Torque):** Speed-dependent friction, present at all speeds (including steady state).

**Special Cases:**

- **Steady state:** $\dfrac{d\omega}{dt} = 0$ → $T = T_L$ (torque balance)
- **Acceleration:** $T > T_L$ → positive $d\omega/dt$
- **Deceleration:** $T < T_L$ → negative $d\omega/dt$

*(3 marks for derivation, 2 marks for term significance, 2 marks for special cases.)*

---

**Part (b) (7 Marks):**
What is steady-state stability? Explain with a suitable example how the slopes of motor and load torque-speed curves determine whether an operating point is stable or unstable.

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** A motor-load system is in **steady-state stability** at an operating point if, after a small disturbance in speed, the system returns to the original operating point.

**Mathematical Condition:**

At the operating point, the small-signal equation is:

$$J \frac{d(\Delta\omega)}{dt} = -\left(\frac{dT_L}{d\omega} - \frac{dT}{d\omega}\right) \Delta\omega$$

For perturbation to decay: $\dfrac{dT}{d\omega} < \dfrac{dT_L}{d\omega}$

**Stability Rules:**
- **Stable:** $\dfrac{dT}{d\omega} < \dfrac{dT_L}{d\omega}$
- **Unstable:** $\dfrac{dT}{d\omega} > \dfrac{dT_L}{d\omega}$
- **Marginally Stable:** $\dfrac{dT}{d\omega} = \dfrac{dT_L}{d\omega}$

**Example 1: Induction Motor + Fan Load (STABLE)**
- Induction motor in operating region: $\dfrac{dT}{d\omega} < 0$ (negative slope — torque decreases as speed increases)
- Fan load: $\dfrac{dT_L}{d\omega} > 0$ (positive slope — load torque increases as speed increases)
- Since (negative) < (positive) → **STABLE**

**Example 2: Shunt DC Motor + Hoist Load (MARGINALLY STABLE)**
- Shunt motor: $\dfrac{dT}{d\omega} \approx 0$ (flat characteristic)
- Hoist: $\dfrac{dT_L}{d\omega} = 0$ (constant torque)
- Slopes equal → **MARGINALLY STABLE** — small disturbance persists; hence feedback control is required.

**Example 3: Shunt DC Motor with Strong Armature Reaction + Hoist (UNSTABLE)**
- Shunt motor with armature reaction: $\dfrac{dT}{d\omega} < 0$ but with large magnitude
- Hoist: $\dfrac{dT_L}{d\omega} = 0$
- If magnitude of negative motor slope > 0 → unstable
- This is why series field or stabilizing winding is added.

**Graphical Method:**
- Plot both curves
- Find intersection
- Draw tangents at intersection
- Compare slopes — if motor slope is less than load slope → stable

*(2 marks for definition + condition, 3 marks for examples, 2 marks for graphical method.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Confusing motor torque with shaft torque.** Motor torque is electromagnetic torque developed inside the motor; shaft torque is what's available at the load after mechanical losses. Always use motor torque in the dynamic equation.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the inertia reflection formula.** If the load and motor are on different shafts (gear-coupled), you MUST reflect the load inertia to the motor shaft: $J_{eq} = J_M + \dfrac{J_L}{n^2}$. Marks are lost for not doing this.
>
> 3. **Wrong stability inequality sign.** Many students write $\dfrac{dT}{d\omega} > \dfrac{dT_L}{d\omega}$ for stability. This is **WRONG**. The correct condition is $\dfrac{dT}{d\omega} < \dfrac{dT_L}{d\omega}$.
>
> 4. **Assuming inertial torque exists at steady state.** It does NOT. At steady state, $\dfrac{d\omega}{dt} = 0$, so $J\dfrac{d\omega}{dt} = 0$.
>
> 5. **Unit confusion.** Use $J$ in kg·m², $\omega$ in rad/s, $N$ in rpm, $T$ in N·m. Mixing units (e.g., J in kg·cm², ω in rpm) without conversion is a common 1-mark killer.
>
> 6. **Forgetting the $9.55$ conversion factor** when using N (rpm): $T = \dfrac{J \cdot 2\pi}{60} \cdot \dfrac{dN}{dt} = \dfrac{J}{9.55} \cdot \dfrac{dN}{dt}$
>
> 7. **Not labeling the block diagram.** Every block in the block diagram must be labeled. Drawing without labels fetches zero marks.

---

> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Points for KTU 2024 Exam:**

- [ ] **Electric Drive** = Power Source + Power Converter + Motor + Mechanical Load + Controller + Sensors
- [ ] **Top 3 Advantages of ASD:** Energy saving, Soft starting, Improved process control
- [ ] **Fundamental Dynamic Equation:** $T - T_L = J\dfrac{d\omega}{dt} + B\omega$
- [ ] **Steady-State Condition:** $T = T_L$ (motor torque balances load torque)
- [ ] **Inertial torque vanishes at steady state** (only present during acceleration/deceleration)
- [ ] **Load Inertia Reflection:** $J_{eq} = J_M + \dfrac{J_L}{n^2}$ (n = gear ratio)
- [ ] **Conversion:** $T = \dfrac{J}{9.55} \cdot \dfrac{dN}{dt}$ (when N in rpm)
- [ ] **Load Types:** Constant $T_L$ (hoist), Linear (mixer), Parabolic (fan), Hyperbolic (rolling mill)
- [ ] **Active loads** = can assist or oppose (e.g., gravity in hoist)
- [ ] **Stability Condition:** $\dfrac{dT}{d\omega} < \dfrac{dT_L}{d\omega}$ at the operating point
- [ ] **Stable Example:** Induction motor + fan (negative slope < positive slope) ✓
- [ ] **Unstable Example:** Motor in unstable region + constant $T_L$ load ✗
- [ ] **Power electronic converter role:** Power form conversion + magnitude/frequency control + 4-quadrant operation
- [ ] **Key converters:** Rectifier (AC-DC), Chopper (DC-DC), Inverter (DC-AC), Cycloconverter (AC-AC)
- [ ] **Energy savings in fans/pumps:** 30–60% with ASD vs throttle/damper control
- [ ] **Inrush current of DOL starter:** 6 to 8 times rated current (eliminated by ASD)
- [ ] **Most common ASD topology:** AC-DC-AC (rectifier + inverter) — used in VFDs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 4 — DC Drives: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a DC Drive?

A **DC Drive** is an electronic system that precisely controls the speed, torque, and direction of a DC motor by varying the voltage/current applied to its armature and/or field. In KTU 2024 parlance, we focus on the **Separately Excited DC Motor (SEDC)** — a motor whose field winding is fed from an independent, usually constant, DC source while the armature is fed from a *variable* power-electronic converter.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why SEDC and not Series/Shunt?** 
> In a separately excited machine, the field current $I_f$ is *independent* of the armature current $I_a$. This means the flux $\Phi$ is decoupled from load, making **linear control** of speed and torque trivially simple — exactly what examiners love to test.

## 1.2 The Three Power-Electronic "Feeding" Topologies

The **SEDC drive can be fed by any of the following**, each with its own quadrant capability:

| Topology | Power Source | Quadrant Operation | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Chopper (DC–DC)** | Fixed DC bus | 1Q, 2Q, 4Q | Battery EVs, traction, robotics |
| **1-Phase Controlled Rectifier** | AC mains | 1Q (semi) / 2Q (full) | Small fans, pumps |
| **3-Phase Controlled Rectifier** | AC mains | 1Q / 4Q (dual) | Industrial drives up to MW |
| **Dual Converter (1-Phase)** | AC mains | True **4-Quadrant** | Reversible rolling mills, hoists |

## 1.3 The Four Quadrants of Operation

This is the **single most tested concept** in this module. Every KTU examiner will draw a torque-speed plane and expect you to identify quadrants.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Q I} &\rightarrow +\omega, +T \;\; \text{(Forward Motoring)} \\
\text{Q II} &\rightarrow +\omega, -T \;\; \text{(Forward Braking / Regeneration)} \\
\text{Q III} &\rightarrow -\omega, -T \;\; \text{(Reverse Motoring)} \\
\text{Q IV} &\rightarrow -\omega, +T \;\; \text{(Reverse Braking / Regeneration)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The Car & Accelerator**
> Imagine driving a manual car:
> - **Quadrant I** = Pressing accelerator forward (positive torque, positive speed) — motoring ahead.
> - **Quadrant II** = Coasting downhill in gear; engine acts as a brake but you still move forward — *regenerative braking*.
> - **Quadrant III** = Pressing reverse accelerator — going backward.
> - **Quadrant IV** = Moving backward downhill while pressing forward accelerator to slow down — reverse regen.
> A **4-quadrant drive** is essentially an "electronic gearbox" that handles all four situations seamlessly.

## 1.4 Key Machine Equations You Must Memorize

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Back EMF: } & E_b = K \Phi \omega \\
\text{Armature Voltage: } & V_a = E_b + I_a R_a \\
\text{Torque: } & T = K \Phi I_a \\
\text{Speed (rad/s): } & \omega = \dfrac{V_a - I_a R_a}{K \Phi} \\
\text{Speed (rpm): } & N = \dfrac{V_a - I_a R_a}{K \Phi} \cdot \dfrac{60}{2\pi}
\end{aligned}
$$

Where $K$ is the machine construction constant, $\Phi$ is the field flux (held constant in armature-control mode), and $R_a$ is the armature resistance.

## 1.5 Why Continuous Conduction Only?

KTU explicitly restricts you to **Continuous Conduction Mode (CCM)** — meaning the armature current $I_a$ is assumed to be **always positive (or always flowing)**, never discontinuous. This eliminates ripple analysis and lets the examiners set clean numerical problems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** In CCM, average values govern behavior. $V_a$ refers to the *average* armature voltage, and $E_b$ is *constant* (since $\omega$ is constant in steady state). This is your licence to use $V_a = \alpha V_s$ for choppers and $V_a = V_{m0}\cos\alpha$ for controlled rectifiers without worrying about ripple.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Module 4 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Chopper-Fed SEDC Drives (DC–DC Conversion)

A **chopper** is a high-speed electronic switch (IGBT/MOSFET) that chops a fixed DC bus voltage $V_s$ into a variable average voltage $V_a = \alpha V_s$ where $\alpha$ = duty cycle = $T_{on}/T_{total}$.

### 2.1.1 One-Quadrant Chopper (Class A)

**Circuit:** Single chopper switch $T$ in series with the motor, freewheeling diode $D_F$ across the armature.

**Operation Cycle:**
1. **$T$ ON** ($T_{on} = \alpha T$): Armature is connected to $V_s$. Current rises through $L_a$.
2. **$T$ OFF** ($T_{off} = (1-\alpha)T$): Armature is disconnected; $D_F$ freewheels the inductive current, clamping $V_a \approx 0$.

$$
V_{a,avg} = \alpha V_s \quad ; \quad I_{a,avg} = \dfrac{T_L}{K\Phi} \quad ; \quad \omega = \dfrac{\alpha V_s - I_a R_a}{K\Phi}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Quadrant I ONLY.** Since $V_a \geq 0$ and $I_a \geq 0$ (freewheeling diode forbids negative current), the drive **can only motor in forward direction**. No regeneration, no reverse.

### 2.1.2 Two-Quadrant Chopper (Class C — Symmetric)

**Circuit:** Two chopper switches $T_1, T_2$ in a half-bridge across the armature, with diodes $D_1, D_2$.

**Two operating modes:**

- **Mode 1 (Motoring, $T_1$ chopped, $T_2$ OFF):** $V_a = +\alpha V_s$, current positive → **Quadrant I**.
- **Mode 2 (Regeneration, $T_2$ chopped, $T_1$ OFF):** $V_a = -(1-\alpha)V_s$, but the *back EMF* drives current *back into the source*. The motor returns energy → **Quadrant II**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** In Quadrant II, the *speed* $\omega$ is still positive, but the *torque* is negative (because $I_a$ is reversed). The motor acts as a **generator** and pushes current back to the DC bus — this is **regenerative braking**.

### 2.1.3 Four-Quadrant Chopper (H-Bridge / Class D)

**Circuit:** Full H-bridge with $T_1, T_2, T_3, T_4$ and diodes $D_1 \ldots D_4$. The armature sees either $+V_s$, $-V_s$, or $0$.

$$
V_a = \begin{cases} +V_s & (T_1, T_4 \text{ ON}) \\ -V_s & (T_2, T_3 \text{ ON}) \\ 0 & (T_1, D_2 \text{ or } T_3, D_4) \end{cases}
$$

**PWM Strategy:** Two reference signals, $+V_{control}$ and $-V_{control}$, are compared with a triangular carrier. This naturally generates four-quadrant operation with $V_{a,avg} = \alpha V_s$ where $\alpha$ can be **negative** (reverse motoring) or **positive** (forward motoring).

## 2.2 Speed-Torque Characteristics of Chopper-Fed SEDC

Starting from the steady-state equations:

$$
\omega = \dfrac{V_a - I_a R_a}{K\Phi} = \dfrac{V_a}{K\Phi} - \dfrac{R_a}{(K\Phi)^2} \cdot T
$$

This is a **straight line** in the $\omega$-$T$ plane with:
- **No-load speed** $\omega_0 = V_a / (K\Phi)$
- **Slope** $= -R_a/(K\Phi)^2$ (very small in CCM, since $R_a$ is small)
- **Stalling torque** $T_{st} = V_a \cdot K\Phi / R_a$

$$
\boxed{\;\omega = \omega_0 - \dfrac{R_a}{(K\Phi)^2} \cdot T\;}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** Vary $\alpha \to V_a$ changes. Each value of $\alpha$ gives a *family* of parallel lines. Below the base speed, $\Phi$ is held constant, so all lines have the same slope. This is the **armature control region**.

## 2.3 Controlled Rectifier–Fed SEDC Drives (AC–DC Conversion)

When the source is AC, we use a **phase-controlled rectifier** (SCR-based) instead of a chopper.

### 2.3.1 Single-Phase Fully-Controlled Rectifier

For a single-phase full converter with **highly inductive load** (continuous $I_a$):

$$
V_{a,avg} = \dfrac{2 V_m}{\pi} \cos \alpha = \dfrac{2 \sqrt{2}\, V_{rms}}{\pi} \cos \alpha
$$

where $V_m$ is the peak AC voltage, $V_{rms}$ is the RMS line voltage, and $\alpha$ is the firing angle.

**Speed Equation:**

$$
\omega = \dfrac{1}{K\Phi}\left[\dfrac{2 V_m}{\pi}\cos\alpha - I_a R_a\right]
$$

**Quadrant Capability:**
- **Semi-converter (half-controlled):** 1 quadrant (forward motoring only).
- **Full converter with $D_F$:** Forward motoring (Q I) + forward regen (Q II) using **inverter mode** of operation ($\alpha > 90°$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Regenerative Braking in Rectifier Mode:** The same SCR bridge that fed the motor as a rectifier ($\alpha < 90°$) can act as a **line-commutated inverter** when $\alpha > 90°$. The motor's back EMF now exceeds $V_a$ in magnitude, current reverses, and energy is pumped back to the AC mains. This is **regenerative braking**.

### 2.3.2 Three-Phase Fully-Controlled Rectifier

For a 3-phase full converter feeding a highly inductive load:

$$
V_{a,avg} = \dfrac{3 V_{mL}}{\pi} \cos \alpha = \dfrac{3 \sqrt{2}\, V_{LL,rms}}{\pi} \cos \alpha
$$

where $V_{mL}$ is the peak line-to-line voltage and $V_{LL,rms}$ is the RMS line voltage.

**Firing Range:** $0° \leq \alpha \leq 90°$ for rectification, $90° < \alpha \leq 180°$ for inversion.

**Speed-Torque Characteristic:** Same straight-line equation as before, but $V_a$ is now controlled by $\alpha$ via the cosine law. Higher $\alpha$ → lower $V_a$ → lower $\omega_0$.

## 2.4 Dual Converter — True Four-Quadrant Operation

A **dual converter** is **two fully-controlled rectifiers connected in anti-parallel** across the motor armature:
- **Converter 1 (P-converter / Forward):** Delivers positive $V_a$, positive $I_a$ (Q I, Q IV).
- **Converter 2 (N-converter / Reverse):** Delivers negative $V_a$, negative $I_a$ (Q III, Q II).

### 2.4.1 Non-Circulating Current Mode (Logical Firing)

- **Only one converter fires at a time.** When the motor must transition from Q I to Q III, Converter 1 is shut off, a brief dead-time (a few ms) is allowed for SCR commutation, then Converter 2 is gated.
- **No circulating current** between converters — saves losses, but introduces a **dead time** where neither converter conducts.
- $V_a$ can be either polarity; $I_a$ cannot change instantaneously (limited by $L_a$).

### 2.4.2 Circulating Current Mode (Simultaneous Firing)

- **Both converters fire simultaneously.** Converter 1 is set to $V_{a1} = V_0 \cos\alpha_1$ (positive), Converter 2 to $V_{a2} = -V_0 \cos\alpha_2$ (negative), with the constraint $\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 = 180°$ so that the **EMFs are equal and opposite** → net voltage across the inter-group reactor = 0, but a small **circulating current** flows limited by the reactor inductance $L_C$.
- **No dead time** → instantaneous four-quadrant response.
- **Disadvantage:** Higher losses, larger reactor, more complex control.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** 
> - **Non-circulating** = a manual gearbox — must disengage one gear before engaging the other (small delay).
> - **Circulating** = a dual-clutch automatic — both clutches are always ready, switching is instantaneous.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

| **Parameter** | **Chopper (DC–DC)** | **1-Phase Full Converter** | **3-Phase Full Converter** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Average $V_a$ | $\alpha V_s$ | $\dfrac{2V_m}{\pi}\cos\alpha$ | $\dfrac{3V_{mL}}{\pi}\cos\alpha$ |
| Rectification range | $\alpha \in [0,1]$ | $\alpha \in [0°, 90°]$ | $\alpha \in [0°, 90°]$ |
| Inversion range | (via topology swap) | $\alpha \in (90°, 180°]$ | $\alpha \in (90°, 180°]$ |
| No-load speed $\omega_0$ | $\dfrac{\alpha V_s}{K\Phi}$ | $\dfrac{2 V_m \cos\alpha}{\pi K\Phi}$ | $\dfrac{3 V_{mL} \cos\alpha}{\pi K\Phi}$ |
| Slope of $\omega$-$T$ line | $-\dfrac{R_a}{(K\Phi)^2}$ | $-\dfrac{R_a}{(K\Phi)^2}$ | $-\dfrac{R_a}{(K\Phi)^2}$ |
| Quadrant capability | 1Q / 2Q / 4Q | 2Q (full converter) | 2Q (single bridge) |
| Regen method | Reverse $V_a$ polarity | $\alpha > 90°$ (inversion) | $\alpha > 90°$ (inversion) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often write $V_a = \alpha V_s$ for rectifiers. **No!** For rectifiers, $V_a \propto \cos\alpha$, not $\alpha$ itself.

## 2.6 Why These Systems Matter in Engineering

- **Chopper drives** dominate **electric vehicles (EVs)** because they run off a fixed-voltage battery bus. The Toyota Prius, Tesla Model S, and most e-bikes use chopper-fed SEDC or BLDC drives.
- **3-Phase rectifier drives** are workhorses in **steel rolling mills, paper machines, and mine hoists** where high power (MW range) and AC mains are available.
- **Dual converter drives** appear in **reversible rolling mills** and **machine tool spindles** where rapid Q I ↔ Q III transitions are required.
- **Regenerative braking** in chopper drives recovers kinetic energy during deceleration — critical for **hybrid vehicles and electric trains** (regen extends range by 10–30%).

# Module 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Speed–Torque Equation of SEDC Under Armature Control

**Starting point:** Kirchhoff's Voltage Law around the armature loop in steady state.

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_a &= E_b + I_a R_a \\
\Rightarrow V_a &= K \Phi \omega + I_a R_a
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1:** Solve for angular speed $\omega$:

$$
\omega = \dfrac{V_a - I_a R_a}{K \Phi}
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute $I_a = T / (K \Phi)$ from the torque equation:

$$
\omega = \dfrac{V_a - \left(\dfrac{T}{K \Phi}\right) R_a}{K \Phi} = \dfrac{V_a}{K \Phi} - \dfrac{R_a}{(K \Phi)^2} \cdot T
$$

**Step 3:** Identify the intercept and slope:
- **No-load speed** (at $T = 0$): $\omega_0 = V_a / (K \Phi)$
- **Slope:** $m = -R_a / (K \Phi)^2$
- **Stall torque** (at $\omega = 0$): $T_{st} = V_a \cdot K \Phi / R_a$

$$
\boxed{\;\omega = \omega_0 \left[1 - \dfrac{T}{T_{st}}\right]\;}
$$

This is the **master equation** for every chopper-fed and rectifier-fed SEDC problem. Memorize it.

## 3.2 Derivation: Voltage Transfer Ratio of a 1-Phase Full Converter

The instantaneous output voltage of a single-phase full bridge firing at angle $\alpha$ into a highly inductive load is the **absolute value of the mains voltage**, shifted in time. Its average over one cycle is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{a,avg} &= \dfrac{1}{\pi} \int_{\alpha}^{\alpha + \pi} V_m \sin\theta \, d\theta \\
&= \dfrac{V_m}{\pi} \left[-\cos\theta\right]_{\alpha}^{\alpha + \pi} \\
&= \dfrac{V_m}{\pi}\left[-\cos(\alpha + \pi) + \cos\alpha\right] \\
&= \dfrac{V_m}{\pi}\left[\cos\alpha + \cos\alpha\right] \quad (\because -\cos(\alpha+\pi) = \cos\alpha) \\
&= \dfrac{2 V_m}{\pi} \cos\alpha
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical meaning:** $\cos\alpha$ is called the **rectification ratio**. At $\alpha = 0°$, $V_a = 2V_m/\pi$ (maximum); at $\alpha = 90°$, $V_a = 0$ (transition between motoring and regen); at $\alpha = 180°$, $V_a = -2V_m/\pi$ (maximum inversion).

## 3.3 Derivation: Voltage Transfer Ratio of a 3-Phase Full Converter

For a 3-phase full bridge, the output is the **maximum** of the three line-to-line voltages. Over one cycle ($60°$ segment), the average of one such segment gives:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{a,avg} &= \dfrac{3}{\pi} \int_{\pi/6 + \alpha}^{\pi/2 + \alpha} V_{mL} \sin\theta \, d\theta \\
&= \dfrac{3 V_{mL}}{\pi} \left[-\cos\theta\right]_{\pi/6 + \alpha}^{\pi/2 + \alpha} \\
&= \dfrac{3 V_{mL}}{\pi} \left[\cos\!\left(\tfrac{\pi}{6}+\alpha\right) - \cos\!\left(\tfrac{\pi}{2}+\alpha\right)\right] \\
&= \dfrac{3 V_{mL}}{\pi} \left[\cos\!\left(\tfrac{\pi}{6}+\alpha\right) + \sin\alpha\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

Using the identity $\cos(\pi/6 + \alpha) = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\cos\alpha - \frac{1}{2}\sin\alpha$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{a,avg} &= \dfrac{3 V_{mL}}{\pi} \left[\tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\cos\alpha - \tfrac{1}{2}\sin\alpha + \sin\alpha\right] \\
&= \dfrac{3 V_{mL}}{\pi} \left[\tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\cos\alpha + \tfrac{1}{2}\sin\alpha\right] \\
&= \dfrac{3 V_{mL}}{\pi} \left[\cos 30° \cos\alpha + \sin 30° \sin\alpha\right] \\
&= \dfrac{3 V_{mL}}{\pi} \cos(\alpha - 30°)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Equivalent compact form (commonly used in textbooks):**

$$
V_{a,avg} = \dfrac{3 \sqrt{2}\, V_{LL,rms}}{\pi}\cos\alpha
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on the angle reference:** Some textbooks define $\alpha$ from the *natural commutation point* of the *phase* voltage (i.e., shift $\alpha \to \alpha - 30°$). The two forms are equivalent — but **be consistent** with your reference in the exam.

## 3.4 Derivation: Condition for Continuous Conduction (Boundary $I_a$)

In CCM, the average armature current must exceed a critical value. For a chopper:

$$
I_{a,crit} = \dfrac{V_s (1 - \alpha)}{2 L_a f} = \dfrac{V_s - V_a}{2 L_a f}
$$

**Derivation sketch:**

During $T_{on}$, the current rises as:

$$
\Delta I_{rise} = \dfrac{(V_s - E_b)}{L_a} \cdot \alpha T
$$

During $T_{off}$, the current falls as:

$$
\Delta I_{fall} = \dfrac{E_b}{L_a} \cdot (1-\alpha)T
$$

At the boundary, peak-to-peak ripple = twice the average. Setting $\Delta I_{rise} = I_{a,avg}$ and equating to $\Delta I_{fall}$:

$$
\dfrac{(V_s - E_b)\alpha T}{L_a} = \dfrac{E_b (1-\alpha) T}{L_a} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; E_b = \alpha V_s
$$

Then:

$$
I_{a,crit} = \dfrac{(V_s - E_b)\alpha T}{2 L_a} = \dfrac{(1-\alpha)V_s \alpha T}{2 L_a} = \dfrac{V_s \alpha(1-\alpha)}{2 L_a f}
$$

## 3.5 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style 14-Mark Problem)

> **Problem:** A 5 HP, 220 V, 1500 rpm, separately excited DC motor has $R_a = 0.5\,\Omega$ and field constant $K\Phi = 1.2\,\text{V·s/rad}$. The motor is fed from a single-phase fully-controlled rectifier with $V_s = 230\,\text{V (rms)}$, 50 Hz. For a firing angle $\alpha = 45°$ and load torque $T_L = 20\,\text{Nm}$, find:
> (a) Average armature voltage and current.
> (b) Motor speed in rpm.
> (c) The firing angle needed to run the motor at 1000 rpm at the same load torque.

**Solution:**

**Part (a):** For a 1-phase full converter:

$$
V_{a,avg} = \dfrac{2 V_m}{\pi} \cos\alpha = \dfrac{2 \cdot \sqrt{2} \cdot 230}{\pi} \cos 45° = \dfrac{650.5}{\pi} \cdot 0.7071 \approx 146.4\,\text{V}
$$

$$
I_{a,avg} = \dfrac{T_L}{K\Phi} = \dfrac{20}{1.2} = 16.67\,\text{A}
$$

**Part (b):**

$$
\omega = \dfrac{V_a - I_a R_a}{K\Phi} = \dfrac{146.4 - 16.67 \cdot 0.5}{1.2} = \dfrac{146.4 - 8.33}{1.2} = \dfrac{138.07}{1.2} \approx 115.06\,\text{rad/s}
$$

Converting to rpm:

$$
N = \omega \cdot \dfrac{60}{2\pi} = 115.06 \cdot 9.549 \approx 1098.7\,\text{rpm}
$$

**Part (c):** For $N = 1000\,\text{rpm}$:

$$
\omega_{new} = 1000 \cdot \dfrac{2\pi}{60} = 104.72\,\text{rad/s}
$$

Required back EMF:

$$
E_b = K\Phi \cdot \omega = 1.2 \cdot 104.72 = 125.66\,\text{V}
$$

Required armature voltage:

$$
V_a = E_b + I_a R_a = 125.66 + 16.67 \cdot 0.5 = 134.0\,\text{V}
$$

Solving for $\alpha$:

$$
\cos\alpha = \dfrac{V_a \pi}{2 V_m} = \dfrac{134 \cdot \pi}{2 \cdot \sqrt{2} \cdot 230} = \dfrac{420.97}{650.5} \approx 0.6472
$$

$$
\alpha = \cos^{-1}(0.6472) \approx 49.66°
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verification:** As $\alpha$ increases (from 45° → 49.66°), $V_a$ decreases, hence speed drops. ✓ Physically consistent.

## 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix: All Four Topologies

| **Property** | **1-Q Chopper** | **2-Q Chopper** | **4-Q Chopper (H-Bridge)** | **1-Phase Full Converter** | **Dual Converter** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $V_{a,avg}$ formula | $\alpha V_s$ | $\pm(1-\alpha)V_s$ | $\alpha V_s$ (signed) | $\tfrac{2V_m}{\pi}\cos\alpha$ | $\pm\tfrac{2V_m}{\pi}\cos\alpha$ |
| $I_a$ sign | Always + | Either | Either | Always + (full converter with $D_F$ forces $I_a \geq 0$) | Either |
| Q I (Fwd Motoring) | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |
| Q II (Fwd Regen) | ✗ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ (via inversion) | ✓ |
| Q III (Rev Motoring) | ✗ | ✗ | ✓ | ✗ | ✓ |
| Q IV (Rev Regen) | ✗ | ✗ | ✓ | ✗ | ✓ |
| Switching device | IGBT/MOSFET | IGBT/MOSFET | 4× IGBT | SCR (thyristor) | 2× SCR bridges |
| Switching frequency | High (kHz) | High (kHz) | High (kHz) | Low (mains sync) | Low (mains sync) |
| Source | DC bus | DC bus | DC bus | AC mains | AC mains |
| Use case | DC microgrids, EVs | Battery traction | Robotics, EVs | Small pumps/fans | Rolling mills, hoists |
