# Module 4 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2023]**
**Define a four-quadrant chopper-fed DC drive. List the switching combinations for motoring and braking in both directions.**

**Model Answer (10 lines):**
A four-quadrant chopper-fed DC drive uses an H-bridge configuration to apply $+V_s$, $-V_s$, or $0$ across the armature, allowing operation in all four quadrants of the torque–speed plane.

| **Mode** | **$T_1$** | **$T_2$** | **$T_3$** | **$T_4$** | **Quadrant** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Forward Motoring | ON | OFF | OFF | ON | I |
| Forward Regen | OFF | ON | ON | OFF | II |
| Reverse Motoring | OFF | ON | ON | OFF | III |
| Reverse Regen | ON | OFF | OFF | ON | IV |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often state that "all four switches are ON" or "all four are OFF" in some mode. **Never** — this would short-circuit the bus. Only the diagonal pairs conduct in motoring.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
**Distinguish between circulating current and non-circulating current modes of a dual converter. Which one is preferred for high-performance applications?**

**Model Answer (8 lines):**

| **Aspect** | **Circulating Current Mode** | **Non-Circulating Current Mode** |
|---|---|---|
| Converter firing | Both simultaneously | One at a time |
| Circulating current | Yes (limited by $L_C$) | None |
| Dead time | None | Few ms |
| Response | Instantaneous | Slight delay |
| Reactor size | Large ($L_C$) | Not required |
| Losses | Higher (circulating $I$) | Lower |
| Preferred for | High-performance reversible drives (rolling mills) | Cost-sensitive, slow-reversal applications |

**Preferred:** **Circulating current mode** for high-performance applications due to its instantaneous four-quadrant response without dead time.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Chopper-Fed SEDC

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, 2024 Scheme Pattern]**
> 
> A 220 V, 1500 rpm, 10 A separately excited DC motor has $R_a = 1.0\,\Omega$ and $K\Phi = 0.8\,\text{V·s/rad}$. The motor is fed from a 220 V DC source through a chopper operating at 500 Hz.
> 
> **(a)** Derive the speed–torque equation of the motor in terms of duty cycle $\alpha$. **(7 marks)**
> 
> **(b)** For $\alpha = 0.6$ and load torque $T_L = 12\,\text{Nm}$, compute the motor speed, armature current, and find the supply power drawn. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Solution to Question A**

**Part (a):** Starting from the steady-state armature equation:

$$
V_a = E_b + I_a R_a
$$

For a chopper, $V_a = \alpha V_s$:

$$
\alpha V_s = K \Phi \omega + I_a R_a
$$

Substituting $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$ and $I_a = T/(K\Phi)$:

$$
\alpha V_s = K \Phi \cdot \dfrac{2\pi N}{60} + \dfrac{T R_a}{K \Phi}
$$

Solving for $N$:

$$
N = \dfrac{60}{2\pi K \Phi}\left[\alpha V_s - \dfrac{T R_a}{K \Phi}\right] = \dfrac{60}{2\pi K \Phi}\cdot \alpha V_s - \dfrac{60\, R_a}{2\pi (K \Phi)^2}\cdot T
$$

Plugging in the given values:

$$
N = \dfrac{60}{2\pi \cdot 0.8}(0.6 \cdot 220) - \dfrac{60 \cdot 1.0}{2\pi (0.8)^2}\,T
$$

$$
N = \dfrac{60}{5.027}(132) - \dfrac{60}{4.021}\,T = 11.935 \cdot 132 - 14.92\, T
$$

$$
\boxed{\;N = 1575.4 - 14.92\,T\;} \quad \text{(with } T \text{ in Nm, } N \text{ in rpm)}
$$

This is the **speed–torque equation** — a straight line with no-load speed 1575 rpm and slope $-14.92$ rpm/Nm. ✓

**Part (b):** With $\alpha = 0.6$ and $T_L = 12$ Nm:

- **Armature current:** $I_a = T_L / (K\Phi) = 12 / 0.8 = 15\,\text{A}$

- **Motor speed (using the derived equation):**
$N = 1575.4 - 14.92 \cdot 12 = 1575.4 - 179.04 = 1396.4\,\text{rpm}$

Verification via fundamental equation:
$V_a = 0.6 \cdot 220 = 132\,\text{V}$
$E_b = V_a - I_a R_a = 132 - 15 \cdot 1.0 = 117\,\text{V}$
$\omega = E_b / (K\Phi) = 117 / 0.8 = 146.25\,\text{rad/s}$
$N = 146.25 \cdot 60 / (2\pi) = 1396.4\,\text{rpm}$ ✓

- **Supply power drawn:**
$P_{in} = V_s \cdot I_a \cdot \alpha = 220 \cdot 15 \cdot 0.6 = 1980\,\text{W} = 1.98\,\text{kW}$

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Students often compute $P_{in} = V_s \cdot I_a$ (forgetting the duty cycle). **Correct:** $P_{in} = \alpha V_s I_a$ because the source delivers current only during $T_{on}$.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Controlled Rectifier–Fed SEDC (Alternative Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, 2024 Scheme Pattern]**
> 
> A 220 V, 1000 rpm, 50 A separately excited DC motor has $R_a = 0.2\,\Omega$ and $K\Phi = 1.5\,\text{V·s/rad}$. It is fed from a 3-phase, 415 V (rms, line), 50 Hz fully-controlled rectifier.
> 
> **(a)** For firing angle $\alpha = 30°$ and load torque $T_L = 75\,\text{Nm}$, find the speed and supply power. **(7 marks)**
> 
> **(b)** Determine the firing angle to obtain a speed of 500 rpm at the same load torque. Comment on the mode of operation. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Solution to Question B**

**Part (a):** Average armature voltage:

$$
V_{a,avg} = \dfrac{3 V_{mL}}{\pi} \cos\alpha = \dfrac{3 \cdot \sqrt{2} \cdot 415}{\pi} \cos 30°
$$

$$
V_{mL} = \sqrt{2} \cdot 415 = 586.9\,\text{V}
$$

$$
V_{a,avg} = \dfrac{3 \cdot 586.9}{\pi} \cdot 0.866 = 560.3 \cdot 0.866 \approx 485.2\,\text{V}
$$

**Armature current:** $I_a = T_L / (K\Phi) = 75 / 1.5 = 50\,\text{A}$

**Back EMF:** $E_b = V_a - I_a R_a = 485.2 - 50 \cdot 0.2 = 485.2 - 10 = 475.2\,\text{V}$

**Speed:**

$$
\omega = \dfrac{E_b}{K\Phi} = \dfrac{475.2}{1.5} = 316.8\,\text{rad/s}
$$

$$
N = 316.8 \cdot \dfrac{60}{2\pi} = 316.8 \cdot 9.549 \approx 3025\,\text{rpm}
$$

> Note: $N > 1000\,\text{rpm}$ (rated) — this is the **field-weakening region** or we need higher $\alpha$.

**Supply power:** $P_{in} = V_{a,avg} \cdot I_a = 485.2 \cdot 50 = 24.26\,\text{kW}$

**Part (b):** For $N = 500\,\text{rpm}$:

$$
\omega_{new} = 500 \cdot \dfrac{2\pi}{60} = 52.36\,\text{rad/s}
$$

$$
E_b = K\Phi \cdot \omega = 1.5 \cdot 52.36 = 78.54\,\text{V}
$$

$$
V_a = E_b + I_a R_a = 78.54 + 50 \cdot 0.2 = 88.54\,\text{V}
$$

Solving for $\alpha$:

$$
\cos\alpha = \dfrac{V_a \pi}{3 V_{mL}} = \dfrac{88.54 \cdot \pi}{3 \cdot 586.9} = \dfrac{278.1}{1760.7} \approx 0.1580
$$

$$
\alpha = \cos^{-1}(0.1580) \approx 80.91°
$$

**Mode of operation:** Since $\alpha = 80.91° < 90°$, the converter is in the **rectification (motoring) mode**. The motor is running at half rated speed with reduced armature voltage. No regenerative braking is occurring (current still positive, torque still positive).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students frequently forget to convert $V_{LL,rms}$ to peak $V_{mL}$ before plugging in. Also, they confuse **$V_m$ (phase peak)** with **$V_{mL}$ (line peak)**. For a 3-phase full converter, **always use line-to-line peak**: $V_{mL} = \sqrt{2}\,V_{LL,rms}$.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Module-Wide Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 ways students lose marks in this module:**
> 1. **Mixing up $\alpha$:** Using $\alpha$ as duty cycle in rectifiers or as firing angle in choppers. They are *unrelated* parameters.
> 2. **Quadrant confusion:** Claiming that a 1-quadrant chopper can regenerate. **No** — it can only freewheel, not return energy to the source.
> 3. **Forgetting units in speed conversion:** $N$ (rpm) vs $\omega$ (rad/s) vs $f$ (Hz) — always state and convert.
> 4. **Wrong $V_m$:** Using RMS instead of peak (or vice versa) in rectifier formulas. **Always peak.**
> 5. **Sign of torque in regeneration:** In Q II, $T$ is negative but $N$ is positive. Drawing the characteristic with positive $T$ in Q II loses full marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final high-density revision checklist — Module 4**

- **SEDC Master Equation:** $V_a = K\Phi\omega + I_a R_a$ and $T = K\Phi I_a$ → combine to get $\omega = \omega_0 - [R_a/(K\Phi)^2] T$.
- **Chopper averages:** $V_{a,avg} = \alpha V_s$ (DC–DC). $\alpha \in [0, 1]$.
- **1-Phase Full Converter:** $V_{a,avg} = (2V_m/\pi)\cos\alpha$. Rectification: $\alpha \in [0°, 90°]$. Inversion: $\alpha \in (90°, 180°]$.
- **3-Phase Full Converter:** $V_{a,avg} = (3V_{mL}/\pi)\cos\alpha = (3\sqrt{2}\,V_{LL,rms}/\pi)\cos\alpha$.
- **Quadrant Summary:**
  - 1-Q chopper → Quadrant I only.
  - 2-Q chopper (Class C) → Quadrants I and II.
  - 4-Q chopper (H-bridge) → All four quadrants.
  - 1-Phase full converter (with $D_F$) → Quadrants I and II.
  - 3-Phase full converter → Quadrants I and II.
  - Dual converter → All four quadrants.
- **Dual Converter Modes:** Non-circulating (one converter at a time, dead time) vs Circulating (both fire, $L_C$ limits circulating current, $\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 = 180°$).
- **Regenerative Braking:** Energy flows from motor → DC bus → back to source. Achieved by:
  - Chopper: Reversing $V_a$ polarity (4-Q) or inversion (rectifier mode, $\alpha > 90°$).
  - Rectifier: Operating in **inverter mode** with $\alpha > 90°$ — the same SCR bridge now pumps current *into* the AC mains.
- **Speed Control Methods (below base speed):** Armature voltage control ($\alpha$ variation). Above base speed: **Field weakening** (reduce $\Phi$) — not in this module's main scope, but you should know it exists.
- **Continuous Conduction Assumption:** Armature current is always positive (or always flowing) — allows use of average values only.
- **KTU 2024 Hot Topics for Numericals:**
  - Speed calculation given $\alpha$ and $T_L$.
  - Firing angle calculation given desired speed.
  - Power drawn (input), power developed ($E_b I_a$), and efficiency.
  - Identification of operating quadrant from given $V_a$ and $I_a$ signs.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Three-Phase VSI Fed Induction Motor Drives: Stator Voltage Control, V/F Speed Control, Speed-Torque Characteristics, Speed Control, Operation Below and Above Base Speed, Braking: Dynamic and Regenerative
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOT502 - POWER ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Three-Phase VSI Fed Induction Motor Drives: Stator Voltage Control, V/F Speed Control, Speed-Torque Characteristics, Speed Control, Operation Below and Above Base Speed, Braking: Dynamic and Regenerative

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Three-Phase VSI Fed Induction Motor Drive: Formal Definition

A **Three-Phase Voltage Source Inverter (VSI) Fed Induction Motor Drive** is a complete adjustable-speed drive (ASD) system in which a squirrel-cage or slip-ring **three-phase induction motor (IM)** is supplied with variable-voltage, variable-frequency (VVVF) AC power synthesized by a **force-commutated, six-step or PWM Voltage Source Inverter**. The DC link of the VSI is fed from either an uncontrolled diode rectifier (for unidirectional power flow) or a fully controlled PWM rectifier (for bidirectional/regenerative operation). The VSI is typically realized using IGBTs, IGCTs, or power MOSFETs switched at frequencies in the range of **2 kHz to 20 kHz**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In the 2024 scheme, this topic is treated as the *culmination* of the power-electronics stack — the application layer where the rectifier, chopper, and inverter modules of the previous modules are integrated into a single, complete industrial drive. Expect derivation-based and block-diagram questions.

The drive is governed by two control philosophies:
1. **Stator Voltage Control (Scalar Control — Varying V at fixed f)** — A sub-scalar method used for limited speed range (fans, pumps).
2. **V/F (Volts per Hertz) Control** — The industry-standard scalar method that keeps the air-gap flux constant, enabling a wide constant-torque operating range.

## 1.2 The Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The Elevator Motor of a Skyscraper**
> Imagine the induction motor as a **giant winch** lifting an elevator. The load on the winch changes constantly (light/few passengers, heavy/full load). If you lower the winch drum (motor) *voltage* alone, the motor loses *grip* (torque) and the elevator stalls. If you spin it *faster* (frequency) without giving it more *pulling strength* (voltage), the motor again becomes too weak to hold the load.
> The **V/F rule** is the elevator engineer's rule of thumb: *"To keep the elevator's pulling strength (torque) the same at any speed, always raise the voltage in exact proportion to the frequency you raise."* This keeps the *magnetic muscle* of the motor (air-gap flux) constant.

**Why we need V/F control — the physics in plain English:**

The **air-gap flux** $\phi_m$ of an induction motor is given by the well-known EMF equation:

$$E \approx 4.44 \, f \, N \, \phi_m \, k_w$$

where $E$ is the induced EMF per phase, $f$ is the supply frequency, $N$ is the number of turns, and $k_w$ is the winding factor. If we neglect the small stator drop ($E \approx V$), then:

$$\phi_m \approx \frac{V}{4.44 \, f \, N \, k_w}$$

Therefore, **to keep $\phi_m$ constant**, we must keep the ratio $V/f$ **constant**. This single equation is the soul of the entire module.

## 1.3 Operational Zones — A First Glance

A V/F controlled drive typically operates in **three distinct zones**:

| Zone | Frequency Range | Voltage Range | Torque Capability | V/f Ratio | Power |
|:----:|:---------------:|:-------------:|:-----------------:|:---------:|:-----:|
| **Below Base Speed** | 0 to $f_{rated}$ | 0 to $V_{rated}$ (linear) | **Constant** | **Constant** | $\propto f$ |
| **At Base Speed** | $f_{rated}$ | $V_{rated}$ | Rated | Constant | Rated |
| **Above Base Speed** | $> f_{rated}$ | $V_{rated}$ (constant) | **Falls as $1/f$** | **Decreases** | **Constant (Field-Weakening)** |

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Base frequency** of the drive system: typically **50 Hz** (India/EU) or **60 Hz** (US).
- **Base voltage**: typically **415 V (line-to-line)** for 3-phase industrial motors in India.
- **Stator voltage boost** at very low frequencies: **2 % to 8 %** of rated voltage to compensate for $I_s R_s$ drop.
- **V/F ratio** for a 415 V, 50 Hz motor: $V/f = 415/50 = \mathbf{8.3\;\text{V/Hz}}$.
- **Switching frequency of modern IGBT VSI**: typically **2 kHz – 16 kHz**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Stator Voltage Control (Varying V at Constant f)

### 2.1.1 The Concept

In this method, the **supply frequency is kept at rated value**, and the **stator voltage is varied** (typically reduced) to control the motor speed. It is the *most primitive* speed-control method and is restricted to **low-power, fan, and pump-type loads** because of a critical limitation explained below.

### 2.1.2 Per-Phase Equivalent Circuit Analysis

The per-phase equivalent circuit of an induction motor referred to the stator is:

- $R_s$ = Stator resistance per phase
- $R_r'$ = Rotor resistance referred to stator per phase
- $X_s$ = Stator leakage reactance per phase
- $X_r'$ = Rotor leakage reactance referred to stator per phase
- $X_m$ = Magnetizing reactance per phase
- $s$ = slip

The approximate (Thevenin) voltage $V_{th}$ seen across the magnetizing branch and rotor loop is:

$$V_{th} = V_s \cdot \frac{j X_m}{R_s + j(X_s + X_m)}$$

The **Thevenin impedance** is:

$$Z_{th} = \frac{j X_m (R_s + j X_s)}{R_s + j(X_s + X_m)}$$

For $X_m \gg R_s$ and $X_m \gg X_s$:

$$V_{th} \approx V_s \cdot \frac{X_m}{X_s + X_m}, \quad Z_{th} \approx R_s \cdot \frac{j X_m}{X_s + X_m} + j X_s \frac{X_m}{X_s+X_m}$$

A useful approximation often used in KTU-level problems (and valid for motors with $X_m \gg X_s$ and $R_s$ small) is:

$$V_{th} \approx V_s \cdot \frac{X_m}{X_s + X_m} \approx V_s \cdot \frac{1}{1 + X_s / X_m}$$

### 2.1.3 The Electromagnetic Torque Equation

The air-gap power transferred to the rotor is $P_{ag} = m \cdot I_r'^2 \cdot R_r'/s$, where $m$ is the number of phases. The developed electromagnetic torque is:

$$T_e = \frac{P_{ag}}{\omega_s} = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{V_{th}^2 \cdot (R_r'/s)}{(R_{th} + R_r'/s)^2 + (X_{th} + X_r')^2}$$

For the maximum torque, differentiating with respect to slip:

$$\boxed{s_{m,T} = \frac{R_r'}{\sqrt{R_{th}^2 + (X_{th} + X_r')^2}}}$$

$$\boxed{T_{emax} = \frac{3}{2 \omega_s} \cdot \frac{V_{th}^2}{R_{th} + \sqrt{R_{th}^2 + (X_{th} + X_r')^2}}}$$

### 2.1.4 Why Stator-Voltage Control is Limited

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Insight (Frequently Asked in KTU):** The slip at maximum torque $s_{m,T}$ is **independent of $V_s$**, but $T_{emax} \propto V_s^2$. Thus, reducing voltage **squares-reduces the maximum torque**, drastically reducing the safe operating envelope. The motor cannot develop enough torque to drive constant-torque loads at low voltage. Hence, this method is **confined to quadratic-torque loads (fans, centrifugal pumps)** where the load torque naturally decreases with speed.

## 2.2 The V/F (Volts per Hertz) Control

### 2.2.1 The Constant-Flux Principle

From the EMF equation: $V \approx 4.44 \, f \, N \, \phi_m \, k_w$. Holding $V/f$ constant keeps $\phi_m$ at its rated value. With flux held constant, the torque equation simplifies to:

$$T_e = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{V_{th}^2 \cdot (R_r'/s)}{(R_{th} + R_r'/s)^2 + (X_{th} + X_r')^2}$$

If frequency is varied, both $\omega_s = 2\pi f / p$ (synchronous speed in rad/s) and the reactances $X_s = 2\pi f L_s$ and $X_r' = 2\pi f L_r'$ scale linearly with $f$. Since $V$ is also scaled linearly, the entire torque expression becomes **invariant with frequency** except for the $1/\omega_s$ prefactor — and since $\omega_s \propto f$, the **developed torque is essentially independent of frequency** in the constant $V/f$ region. The motor thus delivers its rated torque at any speed below base speed.

### 2.2.2 Voltage Boost at Low Frequencies

The constant $V/f$ rule assumes $V \approx E$ (stator drop neglected). But at low $f$, the stator copper drop $I_s R_s$ becomes a significant fraction of the applied voltage:

$$V_s = E + I_s R_s + j I_s X_s$$

Thus the flux $\phi_m = E / (4.44 f N k_w)$ *sags* at low $f$. To compensate, an **extra voltage $V_{boost}$** is added:

$$\boxed{V_s(f) = V_{boost} + (V/f)_{rated} \cdot f}$$

The boost typically varies from **2 % to 8 %** of rated voltage, depending on the motor's $R_s$ and the desired low-speed torque. The resulting $V_s$ vs $f$ characteristic is shown in SECTION 4.

### 2.2.3 Operation Above Base Speed — Field Weakening

Above base speed, the drive is constrained by the **maximum available DC-link voltage**, which limits the output line-to-line RMS voltage to $V_{rated}$. Therefore:

- $f$ continues to increase beyond $f_{rated}$,
- $V$ remains pegged at $V_{rated}$,
- The flux falls as $\phi_m \propto 1/f$,
- The pull-out torque falls as $T_{emax} \propto 1/f^2$,
- The power $P = T \cdot \omega \approx$ **constant** (constant-power region).

This is the **field-weakening region**, analogous to the field-weakening of a DC shunt motor.

## 2.3 Speed-Torque Characteristics — Multiple Slips and Voltages

### 2.3.1 Speed-Torque Curve with Variable V at Constant f

When $V$ is reduced at constant $f$:
- The synchronous speed $N_s = 120 f / p$ remains **fixed**.
- The maximum torque $T_{emax}$ reduces as $V^2$.
- The slip at maximum torque $s_{m,T}$ remains **constant**.
- The starting torque (at $s = 1$) reduces as $V^2$.

### 2.3.2 Speed-Torque Curve with V/f Held Constant

When $V/f$ is held constant:
- The synchronous speed $N_s$ varies **linearly with $f$**.
- The maximum torque $T_{emax}$ is **nearly constant** (small reduction at low f due to $R_s$).
- The starting torque is nearly constant.
- The shape of the curve (after normalization) is **invariant** — a set of parallel curves is obtained.

## 2.4 Braking of Induction Motors

Braking converts the **kinetic energy of the rotating load** into a form that the motor can absorb (heat, or back to the supply). The three principal methods are:

### 2.4.1 Dynamic (Rheostatic) Braking

The motor is **disconnected from the AC supply**, and a **DC current is injected into one or two stator phases** to create a stationary magnetic field. The rotor (still spinning) cuts this stationary field, induces rotor EMFs, drives rotor currents, and produces a **braking torque** proportional to slip speed.

**The slip during dynamic braking** is defined with respect to a *virtual* synchronous speed equal to the rotor speed $N$:

$$s_{db} = \frac{N - 0}{N} = 1 \quad \text{at standstill reference for a stationary field}$$

More rigorously, the dynamic-braking torque is:

$$T_{db} = \frac{3}{\omega_{rotor}} \cdot \frac{V_{dc,eq}^2 \cdot (R_r'/s_{db})}{(R_s + R_r'/s_{db})^2 + (X_s + X_r')^2}$$

where $V_{dc,eq}$ is the equivalent RMS AC voltage producing the same stator flux as the DC injection.

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Implementation:** The DC injection is obtained by the same VSI by using the *brake-chopper* function — but more elegantly, by re-firing the inverter in a special *asymmetric* mode where one leg is held on (top) and another (bottom) is held on, while the third leg is OFF. This creates a circulating DC current through two phases. **This is a KTU-favorite question.**

### 2.4.2 Regenerative Braking

The motor acts as a **generator**, converting the kinetic energy of the load into electrical energy that is **pushed back into the AC supply** (or dissipated in a regenerative brake resistor). For an induction motor to regenerate, the **slip must be negative** ($s < 0$), which means the rotor speed must exceed the synchronous speed.

$$s = \frac{N_s - N}{N_s} \quad < 0 \iff N > N_s$$

**How to achieve $N > N_s$ in a VSI-fed drive:**
1. The load is overhauling (e.g., lowering a hoist, descending elevator, decelerating a high-inertia load from overspeed).
2. The drive reduces the frequency command suddenly — the motor's rotor cannot decelerate instantaneously, so the rotor speed now exceeds the new (lower) synchronous speed, giving $s < 0$ and the motor regenerates.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hardware Requirement:** For *active* regenerative braking (returning energy to the AC mains), the diode bridge rectifier at the front-end must be replaced by a **bidirectional PWM rectifier (Active Front End / AFE)**. Otherwise, the DC link voltage rises uncontrollably; it is then clamped by a **brake chopper + resistor** (called *DC-link dynamic braking*). This is the configuration found in 95 % of low-cost industrial drives.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use | Units |
|:-:|:--------|:--------------|:------|
| 1 | $V/f = \text{constant} = V_{rated}/f_{rated}$ | Constant-flux rule | V/Hz |
| 2 | $E \approx 4.44 f N k_w \phi_m$ | Induced EMF / phase | V |
| 3 | $\phi_m \approx V / (4.44 f N k_w)$ | Flux linkage | Wb |
| 4 | $N_s = 120 f / p$ | Synchronous speed | rpm |
| 5 | $s = (N_s - N) / N_s$ | Slip | dimensionless |
| 6 | $s_{m,T} = R_r' / \sqrt{R_{th}^2 + (X_{th}+X_r')^2}$ | Slip at max torque | dimensionless |
| 7 | $T_{emax} = \dfrac{3 V_{th}^2}{2 \omega_s (R_{th}+\sqrt{R_{th}^2+(X_{th}+X_r')^2})}$ | Max torque | N·m |
| 8 | $T_e \propto V^2$ (at constant $f$ and $s$) | Stator voltage control | – |
| 9 | $T_e \approx \text{constant}$ for $V/f$ = const | Constant-flux torque | N·m |
| 10 | $V_{boost} = I_s R_s$ typical | Low-freq voltage boost | V |
| 11 | $s_{regen} < 0 \iff N > N_s$ | Regeneration condition | – |
| 12 | $P_{regen} = T_e \cdot \omega$ | Regenerated power | W |
| 13 | $f_{sw}$ of VSI: 2–20 kHz | IGBT switching frequency | Hz |
| 14 | $V_{LL,rated} = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_{ph,rated}$ | Line-to-line to phase | V |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **V/F drives** form the workhorse of the HVAC, fan, pump, conveyor, and small-EV industries (golf carts, small forklifts). They constitute over **70 % of all adjustable-speed drives** sold globally.
- **Regenerative V/F drives with active front-end** are used in **regenerative elevators, large centrifuges, wind-turbine pitch systems, and hybrid-electric vehicles** to recapture braking energy and improve overall efficiency by 20–40 %.
- The **DC injection dynamic brake** is universally used in **machine tools (CNC)** and **textile machinery** to obtain fast, controlled stops without mechanical brake wear.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: The V/F = constant Condition from Constant Flux

**Statement to prove:** *To maintain the air-gap flux $\phi_m$ of an induction motor constant, the ratio of applied stator voltage to supply frequency must remain constant.*

### Step 1: Write the per-phase EMF equation
The RMS EMF induced in a stator phase winding is:

$$E = 4.44 \, f \, N_{ph} \, k_w \, \phi_m$$

where $N_{ph}$ is the number of turns per phase and $k_w$ is the winding factor.

### Step 2: Neglect the stator impedance drop
For a high-power industrial motor, $I_s R_s$ and $I_s X_s$ are typically only 3–5 % of $V$ at rated conditions. Thus:

$$V_s \approx E$$

### Step 3: Substitute to express the flux
$$\phi_m = \frac{V_s}{4.44 \, f \, N_{ph} \, k_w}$$

### Step 4: Impose the constant-flux condition
Set $\phi_m = \phi_{m,rated}$ (a constant):

$$\phi_{m,rated} = \frac{V_s}{4.44 \, f \, N_{ph} \, k_w}$$

### Step 5: Rearrange
$$\frac{V_s}{f} = 4.44 \, N_{ph} \, k_w \, \phi_{m,rated} = \text{constant}$$

$$\boxed{\frac{V}{f} = \text{constant} = \frac{V_{rated}}{f_{rated}}}$$

This is the celebrated **V/F rule** — derived in five elementary steps and tested exhaustively in KTU papers.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: The Torque Equation in Terms of V and f

### Step 1: Thevenin equivalent voltage
The Thevenin voltage across the rotor-magnetizing branch is:

$$V_{th} = V_s \cdot \frac{X_m}{\sqrt{R_s^2 + (X_s + X_m)^2}}$$

### Step 2: For $X_m \gg X_s, R_s$
In a typical induction motor $X_m \gg X_s$ and $X_m \gg R_s$, so:

$$V_{th} \approx V_s \cdot \frac{X_m}{X_s + X_m} = V_s \cdot K_1 \quad \text{where } K_1 = \frac{X_m}{X_s + X_m}$$

### Step 3: Thevenin impedance
$$Z_{th} = \frac{j X_m (R_s + j X_s)}{R_s + j (X_s + X_m)} \approx \frac{j X_m R_s}{R_s + j(X_s + X_m)}$$

In magnitude:

$$Z_{th} \approx \frac{X_m \sqrt{R_s^2 + X_s^2}}{\sqrt{R_s^2 + (X_s + X_m)^2}} \angle (\text{some angle})$$

For simplicity, define:

$$R_{th} = R_s \cdot \frac{X_m^2}{R_s^2 + (X_s + X_m)^2}, \quad X_{th} = X_s \cdot \frac{X_m^2}{R_s^2 + (X_s + X_m)^2}$$

### Step 4: Torque from air-gap power
$$T_e = \frac{P_{ag}}{\omega_s} = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot I_r'^2 \cdot \frac{R_r'}{s}$$

The rotor referred current is:

$$I_r' = \frac{V_{th}}{\sqrt{(R_{th} + R_r'/s)^2 + (X_{th} + X_r')^2}}$$

### Step 5: Combine
$$T_e = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{V_{th}^2 \cdot (R_r'/s)}{(R_{th} + R_r'/s)^2 + (X_{th} + X_r')^2}$$

### Step 6: Substitute $V_{th} = V_s \cdot K_1$ and $\omega_s = 2\pi f / p$
$$T_e = \frac{3 \cdot p}{2\pi f} \cdot \frac{K_1^2 \cdot V_s^2 \cdot (R_r'/s)}{(R_{th} + R_r'/s)^2 + (X_{th} + X_r')^2}$$

### Step 7: Apply the V/F rule ($V_s / f = $ constant, i.e., $V_s = K_2 f$)
All reactances $X_{th}, X_r'$ scale with $f$ since $X = 2\pi f L$. Let $X = 2\pi f \cdot \mathcal{L}$ (where $\mathcal{L}$ is the inductance per Hz). Then:

$$(X_{th} + X_r')^2 = (2\pi f)^2 (\mathcal{L}_{th} + \mathcal{L}_r')^2 = (2\pi f)^2 \mathcal{L}_{eq}^2$$

$$T_e = \frac{3p}{2\pi f} \cdot \frac{K_1^2 \cdot K_2^2 f^2 \cdot (R_r'/s)}{(R_{th} + R_r'/s)^2 + (2\pi f)^2 \mathcal{L}_{eq}^2}$$

### Step 8: $f$ in numerator and denominator cancel
$$T_e = \frac{3p \cdot K_1^2 K_2^2 \cdot (R_r'/s)}{(R_{th} + R_r'/s)^2 / f + (2\pi)^2 f \mathcal{L}_{eq}^2 / \cdot 1}$$

Wait — let me be more careful. Dividing numerator and denominator by $f^2$:

$$T_e = \frac{3p \cdot K_1^2 K_2^2 \cdot (R_r'/s)}{(R_{th}/f + R_r'/(s f))^2 + (2\pi \mathcal{L}_{eq})^2}$$

For the constant-torque region, if the slip is at a *fixed percentage* of $f$ (e.g., $R_r'/s \gg R_{th}$), the $1/f$ correction is small and $T_e \approx$ constant. The full expression shows the torque is *nearly* constant, with a small droop at low $f$ caused by $R_s$.

$$\boxed{T_e(f) = \frac{3 p \, K_1^2 \, (V/f)^2 \cdot (R_r'/s)}{2\pi \left[ (R_{th}/f + R_r'/(s f))^2 + (2\pi \mathcal{L}_{eq})^2 \right]}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Implication of the $\frac{1}{f}$ droop:** This is precisely *why* the voltage boost $V_{boost}$ is required at low $f$. The boost compensates the $R_{th}/f$ term and restores the constant-torque behavior at low speeds.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Slip at Maximum Torque and its Independence from V

Starting from the torque equation:

$$T_e = \frac{3 V_{th}^2 (R_r'/s)}{\omega_s \left[(R_{th} + R_r'/s)^2 + (X_{th} + X_r')^2 \right]}$$

### Step 1: Differentiate $T_e$ with respect to $s$ and set $\dfrac{dT_e}{ds} = 0$
The maximum of a function of the form $\dfrac{a \cdot s}{(b + c/s)^2 + d}$ is found by calculus:

Let $u = R_r'/s$, so $du/ds = -R_r'/s^2$. Then:

$$T_e = \frac{3 V_{th}^2 \cdot u}{\omega_s \left[(R_{th} + u)^2 + (X_{th} + X_r')^2\right]}$$

$$\frac{dT_e}{du} = \frac{3 V_{th}^2}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{[(R_{th} + u)^2 + (X_{th} + X_r')^2] - u \cdot 2(R_{th} + u)}{[(R_{th} + u)^2 + (X_{th} + X_r')^2]^2}$$

### Step 2: Set numerator to zero
$$(R_{th} + u)^2 + (X_{th} + X_r')^2 = 2 u (R_{th} + u)$$

$$(R_{th} + u)^2 - 2u(R_{th} + u) + (X_{th} + X_r')^2 = 0$$

$$(R_{th} + u)(R_{th} + u - 2u) + (X_{th} + X_r')^2 = 0$$

$$(R_{th} + u)(R_{th} - u) + (X_{th} + X_r')^2 = 0$$

$$R_{th}^2 - u^2 + (X_{th} + X_r')^2 = 0$$

### Step 3: Solve for $u$
$$u^2 = R_{th}^2 + (X_{th} + X_r')^2$$

$$u = \sqrt{R_{th}^2 + (X_{th} + X_r')^2}$$

### Step 4: Convert back to slip
$u = R_r'/s_{m,T}$, so:

$$s_{m,T} = \frac{R_r'}{\sqrt{R_{th}^2 + (X_{th} + X_r')^2}}$$

### Step 5: Substitute back to find $T_{emax}$
$$T_{emax} = \frac{3 V_{th}^2}{2 \omega_s (R_{th} + u)} = \frac{3 V_{th}^2}{2 \omega_s (R_{th} + \sqrt{R_{th}^2 + (X_{th}+X_r')^2})}$$

### Step 6: Critical observation
$s_{m,T}$ contains **no $V_s$ term** — it is **independent of supply voltage**. However, $T_{emax} \propto V_{th}^2 \propto V_s^2$. This explains why reducing voltage shrinks the available torque envelope without moving the curve horizontally.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Energy Balance During Regenerative Braking

### Step 1: Power balance
The mechanical power developed at the air-gap is:

$$P_{ag} = 3 \cdot I_r'^2 \cdot \frac{R_r'}{s}$$

### Step 2: Decompose into mechanical and electrical
Using the equivalent circuit, the rotor copper loss is:

$$P_{rcl} = 3 \cdot I_r'^2 \cdot R_r'$$

### Step 3: Ratio of mechanical to air-gap power
The mechanical power developed (which drives the shaft, or, when $s < 0$, is supplied by the shaft) is:

$$P_{mech} = (1 - s) \cdot P_{ag} = P_{ag} - s \cdot P_{ag} = P_{ag} - P_{rcl}$$

$$\frac{P_{mech}}{P_{ag}} = 1 - s, \quad \frac{P_{rcl}}{P_{ag}} = s$$

### Step 4: Plug into the slip equation
For $0 < s < 1$ (motoring): $P_{mech} > 0$ (delivered to shaft), $P_{rcl} > 0$ (loss in rotor bars).

For $s < 0$ (regenerating): $P_{mech} < 0$ (absorbed from shaft), $P_{rcl} > 0$ (still loss), and the magnitude of $P_{mech}$ is $|s| \cdot P_{ag}$. The net power returning to the stator is:

$$P_{regen,to\_stator} = P_{ag} \cdot (1 - s) - P_{ag} = -s \cdot P_{ag} = \vert s \vert \cdot P_{ag}$$

The energy returns to the DC bus, raising $V_{dc}$. If the front-end is a PWM rectifier, the energy is pumped back to the AC mains.

$$\boxed{E_{recovered} = \int_{t_1}^{t_2} \vert s(t) \vert \cdot P_{ag}(t) \, dt}$$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Control Strategy vs Operating Region

| Operating Region | Frequency | Voltage | Flux $\phi_m$ | Torque | Power | V/f Ratio |
|:-----------------|:---------:|:-------:|:-------------:|:------:|:-----:|:---------:|
| **0 to $f_{rated}$** (Below base) | Variable | $V_{boost} + (V/f) f$ | Constant | Constant | $\propto f$ | **Constant** |
| **At $f_{rated}$** (Base) | $f_{rated}$ | $V_{rated}$ | Rated | Rated | Rated | $V_{rated}/f_{rated}$ |
| **$> f_{rated}$** (Field-weakening) | $> f_{rated}$ | $V_{rated}$ (clamped) | $\propto 1/f$ | $\propto 1/f^2$ | **Constant** | Decreases |
| **Dynamic Braking** | 0 (DC injected) | DC | Pulsating DC | Braking | Heat in $R_r'$ | N/A |
| **Regenerative** | $f$ constant or $\downarrow$ | AC | Same as motoring | Negative | Negative | Same as motoring |

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Complete V/F Controlled VSI Fed Induction Motor Drive

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA[AC Mains 3 Phase 415V 50Hz]:::pwr --> nodeB[Diode Bridge Rectifier]:::pwr
    nodeB --> nodeC[DC Link Capacitor C_dc]:::pwr
    nodeC --> nodeD[Three Phase VSI IGBT]:::inv
    nodeD -->|PWM AC out| nodeE[Three Phase Induction Motor]:::motor
    nodeF[Speed Reference N_ref]:::ctrl --> nodeG[V by f Generator]:::ctrl
    nodeG -->|V_ref| nodeH[PWM Modulator]:::ctrl
    nodeH -->|Gate Pulses| nodeD
    nodeG -->|f_ref| nodeI[V_f Curve Block]:::ctrl
    nodeI -->|Modulating Wave| nodeH
    nodeE -->|Tachogenerator Encoder| nodeJ[Speed Sensor]:::fbk
    nodeJ -->|Feedback N_actual| nodeK[Comparator]:::ctrl
    nodeF --> nodeK
    nodeK -->|Error Signal| nodeG
    classDef pwr fill:#fde68a,stroke:#b45309,color:#000
    classDef inv fill:#bfdbfe,stroke:#1d4ed8,color:#000
    classDef motor fill:#fecaca,stroke:#991b1b,color:#000
    classDef ctrl fill:#d9f99d,stroke:#365314,color:#000
    classDef fbk fill:#e9d5ff,stroke:#6b21a8,color:#000
```

**Explanation of the block diagram:**

- The **AC mains** (3-phase, 415 V, 50 Hz) is rectified by an uncontrolled **diode bridge**.
- The DC link is filtered by the **DC-link capacitor** $C_{dc}$ which provides a stiff DC voltage for the inverter.
- The **three-phase VSI (IGBT-based)** chops the DC into PWM AC of variable voltage and variable frequency.
- The **V/f generator** is the heart of the controller — it produces the voltage reference $V_{ref}$ from the frequency reference $f_{ref}$ using the linear V/f relationship (with low-frequency boost).
- The **PWM modulator** (typically sinusoidal PWM or space-vector PWM) generates the IGBT gate signals.
- A **speed sensor** (encoder/tachogenerator) provides feedback for closed-loop speed regulation.

## 4.2 Mermaid Flow — V/F Profile and Operating Zones

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1[Start f_ref = 0] --> node2{f less than f_rated?}
    node2 -- Yes --> node3[Region 1 Constant V by f with Boost]:::zone1
    node3 --> node4[V = V_boost + V_over_f x f]
    node4 --> node5[Constant Torque Region]
    node5 --> node6{f increases}
    node6 --> node2
    node2 -- No --> node7[Region 2 Above Base Speed]:::zone2
    node7 --> node8[V clamped at V_rated]
    node8 --> node9[Flux decreases 1 by f]
    node9 --> node10[Field Weakening Constant Power]
    node10 --> node11{f reaches f_max}
    node11 --> node12[Drive Limit]
    classDef zone1 fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#166534,color:#000
    classDef zone2 fill:#fed7aa,stroke:#9a3412,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Mermaid State Machine — Braking Modes

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Motoring : Normal operation s greater than 0
    Motoring --> DynamicBraking : Command STOP and DC injected
    Motoring --> Regenerative : N greater than N_s or f reduced
    DynamicBraking --> Stopped : N reaches 0
    Regenerative --> Motoring : N falls below N_s
    Regenerative --> Stopped : Sustained regen to f = 0
    DynamicBraking : DC current into stator phases
    DynamicBraking : Stationary field Rotor cuts field
    DynamicBraking : Kinetic energy dissipated as heat in R_r
    Regenerative : s less than 0 N greater than N_s
    Regenerative : Power flows back to DC link
    Regenerative : AFE pushes power to AC mains
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram — Dynamic Braking Using VSI

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA[VSI Phase A]:::igbt --> nodeM[Stator Phase A]:::motor
    nodeB[VSI Phase B]:::igbt --> nodeM
    nodeC[VSI Phase C]:::igbt --> nodeM
    nodeD[Brake Controller]:::ctrl -->|Gate A top ON| nodeA
    nodeD -->|Gate B bottom ON| nodeB
    nodeD -->|Gate C OFF| nodeC
    nodeE[DC Link Capacitor]:::cap --> nodeA
    nodeE --> nodeB
    nodeM -->|Braking torque T_b| nodeF[Mechanical Load Decelerating]:::mech
    classDef igbt fill:#bfdbfe,stroke:#1d4ed8,color:#000
    classDef motor fill:#fecaca,stroke:#991b1b,color:#000
    classDef ctrl fill:#d9f99d,stroke:#365314,color:#000
    classDef cap fill:#fde68a,stroke:#b45309,color:#000
    classDef mech fill:#e9d5ff,stroke:#6b21a8,color:#000
```

**Note on the DC injection scheme:** The brake controller holds the *top* IGBT of leg A ON and the *bottom* IGBT of leg B ON continuously. The phase-C leg is OFF. DC current flows from $V_{dc}^+$ through the top IGBT of A, through stator phase A, out through the neutral, into phase B, and back through the bottom IGBT of B to $V_{dc}^-$. This creates a stationary, pulsating magnetic field in two of the three stator phases. The rotor, still spinning, induces currents and develops a braking torque.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Why is it necessary to maintain a constant V/f ratio in a VSI-fed induction motor drive?**

**Model Answer:**

The air-gap flux of an induction motor is given by $\phi_m = V / (4.44 \, f \, N \, k_w)$, where $V$ is the per-phase stator voltage and $f$ is the supply frequency. To keep the flux $\phi_m$ at its rated value, the ratio $V/f$ must be held constant. Maintaining rated flux is essential because:
1. The developed torque of the motor is directly proportional to the flux — a reduced flux drastically reduces the torque capability.
2. A flux lower than rated causes the stator current to rise for a given load, leading to overheating.
3. A flux higher than rated saturates the magnetic circuit, causing excessive magnetizing current and non-linear behavior.
Hence, $V/f = V_{rated}/f_{rated}$ = **8.3 V/Hz** (for a 415 V, 50 Hz motor) is the fundamental control law.

**(3 marks: 1 for the flux equation, 1 for the V/f = const rule, 1 for the practical significance)**

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**List the three operating regions of a V/F controlled induction motor drive and state the limiting quantity in each region.**

**Model Answer:**

A V/F drive has three operating regions:

| Region | Frequency Range | Limiting Quantity | Behavior |
|:------:|:---------------:|:------------------|:---------|
| 1. Below base speed | 0 to $f_{rated}$ | **Current limit** (motor heating) | Constant torque, $P \propto f$ |
| 2. At base speed | $f_{rated}$ | Rated voltage & current | Rated operation |
| 3. Above base speed (Field-weakening) | $> f_{rated}$ | **Voltage limit** ($V = V_{rated}$, DC link maximum) | Constant power, $T \propto 1/f$ |

**(3 marks: 1 per region with its limiting quantity)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern Note:** The 14-mark questions in the 2024 scheme carry internal choice between **Question A and Question B**, each with sub-parts **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Both questions test the same module but with different problem framings.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

#### (a) Derive the condition for constant flux operation in a three-phase VSI-fed induction motor drive. Show that the developed torque becomes independent of frequency in the constant V/f region. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

Starting from the per-phase EMF equation:

$$E = 4.44 \, f \, N \, k_w \, \phi_m$$

For a high-power motor, the stator impedance drop is small, so $V \approx E$. Hence:

$$\phi_m \approx \frac{V}{4.44 \, f \, N \, k_w}$$

For constant flux, $\phi_m = \phi_{m,rated}$, giving:

$$\frac{V}{f} = 4.44 \, N \, k_w \, \phi_{m,rated} = \text{constant} = \frac{V_{rated}}{f_{rated}}$$

This is the **V/f rule**.

The electromagnetic torque, derived in SECTION 3, is:

$$T_e = \frac{3 V_{th}^2 \cdot (R_r'/s)}{\omega_s \left[ (R_{th} + R_r'/s)^2 + (X_{th} + X_r')^2 \right]}$$

Substituting $V_{th} \propto V \propto f$, $\omega_s \propto f$, and $X_{th}, X_r' \propto f$:

$$T_e = \frac{3 (C_1 f)^2 (R_r'/s)}{(C_2 f) \left[ (R_{th} + R_r'/s)^2 + (2\pi f \mathcal{L}_{eq})^2 \right]}$$

The factor of $f$ in the numerator ($f^2$) cancels with one $f$ in the denominator and the $f^2$ in the $(2\pi f \mathcal{L}_{eq})^2$ term, leaving a $1/f$ correction in $R_{th}/f$. At moderate-to-high $f$, this correction is negligible, and:

$$T_e \approx \text{constant} = \frac{3 C_1^2 (R_r'/s)}{(R_{th} + R_r'/s)^2/C_2 + C_2 (2\pi \mathcal{L}_{eq})^2}$$

Hence the developed torque is **essentially independent of frequency** in the constant-V/f region, provided the low-frequency $R_s$ drop is compensated by a voltage boost. **(7 marks: 2 for the V/f derivation, 3 for the torque equation and substitution, 2 for the final conclusion)**

---

#### (b) A 3-phase, 50 Hz, 4-pole, 415 V, star-connected induction motor has the following per-phase parameters referred to the stator: $R_s = 0.5\;\Omega$, $R_r' = 0.6\;\Omega$, $X_s = 1.2\;\Omega$, $X_r' = 1.2\;\Omega$, $X_m = 35\;\Omega$. The motor is fed by a VSI operating in V/f control. The drive operates at a frequency of 25 Hz with a V/f ratio equal to the rated value. Compute (i) the Thevenin voltage and impedance, (ii) the slip at maximum torque, and (iii) the maximum torque at 25 Hz. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $V_{LL} = 415$ V, $f = 25$ Hz, $V/f = 415/50 = 8.3$ V/Hz, so at 25 Hz: $V_{ph,rated} = 8.3 \times 25 = 207.5$ V (line-to-neutral for star).

**Step 1: Thevenin voltage at 25 Hz**
Reactances scale with frequency. At 25 Hz, the scaling factor is 25/50 = 0.5.
- $X_s(25) = 1.2 \times 0.5 = 0.6\;\Omega$
- $X_r'(25) = 1.2 \times 0.5 = 0.6\;\Omega$
- $X_m(25) = 35 \times 0.5 = 17.5\;\Omega$

$$V_{th} = V_{ph} \cdot \frac{X_m}{\sqrt{R_s^2 + (X_s + X_m)^2}} = 207.5 \cdot \frac{17.5}{\sqrt{0.5^2 + (0.6 + 17.5)^2}}$$

$$V_{th} = 207.5 \cdot \frac{17.5}{\sqrt{0.25 + 327.24}} = 207.5 \cdot \frac{17.5}{18.11} = 207.5 \times 0.9665 = 200.5\;\text{V}$$

**Step 2: Thevenin impedance**

$$R_{th} = R_s \cdot \frac{X_m^2}{R_s^2 + (X_s + X_m)^2} = 0.5 \cdot \frac{17.5^2}{0.25 + 327.24} = 0.5 \cdot \frac{306.25}{327.49} = 0.467\;\Omega$$

$$X_{th} = X_s \cdot \frac{X_m^2}{R_s^2 + (X_s + X_m)^2} = 0.6 \cdot \frac{306.25}{327.49} = 0.561\;\Omega$$

So $Z_{th} = (0.467 + j0.561)\;\Omega$.

**Step 3: Slip at maximum torque**
$$s_{m,T} = \frac{R_r'}{\sqrt{R_{th}^2 + (X_{th} + X_r')^2}} = \frac{0.6}{\sqrt{0.467^2 + (0.561 + 0.6)^2}}$$

$$s_{m,T} = \frac{0.6}{\sqrt{0.218 + 1.347}} = \frac{0.6}{\sqrt{1.565}} = \frac{0.6}{1.251} = 0.4796 \approx 0.48$$

**Step 4: Maximum torque at 25 Hz**
Synchronous speed in rad/s: $\omega_s = 2\pi f / p = 2\pi \times 25 / 2 = 78.54$ rad/s.

$$T_{emax} = \frac{3 V_{th}^2}{2 \omega_s (R_{th} + \sqrt{R_{th}^2 + (X_{th} + X_r')^2})}$$

$$T_{emax} = \frac{3 \times 200.5^2}{2 \times 78.54 \times (0.467 + 1.251)} = \frac{3 \times 40200.25}{2 \times 78.54 \times 1.718}$$

$$T_{emax} = \frac{120600.75}{269.93} = 446.8\;\text{N·m}$$

**Result:** At 25 Hz, $V_{th} = 200.5$ V, $Z_{th} = 0.467 + j0.561\;\Omega$, $s_{m,T} \approx 0.48$, and $T_{emax} \approx 447$ N·m. **(7 marks: 2 for the Thevenin equivalent, 2 for $s_{m,T}$, 3 for the maximum torque calculation)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

#### (a) Explain with suitable diagrams the operation of a three-phase VSI-fed induction motor in (i) the below-base-speed region and (ii) the above-base-speed region. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Below-Base-Speed Region (Constant-Torque Region):**

In this region, the frequency $f$ is varied from 0 up to the rated frequency $f_{rated}$. The applied voltage $V$ is varied in direct proportion to $f$ such that $V/f = $ constant. This is enforced by the **V/f curve generator** in the controller. A small **voltage boost** $V_{boost}$ (typically 2–8 % of $V_{rated}$) is added at very low $f$ to compensate for the stator $I_s R_s$ drop and maintain the rated air-gap flux.

- **Behavior:** Synchronous speed $N_s = 120 f / p$ changes linearly with $f$. The maximum torque $T_{emax}$ remains nearly constant (independent of $f$), and the power developed $P = T \cdot \omega$ rises linearly with $f$.
- **Practical use:** All constant-torque loads (conveyors, hoists, machine tools) operate in this region. The motor is current-limited, not voltage-limited.

**(ii) Above-Base-Speed Region (Field-Weakening Region):**

When $f$ is increased beyond $f_{rated}$, the output voltage of the VSI is **limited to $V_{rated}$** because the DC link voltage is fixed (it is the rectified mains voltage). Hence, the ratio $V/f$ *decreases*, and the air-gap flux falls as $\phi_m \propto 1/f$. This is the **field-weakening** region, analogous to weakening the field of a separately-excited DC motor.

- **Behavior:** The maximum torque falls as $T_{emax} \propto 1/f^2$, but the power remains approximately constant at $P = T \cdot \omega \approx$ constant.
- **Practical use:** Applications requiring speeds above rated — e.g., spindle drives, traction drives during high-speed cruise, centrifugal separators.
- **Limitation:** Mechanical and thermal limits; the drive is voltage-limited here.

**Diagrams to be drawn in the exam:** (1) $V/f$ linear graph with boost knee, (2) $T$–$N$ family of curves for multiple frequencies, (3) $P$–$N$ characteristic showing constant-torque and constant-power regions. **(7 marks: 3 for below-base, 3 for above-base, 1 for the diagrams)**

---

#### (b) Discuss in detail the dynamic (DC-injection) braking and regenerative braking methods for a VSI-fed induction motor drive. Compare the two methods. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Dynamic Braking (DC Injection):**

1. The AC supply to the stator is disconnected (the VSI is commanded to stop switching in the normal PWM mode).
2. A **DC current is injected** into the stator winding — typically by asymmetric firing of the VSI (e.g., top IGBT of leg A and bottom IGBT of leg B ON, others OFF). This creates a stationary (non-rotating) magnetic field aligned along a fixed axis.
3. The spinning rotor cuts this stationary field, inducing rotor EMFs at a frequency equal to the rotor speed. Rotor currents flow, and by Lenz's law, a **braking torque** is produced.
4. The braking torque is zero at standstill (no relative motion), so the system needs a **mechanical brake or holding torque** to come to a complete stop.
5. The kinetic energy of the load is dissipated as **heat in the rotor resistance** $R_r'$ — hence the alternative name *rheostatic braking*.

**Regenerative Braking:**

1. The motor continues to operate as an AC machine, but the slip becomes **negative** ($s < 0$), i.e., the rotor speed exceeds the synchronous speed.
2. This is achieved either by (a) an overhauling load (e.g., descending hoist) or (b) sudden *reduction* of the frequency command so that the rotor speed now exceeds the new (lower) synchronous speed.
3. The torque becomes **negative** (opposing motion), and the **mechanical power** is converted to **electrical power** and returned through the VSI to the DC link.
4. The DC link voltage rises; this energy is either:
   - **Pushed back to the AC mains** (requires an Active Front End — bidirectional PWM rectifier), or
   - **Dissipated in a brake resistor** via a DC-link chopper (a "regenerative brake chopper" — this is more common and is the only option with a diode-bridge front end).

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Dynamic Braking | Regenerative Braking |
|:--------|:---------------:|:--------------------:|
| Energy disposal | Heat in rotor resistance | Returned to mains / brake resistor |
| Hardware | Simple (DC source or VSI in brake mode) | Needs AFE or brake chopper |
| Torque at standstill | Zero (needs mechanical brake) | Can be controlled to zero smoothly |
| Stator field | Stationary DC | Rotating AC at slip frequency |
| Speed range | Wide | Limited by field-weakening region |
| Efficiency | Low (energy wasted as heat) | High (energy recovered) |
| Complexity | Low | Higher |

**(7 marks: 2 for dynamic braking, 2 for regenerative braking, 2 for the comparison, 1 for the diagram/discussion)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in KTU Valuation (Module 4):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the per-phase vs line-to-line conversion:** Students often apply $V = 415$ V directly into the per-phase EMF equation, leading to a flux value that is $\sqrt{3}$ times too high. Always use $V_{ph} = V_{LL}/\sqrt{3}$ for a star-connected motor.
> 2. **Not scaling the reactances with frequency:** When computing $X_s, X_r', X_m$ at a non-rated frequency, multiply the rated value by $f / f_{rated}$. Forgetting this is the #1 cause of incorrect $T_{emax}$ in numericals.
> 3. **Mixing up synchronous speed in rad/s and rpm:** $\omega_s$ in the torque equation is in **rad/s**. If a problem gives $N_s$ in rpm, convert using $\omega_s = 2\pi N_s / 60$.
> 4. **Confusing motor and generator modes:** In regenerative braking, students often write $s = 0.05$ (positive) for the slip, but the slip must be **negative** ($s < 0$) for regeneration. Verify the sign before concluding the operating mode.
> 5. **Stator voltage control vs V/F control:** A frequent 3-mark question asks *"Why is stator voltage control alone not used for constant-torque loads?"* The answer: $T_{emax} \propto V^2$, so reducing $V$ squares-reduces the maximum torque — the motor cannot hold the load. Always answer with the $V^2$ relationship.
> 6. **Incomplete V/F derivation:** Just writing $V/f =$ constant without invoking the EMF equation $E = 4.44 f N k_w \phi_m$ and the constant-flux assumption **loses 2 out of 7 marks** in derivations.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 4**
>
> - ☐ **EMF equation:** $E = 4.44 f N k_w \phi_m$ — the foundation of all V/F analysis.
> - ☐ **V/F rule:** $V/f = V_{rated}/f_{rated} =$ **8.3 V/Hz** for 415 V, 50 Hz; **7.0 V/Hz** for 415 V, 60 Hz.
> - ☐ **Slip formula:** $s = (N_s - N)/N_s$ — negative for generation/regeneration.
> - ☐ **Synchronous speed:** $N_s = 120 f / p$ (rpm); $\omega_s = 2\pi f / p$ (rad/s).
> - ☐ **Stator voltage control:** $T_{emax} \propto V^2$ at constant $f$; $s_{m,T}$ is **independent of $V$**.
> - ☐ **V/F control:** $T_{emax} \approx$ constant in the constant-V/f region.
> - ☐ **Voltage boost:** Required at low $f$ to compensate $I_s R_s$ drop; typically 2–8 % of $V_{rated}$.
> - ☐ **Three operating regions:** Constant-torque (below base), rated, constant-power/field-weakening (above base).
> - ☐ **Field weakening:** $\phi_m \propto 1/f$, $T_{emax} \propto 1/f^2$, $P \approx$ constant.
> - ☐ **Dynamic braking:** DC injection into stator → stationary field → rotor dissipates kinetic energy as heat in $R_r'$.
> - ☐ **Regenerative braking:** $s < 0$ (rotor faster than synchronous), requires AFE or DC-link brake chopper.
> - ☐ **Energy flow:** $P_{mech} = (1-s) P_{ag}$, $P_{rcl} = s P_{ag}$, $P_{regen} = -s P_{ag}$ (when $s<0$).
> - ☐ **Hardware building blocks:** Diode bridge → DC link → VSI (IGBT) → IM. Speed feedback via encoder/tacho.
> - ☐ **KTU-favorite numerical:** Compute $V_{th}, Z_{th}, s_{m,T}, T_{emax}$ at a given frequency $f$ (use the procedure in Q3(b) above).
> - ☐ **KTU-favorite theory:** Why V/f must be constant; why stator voltage control is limited; comparison of braking methods.
